An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Rhetoric and Popular Culture
description: Introduces the rhetorical strategies and persuasive techniques embedded in popular culture. Analyzes and critiques a variety of popular culture texts and artifacts (e.g., comics, digital media, games, political discourse, sports, TV shows).

title: Introduction to Humanities and Cultural Studies
description: Introduction to humanities and cultural studies and its three emphases. Topics cover key methods and critical theories and their application to literature, film, art, and various forms of media in a global context. Students will learn to understand the requirements and expectations of their major, learn about advising, how to use library resources, as well as about student support available on campus.

title: Humanities, Entrepreneurship, and Leadership
description: Introduces the specific durable humanities skills and knowledge needed in the private and public sectors. Provides a strong foundation in global cultural awareness, historic groundings, ethical storytelling and representation, required alongside traditional skills in the ever expanding social, cultural, economic, and environmental demands of consumers, investors, and governments.

title: Literature and Global Consciousness
description: A survey of literature in relation to global consciousness. Readings may encompass a wide range of texts through a globalized perspective. Students will examine how literature addresses race, gender, ethnic and national identities and reflects anthropological, historical and political contexts emerging from cultural encounters. All readings in English.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Literature and Advocacy
description: Study and analysis of texts engaging with advocacy, human rights, the ethics of community, and civic responsibility.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Storytelling in Cinema
description: Analysis of film narratives with a focus on both content and craft of narration. Topics, themes, and coverage may vary.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Research Methods in Humanities and Cultural Studies
description: Preparation for upper-division HCS courses. Introduction to research methodologies and central questions in cultural studies and global humanities. Application of knowledge through writing, critical thinking, and projects on art, texts, culture, and various forms of media.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
HCS185 - Introduction to Humanities and Cultural Studies (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Studies in World Literature
description: Study and analysis of selected texts from world literature. All readings in English. Topics may vary.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Writing, Advocacy, and Leadership
description: Study and apply literacy theories appropriate to professional writing in organizations and communities. Analyzes formal and informal learning in a variety of settings beyond the classroom.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Contemporary American Lit
description: Study and analysis of contemporary American literature with a focus on emerging trends and developments.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Body Politics
description: Study and analysis of literature in connection with other disciplines, such as new media studies, sociology, history, political science, law, or medicine, to examine the body as a contested site of pleasure, oppression, and identity formation.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Film in Context
description: Explorations of critical social, cultural, and philosophical questions relevant to contemporary life through analysis of films. May be repeated twice for credit, if topic is different.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Humanities and Cultural Studies BA
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Humanities and Cultural Studies BA
Must have a class standing of upper-division
Non-majors
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Topics in Public Humanities
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in public humanities, such as digital humanities, memory studies, international diaspora and local community, nature and society, etc. Topics differ by semester. Course may be repeated once for credit, topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Topics in Literature, Culture, and Theory
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in literature, culture, and theory such as prison narratives, objects and materiality, horror films and fiction, climate-fiction, psychoanalysis and literature, etc. Topic differs by semester. Course may be repeated once for credit, topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Topics in Rhetoric and Community Engagement
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in writing for change such as disability, educational, or environmental rhetorics. Topic will differ by semester. Course may be repeated once for credit, topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Humanities at Work: Humanistic Inquiry and Public Engagement
description: Introduction to the field, methodologies, and practice of the public humanities. Expand understanding of human experiences, values, and cultures into the current public sphere and explore innovative ways through which local, regional, and global communities can apply or benefit from humanistic learning.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Survey of Critical and Cultural Theory
description: Introduction to key critical and cultural theories and their application to literature, film, art, graphic novels, and various forms of media in a global context.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Survey of Rhetoric and Culture
description: Study of the relationship between rhetoric and culture through examination and analysis of key rhetorical theories and practices.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Ethnic Literature
description: Study and analysis of the roles of ethnic and racial consciousness in literature.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Film and Literature
description: Comparative study of literature and cinema as aesthetic media. Topics vary each time the course is taught and may be focused on period, genre, style/technique, or cultural context.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Postcolonial Literature
description: Study and analysis of colonial and postcolonial cultures in literature.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Rhetoric and Society
description: Uses rhetorical theories to analyze relationships between power, identity, and culture in social contexts. Students examine and explore case studies of rhetorical situations to develop a deeper understanding of participation in the community and beyond. Areas may include social movements, mainstream media, political discourses, and digital spaces.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Advanced Symposium in Literature
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in literature from different parts of the globe. Classes in English and/or target language. Topic differs by semester. Course may be repeated once for credit, topics are not repeatable.

title: Advanced Symposium in Cinema
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in cinema from one or different parts of the globe. Classes in English and/or target language. Topic differs by semester. Course may be repeated for credit, topics are not repeatable.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student’s major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Seminar in Humanities and Cultural Studies
description: Advanced study and analysis of topics, issues, theories and trends in HCS. Topics and focus vary. Course may be repeated once for credit, topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Senior Capstone Seminar (FF)
description: Capstone course for humanities and cultural studies majors to draft, workshop, and prepare a senior thesis.

title: Civic Engagement, Ethics and Global Diversity
description: Focused on guiding students to become influential leaders in thought and skill through discussion of civil engagement, ethics, diversity, and internationalization.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Professionalism and Research in Imaging Sciences
description: Familiarization with research and communication expectations related to the online AS to BS Program; improves comfort within the online environment through the use of technology, time management skills, and an understanding of program outcomes and expectations.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Human Resource Management in Health Care
description: Overview and application of the major human resource management functions: selection and placement, compensation and benefits, training and development, employee and labor relations, health, safety, and security, and strategic management practices related to the health care industry. Legal, motivational, international, merger, and acquisition, and human resource information system issues are included.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Advanced Digital Imaging
description: Employment of critical thinking to analyze patient dose and safety consideration within radiographic digital imaging. Primary emphasis on problem solving and reasoning to improve patient care through analysis of digital imaging methods and equipment.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Public Health
description: Public health concepts and practice. Topics include philosophy, purpose, history, organization, functions, tools, activities and results at national, state, and community levels.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Information Technology for Imaging Professionals
description: Managerial application of information technology in medical imaging to include basis networking, PACS, RIS, HIS, DICOM, standards and information security.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Health Law and Ethics
description: Process of legal change and health care practitioners' potential interactions with patients, law enforcement, and governmental agencies. Consent, liability, negligence, employment and licensure of professionals.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Health Informatics in Imaging Sciences
description: Provides an introduction to health information systems, current legislation impacting health informatics, privacy and security of healthcare records, and healthcare technology as related to Imaging Sciences.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Research Methods in Imaging Sciences
description: Design of experiments, analysis methods, and interpretation of results and conclusions as related to evidence based research in health.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Comprehensive Analysis on Radiation Protection
description: Analysis of the biological effects of ionizing radiation. Promotion and advocacy for patients, focused on dose and exposure reduction.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Study of Diseases in Imaging Sciences
description: Examination of the disease processes demonstrated with imaging sciences related to pathogenesis, patient populations, treatment options and prognosis.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Advanced Sectional Anatomy in Imaging Sciences
description: Advanced level identification of anatomical structures on sectional images to include sagittal, coronal, and transverse body planes utilizing MRI and CT imaging.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Preventative Care and Patient Advocacy in Imaging Sciences (FF)
description: Analysis of preventative care measures provided through medical imaging procedures. Patient care, responsibilities, ethics, and policies required of healthcare providers.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Imaging Sciences BS, Advanced Medical Imaging BS, or Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Operating Systems and Word Processing Topics
description: Introduces computer and technology concepts and develops skills using current home/office and Internet productivity software. Basic functions of the operating system, basic to intermediate word-processing skills, introduction to hardware, software, Internet and networking concepts for use in the workplace, educational settings, and the home.

title: Spreadsheet Topics
description: Develops basic to intermediate spreadsheet skills using Excel and then further expands on these skills through business discipline specific assignments.

title: Database Topics
description: Basic to intermediate database skills development, hardware, software, Internet, and networking concept materials for use in the workplace, educational settings, and the home.

title: Introduction to IT Careers
description: Introduction to a variety of career paths in information technology, such as data, analytics, infrastructure, development, security, and project management, through guest lectures from industry experts and hands-on activities. Preparation for internship and career opportunities in IT will be discussed.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ITM105 - Spreadsheet Topics (2)

title: Introduction to Programming
description: Introduction to Programming with C# and object-oriented design principles. Includes essential programming concepts of data types, decision-making, iteration, encapsulation, inheritance, error handling, testing, and documentation.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH149 - Precalculus: Function for Business (FM) (3)
MATH160 - Survey of Calculus (FM) (4)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
Or satisfactory placement score into MATH 160 or MATH 170

title: Introduction to Programming with Python for Business
description: Introduction to Python, use of rapid development tools, and object oriented design principles. Includes essential programming concepts of sequence, selection, iteration, arrays and string manipulation, testing and program documentation for business applications.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH149 - Precalculus: Function for Business (FM) (3)
MATH160 - Survey of Calculus (FM) (4)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
Or satisfactory placement score into MATH160 or MATH170

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Information Technology and Networking Essentials
description: Topics include basic concepts of computer hardware, operating systems, data and file management, networking standards, protocols, topologies, architectures, and telecommunications principles.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, Games, Interactive Media, and Mobile BS, Health Studies BS, Business and Economic Analytics BS, Economics, Quantitative Emphasis BA, Computer Science Minor, or Economics BA

title: Business Intelligence
description: Study of information technology resources that support decision making in organizations. Enterprise systems, databases, networks, and other technologies that support the collection, organization, and analysis of data are discussed. Skills necessary to collect, process, analyze, and present business intelligence are practiced through hands-on project experience. Ethical, legal, and behavioral issues of conducting business intelligence considered throughout.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ITM105 - Spreadsheet Topics (2)
Or successful completion of the COBE Computer Placement Exam for ITM 105.
