Business Majors
Completed the following: 
BUSCOM201 - Business Communication (3)
Business and Economic Analytics Majors
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Non-business majors
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BUSCOM201 - Business Communication (3)
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)

title: Database Systems
description: A foundation for data science  focused on the design, development, and use of relational databases and database management systems. Emphasis on data modeling and SQL retrieval scripts.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Systems Planning and Analysis
description: Examines system development life cycle and agile methods to organize the systems development process. Emphasis on techniques to conduct the planning and analysis phases, requirements documentation, use case development, UML modeling, and prototyping through development of a validated set of requirements.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH160 - Survey of Calculus (FM) (4)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CS117 - C++ for Engineers (3)
CS121 - Computer Science I (4)
ITM226 - Introduction to Programming with Python for Business (3)
ITM225 - Introduction to Programming (3)

title: Web Application Development I
description: Design and implementation of client-server web-based information systems. Topics include structuring and styling of semantic documents and creating interactive features. Implement dynamic projects by generating client- and server-side code with the aid of an integrated development environment.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, Games, Interactive Media, and Mobile BS, or Health Studies BS
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ITM225 - Introduction to Programming (3)
GIMM110 - Interactive Programming (3)
CS117 - C++ for Engineers (3)
CS121 - Computer Science I (4)

title: Big Data and Web Analytics
description: Explores challenges and opportunities with Big Data and Web 2.0 using R Project. Topics include: introduction to Big Data and Web 2.0, introduction to R Project for Statistical Computing, sourcing data from web-based applications, data cleaning and preparation, structured and unstructured data analysis, and visualizing and communicating findings.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ITM310 - Business Intelligence (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CS111 - Introduction to Programming (3)
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
ITM226 - Introduction to Programming with Python for Business (3)
ITM225 - Introduction to Programming (3)
CS121 - Computer Science I (4)

title: AI Augmented Projects
description: An introduction into AI image, text, and other media generating tools. Students will use these tools to automate, augment, and streamline projects. Includes prompt engineering, output analysis, and ethical considerations.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ITM225 - Introduction to Programming (3)
GIMM110 - Interactive Programming (3)
CS117 - C++ for Engineers (3)
CS121 - Computer Science I (4)
ITM226 - Introduction to Programming with Python for Business (3)

title: Advanced Database
description: Advanced database management system design principles and techniques. Topics include, but are not limited to, advanced SQL statements, access methods, query processing and optimization, concurrency controls, non-relational databases.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ITM315 - Database Systems (3)

title: Predictive Analytics
description: Fundamentals of predictive analytics will be examined. Topics will include problem definition, data preparation, and the use of analytic techniques to solve business problems. Modeling techniques will focus on predictive analytics utilizing both data and text.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BUSSTAT207 - Introduction to Business Analytics (3)
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
MATH360 - Engineering Statistics (3)
MATH361 - Probability and Statistics I (3)
MATH153 - Statistical Reasoning (FM) (3)
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Information Security
description: In-depth exploration of security issues and challenges in organizations. Topics include the need for security, policy development and implementation, risk assessment, security threats and vulnerabilities, security controls and tools. Exercises explore defense against security threats, secure application development, and network design issues.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, Games, Interactive Media, and Mobile BS, Health Studies BS, Business and Economic Analytics BS, Economics, Quantitative Emphasis BA, Computer Science Minor, or Economics BA
Completed the following: 
ITM305 - Information Technology and Networking Essentials (3)

title: Cloud Computing
description: An introduction to the essentials of cloud computing. Hands-on exercises provide a foundation in infrastructure, platform, and software as a service concepts including storage, computing, networking, identity and access management, content delivery and management.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Information Technology Management BBA, Games, Interactive Media, and Mobile BS, Health Studies BS, or Computer Science BS
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ITM305 - Information Technology and Networking Essentials (3)
CS153 - Navigating Computer Systems (1)

title: Internship
description: Field learning for information technology in an applied environment under supervision of both a manager and professor. Recommended admission to COBE and completion of 9 credit hours of ITM coursework.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time is spent on site with employers and on campus in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 Work U and/or internship credits may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Current Topics in Information Technology Management
description: Key topics in Information Technology Management area currently receiving heavy emphasis in business practitioner journals and/or in academic literature. May be repeated for credit. Recommended admission to COBE, completion of ITM 320 and ITM 325.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: IFITS Seminar
description: Students in an IFITS supported cohort work on professional skills related to becoming a scholar, including oral, written, and presentation communication skills, effective teamwork, using one’s strengths to contribute to a collaborative project, and asking meaningful research questions. Each section of this course is dedicated to a different supported cohort program which will have its own specific learning outcomes in addition to the skill set listed above. Course may be repeated up to 6 semesters for credit.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: IFITS Seminar
description: Students in an IFITS supported cohort work on professional skills related to becoming a scholar, including oral, written, and presentation communication skills, effective teamwork, using one’s strengths to contribute to a collaborative project, and asking meaningful research questions. Each section of this course is dedicated to a different supported cohort program which will have its own specific learning outcomes in addition to the skill set listed above. Course may be repeated up to 6 semesters for credit.

title: Intermediate English Grammar I
description: Intensive review of fundamentals of grammatical structures in a communicative context. Exploration of compound and complex sentence structures and correct usage of intermediate level grammatical verb tenses and structures. Focus on expression and accuracy.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Communication I
description: Emphasis on the development of emergent fluency and accuracy in spoken English. Students will discuss a variety of topics with a focus on development and integration of academic vocabulary.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Composition I
description: Study and practice of the essay writing process with attention to thesis, clarity, organization, and cohesion. Students will demonstrate sentence variety through writing compound, complex, and compound-complex sentences.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Critical Listening Skills I
description: Introduction to academic presentations and short lectures. Students will apply various listening comprehension strategies and note-taking skills.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Academic Reading and Vocabulary I
description: Emphasis on intensive and extensive reading of adapted fiction and nonfiction with a focus on vocabulary development. Addresses reading skills such as note-taking, skimming, scanning, inferring, and summarizing.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Grammar II
description: Further development of the formation of complex sentence structures with a focus on expression and accuracy. Emphasis on the accurate use of tense, voice, and dependent clauses to express ideas about texts and the opinions of others. Intensive study of high intermediate structures in a communicative context.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Communication II
description: Emphasis on the development of public speaking skills through academic discussion, debate, presentations, and research. Guided acquisition of integrating academic vocabulary in public speaking.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Composition II
description: Development of critical thinking and writing through analysis of sample essays and discussions of different rhetorical styles. Emphasis on essay process writing and accurate integration of research. Introduces U.S. techniques to citing sources. Students will practice using a variety of complex sentences and verb tenses.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: University Success Seminar
description: Emphasis on integration into U.S. university classroom culture, academic social protocols, and collaborative learning styles. Targets acquisition of academic vocabulary, and study strategies for reading college textbooks, academic articles and informational essays. Direct applications of strategies to the reading materials in IEPATH student's current university courses.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Critical Listening Skills II
description: Focus on effective and clear note-taking skills through practice and exposure to a variety of spoken input. Students will develop listening strategies such as identifying verbal cues and analyzing logical arguments, effective persuasion, appropriate justification, and rebuttal.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intermediate English Academic Reading and Vocabulary II
description: Development of critical thinking through the reading and interpretation of authentic materials. Guided acquisition of academic vocabulary using a variety of decoding techniques and corpus linguistics.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Pathway Seminar
description: Focuses on an inquiry-based research project. Development of a research question, collection evidence and data, process writing and presentation of research essay. Continued discussion of academic integrity with a focus on improving effective U.S. citation practices.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Advanced English Grammar I
description: An intensive study of the formal written and spoken structures of English grammar with a focus on accurate expression. Development and application of a variety of advanced grammatical structures including in-depth analysis of morphology and rules through output in written and spoken communication. Addresses the subtleties of structures and exceptions.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Advanced English Communication
description: Emphasis on the development of advanced speaking skills in preparation for presentations conforming to U.S. university expectations. Expansion of active use of academic vocabulary. Students will prepare community-based research projects.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Advanced English Composition
description: Focus on citing sources, summarizing and paraphrasing, and other effective, ethical citation practices used in extensive research-based writing. Practice independent vocabulary acquisition and using sentence variety to produce more sophisticated essays and responses in preparation for a U.S. university classroom. May be repeated for credit one time.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Pathway Seminar II
description: IEPATH exit requirement course. Demonstrate advanced proficiency in the written and spoken discourse of US English by means of a research paper, an expanded oral presentation on the topic of the paper, and a completion of IEPATH portfolio. Course includes an exit oral proficiency interview. May be repeated for credit one time.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Advanced English Critical Listening Skills
description: Emphasis on the development of advanced critical listening and note-taking skills through longer academic lectures. Students will develop interview skills and conduct primary research to support their academic portfolios.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Advanced English Academic Reading and Vocabulary
description: Close readings of academic material and novels, including analysis of literary devices, and abstract and concrete concepts. Students will evaluate sources for logic, accuracy and quality of scholarship as well as practice independent vocabulary acquisition and expand targeted academic vocabulary. May be repeated for credit one time.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Academic English Communication I
description: Emphasis on the development of advanced speaking skills to prepare for successful participation in graduate-level classes in the U.S. Focus on the development of presentation skills, integration of academic research and the expansion of active use of academic vocabulary. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Academic English Communication II
description: Further development of advanced speaking skills to prepare for successful participation in graduate-level classes in the U.S. Continued focus on the development of presentation skills and the expansion of active use of academic vocabulary. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Academic English Writing
description: Exploration of academic writing, discourse and argument. Emphasis on application of organizational patterns, research integration and strategies of writing for academic purposes. Students will write extensive research-based essays and responses in preparation for an American graduate program. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Academic English Reading and Vocabulary
description: Focus on extensive critical reading and analysis of academic topics in preparation for graduate studies. Exploration of rhetorical techniques and structures. Students will learn strategies to respond to academic texts and will expand targeted academic vocabulary. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Academic English Pathway Seminar
description: Emphasis on enhancing research and project management skills. Conduct extensive research for writing projects and evaluate sources for logic, accuracy, and quality of scholarship. Use collaborative writing process for writing annotated bibliographies, literary analysis and research essays. May be repeated for credit one time. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Research Methods
description: Introduction to and practical application of research methods appropriate for conducting research in various contexts. Investigation of a variety of research approaches and strategies. Students will design, conduct, report and evaluate research projects. May be repeated for up to six credits. May not be applied to a graduate degree.
prerequisites: Admission to IEPATH program, IEP Placement scores

title: Intensive Semester Learning Experience
description: ISLE dedicates a semester of coursework to a discrete project. Individually-designed immersive learning experiences encourage creative responses to tangible challenges through the development of creative/research projects. With modeling and mentoring, students work collaboratively and cross-disciplinarily, develop projects from start to finish, present their results, and build relationships with community partners.

title: Intensive Semester Learning Experience
description: ISLE dedicates a semester of coursework to a discrete project. Individually-designed immersive learning experiences encourage creative responses to tangible challenges through the development of creative/research projects. With modeling and mentoring, students work collaboratively and cross-disciplinarily, develop projects from start to finish, present their results, and build relationships with community partners.

title: Intensive Semester Learning Experience
description: ISLE dedicates a semester of coursework to a discrete project. Individually-designed immersive learning experiences encourage creative responses to tangible challenges through the development of creative/research projects. With modeling and mentoring, students work collaboratively and cross-disciplinarily, develop projects from start to finish, present their results, and build relationships with community partners.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Prior Learning Portfolio Development
description: Discuss and apply prior learning frameworks and methods for presenting experiences outside of a traditional classroom as verifiable college-level learning. Create a Prior Learning Portfolio, which includes an educational narrative, a skills-based prior learning resume, and relevant supporting documentation, and earn academic credit by taking the program-prepared challenge exams for specific courses.

title: Introduction to Interdisciplinary Professional Studies
description: Explore academic and career goal-setting strategies, innovative thinking frameworks, the connection of theory to practice, academic culture, academic integrity, the vital nature of an academic support community, and the qualities of a reflective and adaptive learner.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Interdisciplinary Professional Studies Online BA, or Interdisciplinary Professional Studies BA

title: Finding Professional Purpose
description: Develop an individual and clear professional purpose by exploring personal values, interests, and skills and by learning how to articulate unique contributions to the workplace.

title: Integrative Thinking
description: Explore the theory and practice of integrative thinking and the skills and strategies necessary to develop an integrative mindset, to integrate personal histories and experiences with new ideas, and to become a reflective and adaptive learner.

title: Designing Your Work Life
description: Discover and use design thinking principles and frameworks to craft a work life that is meaningful, fulfilling, and aligned with your professional values and goals.

title: Perspective Taking
description: Examine an issue from multiple perspectives and learn to broadly and accurately grasp other viewpoints, to identify the differences between them, to discover the common ground they share, and to evaluate their soundness.

title: Asking Questions and Framing Problems
description: Question a practical problem holistically to identify its conditions and costs, to discover new connections and relationships, and ultimately, to recognize, understand, and clearly articulate its breadth and complexity.

title: Readings in Professional Studies
description: Readings-based course that allows students to work with faculty to design their own course of study focused on the professional skills and knowledge they need to succeed in their chosen careers. Students will work with the instructor to develop a personalized reading list and assignment schedule that aligns with their career goals.

title: Case Studies in Leadership
description: Introduces and analyzes effective leadership styles. Leadership practices and models are applied to case studies. Through various forms of reading, writing, presentations, video and/or multi-media, students will apply theories to assess their own leadership style and identify styles of popular companies/people. Completion of LEAD 325 is recommended.

title: Globalization
description: Introduces the historical and modern phenomenon of Globalization. Various themes in globalization will be studied, including: economics, national powers, history, trade, environment, religion, and education. Each module will introduce particular aspects of globalization.

title: Ethics
description: Examines universal ethics principles and standards practiced across various disciplines. Exploration of personal and professional conduct and social responsibility in the light of existing ethical, moral and social values across disciplines will also be discussed. Designed to enable students to form individual positions on ethical conduct and social responsibility, this course both identifies and applies ethical principles to real world situations.

title: Project Management and Design
description: Develops a foundation of concepts that support the project management process groups required for successful implementation and completion of a project. Principles and applied techniques of effective planning, communication, risk, schedule, and cost management are major themes discussed in this course.

title: Creativity and Collaboration
description: Explore and apply the elements of a creative and collaborative mindset to generate original and adaptive solutions to challenging problems. Review and practice the stages of the creative process, from risk to revision, and set individual goals to develop more deliberate and productive creative collaborations.

title: The Practice of Wellness
description: Explore the eight dimensions of wellness and their connections, from support networks and stress management to physical and financial wellness. Evaluate personal well-being, enhance self-awareness, and set goals to create a holistic personal growth and wellness plan.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of junior or higher
Declared or admitted to Interdisciplinary Professional Studies Online BA
or permission of instructor

title: Community Impact and Leadership Seminar I: Discovery and Teamwork
description: Discover and evaluate core leadership competencies, enhance self-awareness, and develop an individual leadership portfolio.

title: Community Impact and Leadership Seminar II: Emotional Intelligence
description: Explore emotional intelligence assessments to develop more effective communication strategies, to nurture healthy and productive relationships, and to improve essential emotional and social leadership skills.

title: Community Impact and Leadership Seminar III: Integration and Design
description: Identify a community-focused challenge, connect with local stakeholders, and design an integrative, authentic, and community-driven solution concept.

title: Community-based Learning Project
description: Explore the dynamics of community development, social impact, and solution-driven community collaborations. Apply community-based learning principles, theories, and practices to create a meaningful partnership and community-based or service-oriented project with a local organization, nonprofit, or community group.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division
or permission of instructor.

title: Internship
description: A supervised on-site or remote experiential learning opportunity. Deepen critical knowledge areas, enhance professional skills, and reflect on applied work experiences and academic and professional goals. Internships can be in any field or area of interest and require the approval of the Internship Coordinator. One credit equals 45 hours of on-site or remote field work. May be repeated up to a maximum of 9 credits.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Interdisciplinary Professional Studies Capstone (FF)
description: Complete and present an integrative capstone project and a culminating self-evaluation.

title: Skill Advancement I
description: Through guided reflection and career coaching, students will develop the foundational knowledge to connect skills, interests, and values to professional aspirations. Areas explored include personal assessment, professional brand generation, and academic plan development for intentional and purposeful career-building.

title: Skill Advancement II
description: Build on career aspirations through exploration of interests, occupational trends, and professional brand. Identify gaps in knowledge, skills, and abilities and reflect upon how to further develop your academic and co-curricular plan.

title: Technology Essentials for the Modern Workplace
description: Transition technology experience into skills essential for operating in a modern, collaborative workplace. This project-based class covers core project management functions such as standardized file naming conventions, file sharing, and building competency in using workplace software and platforms.

title: Data Informed Problem Solving
description: Prepare for any industry by developing comfort in defining research questions and measures, collecting and analyzing data,  and interpreting and communicating results in an open manner. Utilize the critical thinking process to create effective and informed strategies to solve a variety of problems.

title: Creating and Communicating Professional Content
description: Develop the skills and knowledge to produce content for the professional workplace that informs and inspires action.

title: Fostering Cooperation and Innovation
description: Develop strategies to navigate times of change in the workplace while also fostering professional and cooperative relationships. Explore skill areas, including pitching entrepreneurial ideas, innovating through conflict, and communicating in various professional interactions.

title: IDS Practicum
description: Students work directly with a faculty Learning Mentor to reflect upon their developed skills and apply them to their professional aspirations. As an experiential learning opportunity, practicum commonly includes working within an instructional or training context or an applied project. One credit equals 45 hours of on-site or remote field work. May be repeated for a maximum of 3 credits.

title: IDS Internship
description: Students will be introduced to professional experiences, have an opportunity to apply professional skills, and reflect on applied work experiences. Internships will need faculty/coordinator approval and they can be in any field that’s related to the student’s academic and/or professional goals. One credit equals 45 hours of on-site or remote field work. May be repeated for a maximum of 3 credits.

title: Skill Advancement III
description: Students will explore industries of interest and interview current professionals on experience and advice. Reflect on strengths and limitations, and create a comprehensive plan to achieve career goals and continue development of professional skills in the future.

title: Introduction to Interdisciplinary Studies
description: Explore academic and career goal-setting strategies, innovative thinking frameworks, the connection of theory to practice, academic culture, academic integrity, the vital nature of an academic support community, and the qualities of a reflective and adaptive learner.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Interdisciplinary Studies BA, or Interdisciplinary Studies BS

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Interdisciplinary Studies Capstone (FF)
description: Complete and present an integrative capstone project and a culminating self-evaluation.

title: Go Global: You and the World Economy
description: Students will gain an intermediate level of understanding of international trade and business practices, as well as the historical influences of culture and ethics within the context of the global business environment. This course will promote greater awareness of the world as a community in which we all participate and are responsible.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Asia Biztech: Doing Business in Asia-Pacific
description: Introduces students to the basics of doing business in the Indo-Pacific region. Course will include an overview of the history, politics, economies, technological development, and cultures of the various countries within the region and how they interact both regionally and globally, and explores their influence on how business is conducted. Course includes online meetings with representatives from various regional actors.

title: Cultures, Communication, and Global Business
description: Defines both culture and communication broadly and explores their influence on the conduct of business in the international arena. Includes linkages between culture and communication in general; and specifically, the impact of dimensions such as education, language, historical experience, social structure, and diplomatic relations on bilateral and international trade.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission

title: Importing and Exporting Procedures
description: Focusing on exporting and importing, this course offers practical experience in international trade. Specifically, the course will cover payment and financial procedures, export procedures and documents, shipment methods, counter trade, and resources available for importers and exporters.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
INTBUS230 - Go Global: You and the World Economy (3)

title: International Management Simulation
description: The course uses a computer-simulated business game to provide teams of students the opportunity to learn how firms from Japan, the U.S., and Germany compete in a global economy.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
INTBUS230 - Go Global: You and the World Economy (3)

title: International Trade and Investment Law
description: The law and policy of international economic institutions (e.g., World Trade Organization, NAFTA), national government regulation and private law affecting international transactions in trade in goods, services, technology, and investments. Also selected issues in U.S. foreign/trade policy and ethical/social responsibility. Recommended completion of INTBUS 230. Cross-listed with POLS 445, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of senior or higher
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
POLS305 - Comparative Global Politics (3)
POLS306 - International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods (3)

title: Global Strategy
description: Covers how to take effective action in the global business environment through strategic frameworks that consider market choices, investment and ownership modes, management processes, sustainability, and the role of government and industry in strategically promoting economic development.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to International Business BBA
Completed the following: 
BUS334 - International Management (3)
INTBUS230 - Go Global: You and the World Economy (3)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: International Business Internship
description: Internships with local and overseas companies who work in international business are available to INTBUS majors who meet internship requirements. Recommended admission to COBE, a cumulative GPA of 2.5; a business GPA of 3.0; a current resume submitted to the INTBUS office; and a recommendation of faculty advisor.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Elementary Japanese I (FH)
description: Develops beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. The course also introduces Katakana, Hiragana, and a limited number of Chinese characters. Course conducted in Japanese.

title: Elementary Japanese II (FH)
description: Continues to develop beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. The course also introduces hiragana, katakana, and a limited number of kanji characters. Course conducted in Japanese. Introduces students to Japanese culture.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JAPANESE101 - Elementary Japanese I (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Japanese I (FH)
description: Develops conversational skills including the casual, honorific, and humble styles of Japanese speaking. Additional emphasis placed on formal and colloquial writing through a combination of Katakana, Hiragana, and Kanji. These oral and written skills are practiced through study of Japanese culture and literature. Course conducted in Japanese.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JAPANESE102 - Elementary Japanese II (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Japanese II (FH)
description: Continues to develop conversational skills including the casual, honorific, and humble styles of Japanese speaking. Additional emphasis placed on formal and colloquial writing through a combination of Katakana, Hiragana, and Kanji. These oral and written skills are practiced through study of Japanese culture and literature. Course conducted in Japanese.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JAPANESE201 - Intermediate Japanese I (FH) (4)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Advanced Japanese
description: Refines conversational skills including the casual, honorific, and humble styles of Japanese speaking. Additional emphasis placed on formal and colloquial writing through a combination of Katakana, Hiragana, and Kanji. These oral and written skills are practiced through study of Japanese culture and literature. Course conducted in Japanese.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JAPANESE202 - Intermediate Japanese II (FH) (4)

title: Kanji
description: Introduction of new kanji symbols. Emphasis on reading and writing kanji.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JAPANESE201 - Intermediate Japanese I (FH) (4)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Reporting and News Writing
description: Fundamentals of reporting, from techniques of interviewing and fact-gathering through the construction of the news story. Emphasis on accuracy, conciseness, and clarity in writing. Study of newspaper styles, usage, grammar, punctuation, capitalization, and the use of copy editing symbols.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA201 - Intro to Integrated Media and Strategic Communications (2)

title: Advanced Journalistic Writing
description: Advanced instruction in various forms of journalistic writing, including feature and critical writing.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JOUR301 - Reporting and News Writing (3)

title: Reporting Public Affairs
description: Theory and practice of covering governmental and community affairs. Examination of the beat system and developing sources.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
JOUR301 - Reporting and News Writing (3)

title: Studies in Journalistic Comm
description: Advanced instruction in theories about, history of, and preparation of nonfiction content for the mass media. Content varies from semester to semester. Subjects may include public affairs reporting, journalism history, documentary scriptwriting, etc. Course may be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA201 - Intro to Integrated Media and Strategic Communications (2)

title: Instructional Tennis
description: Instruction and practice in tennis activities emphasizing concepts, fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Indoor Racket Activities
description: Instruction and practice in badminton, pickle ball, and table tennis emphasizing fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Water Safety Instructor
description: Designed to teach skills necessary to become an American Red Cross certified Water Safety Instructor. Strong swimming skills recommended. Special fee required.

title: Instructional Volleyball
description: Instruction and practice in volleyball activities emphasizing fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Basketball
description: Instruction and practice in basketball activities emphasizing fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Outdoor Education
description: Instruction and practice in a variety of wilderness sports and outdoor recreation activities, emphasizing safety, fundamental skills, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Recreational Games
description: Instruction and practice in flag football and softball, emphasizing fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions, and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Rhythmic Skills/Dance
description: Instruction and practice in rhythmic skills and dance, emphasizing fundamental skills, teaching progressions and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Instructional Soccer and Lacrosse
description: Instruction and practice in soccer and  lacrosse activities, emphasizing fundamental skills, rules, strategies, teaching progressions, and learning cues. Five-week course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS

title: Taping and Wrapping Techniques in Athletic Training
description: Instructs students in a variety of wrapping and taping procedures used in the field of athletic training as forms of external support. A prerequisite for admission to the Athletic Training Education Program.

title: Personal Health
description: Covers nutrition, diseases, health needs, services, drugs, family living, and personality structure and development. Enhances student adjustment toward effective functioning in a changing environment.

title: Living Learning Community: BroncoFit
description: First-year BroncoFit Living Learning Community participants will learn about the campus and community resources, explore the eight dimensions of wellness, are civically engaged, and participate in service projects. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: Introduction to Sport Coaching
description: An exploration of the principles, issues, and responsibilities related to the practice of sport coaching including the role of sport in society and leadership in sport settings comprising philosophy, leadership styles, communication, group dynamics, and teaching and instruction.

title: Introduction to Strength and Conditioning I
description: Introduction to and participation in the major movements, techniques, and principles of resistance training and conditioning.

title: Introduction to Strength and Conditioning II
description: Introduction to and participation in advanced movements, techniques, and principles of resistance training and conditioning to include Olympic weightlifting.

title: Introduction to Kinesiology
description: Introductory investigation into the scientific principles of physical activity, human movement, and exercise as it relates to personal and population health.  Topics will include basic exercise physiology, biomechanics, motor behavior, cardiovascular endurance, strength and conditioning, and fitness. Students will also explore various professional and career opportunities related to the study of kinesiology.

title: Cultural, Historical, and Philosophical Dimensions of Physical Activity
description: A study of sociocultural, historical, and philosophical factors and issues that influence attitudes about and practices of physical activity.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL101 - Writing and Rhetoric I (FW) (3)

title: Fundamentals of Human Performance and Fitness
description: Basic principles of human performance and fitness principles for improving cardio-respiratory endurance, strength, power, flexibility, body composition and overall physical wellness. Exposure to the use of modern technology and equipment  in the management of fitness programs to improve performance.

title: Introduction to Athletic Injuries
description: A survey course introducing the principles of care and prevention of sport induced injury. Emphasis will be on identification and differentiation of minor and major trauma related to sports participation. A prerequisite for admission to the Athletic Training Education Program.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL107 - Introduction to Human Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)

title: Applied Principles of Nutrition in Exercise and Sport
description: Principles of nutrition in relation to exercise and sport. Practical understanding of food and nutrient choices before, during and after exercise training and/or competition. Designed for students who want to become exercise science professionals, exercise trainers, fitness instructors, or coaches.

title: Human Sexuality
description: The study of individual sexuality, emphasizing both physiological and psychological aspects. Topics include sexual anatomy and physiology, sexual response cycle, childbirth, contraception, sexual dysfunction, sex role development, and sexual deviation. Cross-cultural values will be examined and a values clarification unit will be included.

title: Residential College: Health Professions
description: Required course for students residing in the University Housing Health Professions Residential College. Students learn about the campus and community resources, explore various health-related professions, are civically engaged, and participate in service projects. Cross-listed with HLTH 250, may be taken once for credit.

title: Introduction to Teaching Physical Education
description: Foundations in the history and philosophy of physical education and fundamentals in pedagogical strategies and theory. Basic tenets of sound teaching will be discussed and applied.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Kinesiology BS

title: Applied Anatomy
description: Investigation of human osteology, myology, anthropology, and neurology as they relate to movement. Emphasis is on application of the knowledge of human anatomy to the principles underlying human movement.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL107 - Introduction to Human Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Internship
description: Practicum field experience in physical education-related areas. Practical experience utilizing theory and practice of the assigned activity in various settings. Required in some options.

title: Statistics, Measurement and Evaluation Concepts
description: Scientific reasoning approaches will be presented that enable students to make reliable and valid judgments based on empirical data. Topics include basic descriptive, correlational and inferential statistics, basic measurement theory of reliability, validity, and objectivity, with emphasis on these statistics and theories associated with the assessment of health and human performance.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH157 - Foundations of Number and Operations (FM) (3)

title: Neuroanatomy and Physiology
description: An introduction to human neuroanatomy and physiology including nomenclature, nervous system cellular and structural organization, major landmarks, sensory and motor systems, and main pathways.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL107 - Introduction to Human Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)

title: Adapted Physical Education
description: Course is designed to acquaint physical educators with the unique needs of the disabled. Emphasis will be on planning activities, games, sports, and exercise programs that will contribute to the special student's developmental health and wellness.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Exercise Physiology
description: Instruction in the physiological and biochemical changes accompanying exercise and training with emphasis on bioenergetics, metabolism, and the muscular, cardiovascular, and pulmonary systems.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CHEM101 - Introduction to Chemistry (FN) (3)
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)
or permission of instructor

title: Laboratory for Exercise Physiology
description: The laboratory to accompany KINES 330.

title: Nutrition in Exercise and Sport
description: An integration of exercise physiology and nutrition, this course will investigate nutrition for health, exercise, and athletic performance benefits.

title: Exercise Physiology Recitation
description: Review, discussion, and application of concepts introduced in KINES 330 Exercise Physiology.

title: Applied Principles of Exercise Science
description: Principles of exercise and sport physiology including how human movement can help improve health and performance. Designed for students who want to become exercise science professionals, exercise trainers, fitness instructors, or coaches.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)

title: Elementary School PE Methods and Evaluation
description: Instruction in methods and assessments of teaching elementary school physical education emphasizing movement needs, analysis, development of skills, evaluation techniques, and practical application. Must be completed with a C or higher.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Completed the following: 
KINES251 - Introduction to Teaching Physical Education (3)

title: Field Experience for Elementary School Physical Education Methods
description: Sixty-hour teaching experience at an elementary school. Observation of teaching/learning process and demonstration of teaching competence in a classroom setting.

title: Elementary School Health and Physical Education Curriculum and Instruction
description: Required for elementary education majors. Planning, organization, and management techniques for teaching elementary school health and physical education. The health content focuses on issues, trends, practices, individual/social health problems, and topic sequencing, while the physical education portion emphasizes movement needs, skill analysis/development, and activity progressions. Elementary education majors also seeking a health teaching endorsement will take the PE-only option (1-credit). Elementary health will be fulfilled in KINES 445 as part of the health teaching endorsement. Students not seeking a health teaching endorsement will take the full 3-credit course.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Psychology of Sport Coaching
description: An examination of psychological aspects of the coaching profession including concepts focused on motivation, communication, stress and anxiety, and team dynamics and cohesion.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES181 - Introduction to Sport Coaching (3)

title: Conditioning and Exercise Physiology Principles for Sport
description: Best practices and developmental considerations on cardiovascular training and strength conditioning procedures for children, adolescents, and young adults. Emphasis on safety factors, along with planning and administration of training programs.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES181 - Introduction to Sport Coaching (3)

title: Sport Coaching Methods and Administration
description: Instructional methods of sport coaching in diverse contexts with an emphasis on sport pedagogy and skill acquisition, planning, leadership development, and administration.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES181 - Introduction to Sport Coaching (3)

title: Exercise Psychology
description: Issues related to the differentiation between physical activity and exercise, benefits and determinates of physical activity, and models for involvement in physical activity as well as theories of change. Focus on cognitive and social psychological perspectives.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
or permission of instructor

title: Social Psychology of Sport and Physical Activity
description: Overview of fundamental concepts, principles, and theories related to the psychology of human behavior in sport and exercise settings. Emphasis on understanding how competition, feedback and reinforcement, personality, motivation, anxiety, and sport injuries affect performance and psychological make-up of participants.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
or permission of instructor

title: Biomechanics
description: Instruction in anatomical and mechanical considerations applied to human motion in sport and exercise.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH144 - Precalculus II: Trigonometry (2)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHYS111 - General Physics I (FN) (4)
PHYS211 - Physics I with Calculus (FN) (4)
Earned a minimum grade of C+ in each of the following courses: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)
or permission of instructor

title: Laboratory for Biomechanics
description: The laboratory to accompany KINES 370.

title: Applied Principles of Biomechanics
description: Introduction to basic concepts of biomechanics and application to sport and physical activity.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)

title: Motor Learning and Human Performance
description: Focus on the acquisition of motor skills to enhance motor performance along with a basic understanding how decisions are made and executed from sensory systems, existing knowledge, and previous movement experiences. In depth examination of effective learning strategies including demonstrations, verbal cueing, attentional focus, designing and organizing practice schedules, as well as correcting errors. Meet with an advisor to determine if the corresponding lab, KINES 376, is required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)
KINES303 - Neuroanatomy and Physiology (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Laboratory for Motor Learning and Human Performance
description: The laboratory to accompany KINES375.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Motor Development and Human Behavior
description: Designed around principles of motor development that are related to movement across the lifespan. Topics will include, but are not limited to, models of motor development, fundamental motor skills that lead to successful movement patterns, and physical changes that occur in children, adolescents, and adults. Raises a critical awareness of the issues surrounding facilitators and barriers that will influence sport and/or physical activity participation across all ages. Meet with an advisor to determine if the lab, KINES379, is required.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Laboratory for Motor Development and Human Behavior
description: This laboratory accompanies KINES378.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Head and Neck Anatomy
description: Use of human cadavers to study prosections of head and neck with emphasis on clinical relevance. Integument, osteology, myology, circulatory systems, lymphatics, oral and dental tissues, neuroanatomy, cranial nerves, general innervation, and salivary glands. Cross-listed with ZOOL 403, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Foundations of Physical Activity and Health
description: This course explores the impact of physical activity on individual and community health including the benefits of improving health and preventing chronic disease.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Sedentary Behavior and Health
description: Explore the role of sedentary behavior in the development of chronic disease and interventions specific to the following four domains: a) discretionary, recreational, and leisure-time, b) transportation, c) occupational, and d) the household.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of junior or higher
or permission of instructor

title: Neuromechanics
description: Explores the neural aspects of human movement. Topics include neuroanatomy, voluntary and involuntary motor control, neural aspects of motor behaviors and systems-level functions and dysfunctions of the nervous system.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
KINES370 - Biomechanics (3)
KINES375 - Motor Learning and Human Performance (2)

title: Athletic Training Clinical Instruction VI
description: Clinical instruction review in the organization and administration procedures and techniques of prevention, evaluation, treatment of common injuries/illnesses within Athletic Training, and supervised clinical experiences.

title: Lab Techniques in Biomechanics (FF)
description: An introduction to the analysis techniques used to study the mechanics of human motion. Topics include cinematography, videography, force transducers, electromyography and computer analysis techniques.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES370 - Biomechanics (3)

title: Athletic Therapy and Corrective Exercise
description: Theory and application of human movement, corrective exercise, and physiological recovery strategies to prevent or reduce athletic related injuries. Emphasis on system- and technology-based solutions that promote optimal human performance. Scientific evidence supporting design and implementation of injury prevention programs are discussed.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)

title: Athletic Therapy and Corrective Exercise Lab
description: Laboratory to accompany KINES 427

title: Neuromechanics Research
description: Apply data collection and analysis strategies by designing and conducting a research project and composing a research abstract.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES425 - Lab Techniques in Biomechanics (FF) (3)

title: Physical Activity for Special Populations
description: Impact and practical applications of physical activity and exercise across the life span in individuals with various physical, cognitive, and/or developmental disabilities. Specific considerations include general exercise recommendations, exercise facility design, particular training equipment, and risk factor modification and contraindications to exercise.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of junior or higher
or permission of instructor

title: Conditioning Procedures (FF)
description: Instruction to conditioning procedures with emphasis on program planning, objectives, exercise analysis, and prescription.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
KINES330 - Exercise Physiology  (3)
or permission of instructor

title: Laboratory for Conditioning Procedures
description: Laboratory to accompany KINES 432.

title: Neuromechanics Research
description: Apply data collection and analysis strategies in designing a research project and abstract.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES425 - Lab Techniques in Biomechanics (FF) (3)

title: Exercise Testing and Prescription (FF)
description: Instruction in current procedures for clinical exercise testing including patient screening, pre-test procedures, basic electrocardiography, submaximal assessments, symptom limited graded exercise testing, test result interpretation and exercise prescription.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division
or permission of instructor

title: Laboratory for Exercise Testing and Prescription
description: Laboratory to accompany KINES 436.

title: Qualitative Analysis of Human Movement
description: An integrated approach to qualitative analysis involving the systematic and critical observation of motor skill performance. This course utilizes basic video analysis and motion capture tools.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES375 - Motor Learning and Human Performance (2)
KINES370 - Biomechanics (3)

title: Medical Aspects of Exercise
description: Explores the role of exercise and physical activity in the prevention and treatment of chronic disease. Focusing on pathophysiology, exercise responses, and benefits of exercise specific to many of the most prevalent chronic diseases impacting our society.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
KINES330 - Exercise Physiology  (3)
HLTH300 - Pathophysiology (4)

title: Elementary and Secondary School Health Methods and Administration
description: Emphasis is placed on school health education instructional methods, health literacy strategies, and current classroom administrative practices at the elementary and secondary level. Field experience is included.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Secondary School Physical Education Methods and Evaluation
description: Instruction and practice in teaching styles and strategies, assessments and evaluation, reflection, and class management and organization for teaching Physical Education at the secondary (6-12) level. Must be completed with a C or higher.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Professional Year Admission
Completed the following: 
KINES251 - Introduction to Teaching Physical Education (3)

title: Field Experience for Secondary School Physical Education Methods
description: Sixty-hour teaching experience at a secondary school. Observation of teaching/learning process and demonstration of teaching competence in a classroom setting.

title: Organization and Administration of Physical Education
description: Instruction in organization and administration of physical education and athletic programs. Emphasis on the role of physical education and athletics in the total education program. Required of all physical education teaching majors.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Curriculum Design and Administration in Physical Education (FF)
description: The planning of a school physical education program including the activity selection, sequencing unit development, program models, evaluation, funding, grant writing, and equipment considerations.

title: Professional Year Elementary Teaching Experience
description: Supervised student teaching in an elementary school. Students are placed with a master physical education teacher for one half-semester (full-time) under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS
Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Professional Year Secondary Teaching Experience
description: Supervised student teaching in either a junior or senior high school. Students are placed with a master physical education teacher for one half-semester (full-time) under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to K-12 Physical Education and Health BS
Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Health Coaching Practice
description: Supervised field experience in one-on-one health coaching sessions alongside classroom meetings designed to unpack health coaching experiences to best practices. To sit for the Health Coaching exam, a minimum of 50 sessions of at least 20 minutes per session will be required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
KINES363 - Exercise Psychology (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ADST448 - Motivational Interviewing (3)
PSYC357 - Introduction to Counseling Skills (3)

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Current Trends in Rehabilitation Science
description: Exploration and discussion of current and emerging trends in the research and practice of musculoskeletal rehabilitation. Students will also be exposed to the use of modern technology in the management of rehabilitation and exercise programs in a variety of settings.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
KINES270 - Applied Anatomy (3)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Research Methods in Health
description: Design of experiments, methods of analysis, interpretation of results, and use of research to support evidence-based practice.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
PSYC295 - Statistical Methods (3)
SOC310 - Elementary Social Statistics (3)
KINES301 - Statistics, Measurement and Evaluation Concepts (3)

title: Technology in Physical Activity and Sport
description: Exploration of the uses and impact of integrated technology in physical activity, sport, and health. Topics include sports performance and physical activity assessment, the impact of social media, and the use of apps and videography.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Sport Coaching Practicum
description: Provides students with a supervised practical experience in sport coaching under the direct supervision of a qualified mentor.

title: Internship in Kinesiology
description: Practical field experience in emphasis areas of Kinesiology. Opportunity to apply knowledge and theory learned in the classroom to the practical setting. Required in some areas of emphasis. Areas of emphasis may maintain policies applicable to this internship. May be repeated for credit, maximum of six credits. Recommended junior standing.

title: Telehealth in Physical Activity and Health
description: Telemedicine and telehealth technologies and processes for the delivery of physical activity and exercise programming to improve health and performance. Topics include technology requirements, planning a telehealth workflow, health equity in telehealth, telehealth policy, telemedicine for patient empowerment, telecare for remote locations, and legal provisions for telehealth implementation.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Elementary Korean I (FH)
description: Develops beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Introduces aspects of Korean culture.

title: Elementary Korean II (FH)
description: Continues to develop beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Continues introducing aspects of Korean culture.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KOREAN101 - Elementary Korean I (FH) (4)

title: Elementary Korean Language and Culture
description: Conversational practice in Korean oriented to common situations in daily routines. Particular emphasis is given to phonetic accuracy and appropriate use of idioms. Field trips to practice Korean conversation in specific cultural settings. Typically taught abroad in Korea.

title: Intermediate Korean I (FH)
description: Builds communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Korean culture. Course conducted primarily in Korean.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KOREAN102 - Elementary Korean II (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Korean II (FH)
description: Continues building communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Korean culture. Course conducted primarily in Korean.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KOREAN201 - Intermediate Korean I (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Korean Language and Culture
description: Designed to improve spoken proficiency through cultural experiences and field trips. Typically taught abroad in Korea.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KOREAN102 - Elementary Korean II (FH) (4)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Elementary Classical Latin (FH)
description: An intensive introduction to the basic vocabulary, grammar and syntax of classical Latin with emphasis on comprehension of the nominal declension and verbal conjugation forms of the language. Survey of Roman republican literature with illustrative reading passages excerpted from the ancient authors.

title: Advanced Classical Latin (FH)
description: Second semester of the intensive introduction to the study of classical Latin with emphasis on comprehension of the advanced grammatical forms and syntactical patterns of the language. Survey of Roman imperial literature with translations and analysis of extended historical and literary texts from the ancient authors.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LATIN211 - Elementary Classical Latin (FH) (4)

title: The Augustan Age
description: Translation and analysis of classical texts from authors of the “Golden Age of Latin Literature,” such as Cicero, Caesar, Vergil, and Livy. Survey of materials and methods of teaching Latin in secondary schools.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LATIN212 - Advanced Classical Latin (FH) (4)

title: Early Church Latin Literature
description: Translation and analysis of selections from the major writings of the Latin Fathers of the early Church, such as Tertullian, Cyprian, Lactantius, Ambrose, Jerome and Augustine.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LATIN212 - Advanced Classical Latin (FH) (4)

title: The Constantinian Era
description: Translation and analysis of Christian texts from the Constantinian Era, such as imperial biographies, laws, letters, and creeds. Survey of materials and methods of teaching Latin in secondary schools.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LATIN212 - Advanced Classical Latin (FH) (4)

title: Medieval Latin Literature
description: Translation and analysis of selections from significant medieval Latin writers, such as the papal biographers, Egeria, Gregory of Tours, the Venerable Bede, Einhard, Pope Gregory VII, Fulcher of Chartres, Abelard and Jacques De Vitry.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LATIN212 - Advanced Classical Latin (FH) (4)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.
