title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Civic Engagement and Leadership
description: Provides students with opportunities to learn about political and social community dynamics while becoming catalysts for collaborative social change. Students will integrate service, education, and reflection to create meaningful change in communities. The culmination of this learning will take place on an intensive weeklong service trip. Students must be accepted into alternative break program prior to registration. May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credits.

title: Foundations of Leadership
description: An introduction to concepts, frameworks, ideas and beliefs related to leadership. Intended to inspire students to engage in deeper self-exploration about why they lead and how they can begin to serve as a catalyst for progress in their relationships, communities and organizations.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: The Practice of Leadership
description: An exploration of selected concepts, frameworks, ideas and beliefs related to the art and practice of leadership. The course is intended to help students move from knowledge and awareness, to applying their learning in various contexts.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
LEAD325 - Foundations of Leadership (3)
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Relational Leadership
description: The purpose of this course is to enhance the critical leadership component of working effectively with others. Tools will be provided for developing the internal qualities and the external behaviors that lead to both individual and team success. The completion of LEAD 325 is recommended.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Studies in Leadership
description: Examination of special topics and skills in leadership. Content varies from semester to semester. Subjects may include leading groups, leading change, gender and leadership, creativity and leadership, etc. Course may be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Capstone in Leadership
description: This experiential course offers students an opportunity to practice the skills and perspectives they have learned in previous leadership courses. Students will select a team-based community or business project to organize and implement as a vehicle to demonstrate and reflect upon leadership principles. The completion of LEAD 326 is recommended.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
LEAD325 - Foundations of Leadership (3)
IPS410 - Case Studies in Leadership (3)
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Leadership Experience
description: This experiential course offers students an opportunity to practice the skills and perspectives they have learned in previous leadership courses. Students will select a team-based community or business project to organize and implement as a vehicle to demonstrate and reflect upon leadership principles. The completion of LEAD 326 is recommended.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
LEAD325 - Foundations of Leadership (3)
LEAD327 - Relational Leadership (3)
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Independent Study in Leadership
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Library Research Skills I
description: Practical research skills with a focus on thinking critically about information. At the end of the course, students will know how to develop a research question, locate and evaluate information in a variety of types, and understand the basics of information ethics.

title: AI, Information Literacy, and You
description: Introduction to the basics of generative AI tools. Investigate issues related to identifying misleading AI-generated content. Learn techniques for refining prompts to maximize the use of generative AI.

title: Library Research Skills II
description: Advanced research skills that will help conduct high-level, scholarly research to produce an academic paper, while also developing an understanding of ethical research practices and plagiarism. Develop and deliver a presentation on their paper and be able to discuss their research methods.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LIBR106 - Library Research Skills I (1)

title: Language Myths (FS)
description: A critical examination of commonly held myths about language. Topics may include dialects of English, the relationship between language and social identity, grammatical correctness, and language use in bilingual households. This course welcomes students from U.S. and international backgrounds.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as Pass/Fail.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Introduction to Language Studies
description: A general survey of contemporary language study as it is carried on in the fields of linguistics, anthropology, and psychology, with emphasis on meaning, sounds, words, and sentence formation in English.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: English Grammar for Teachers
description: A comprehensive overview of the systems of English grammar, with a focus on applications for teaching in k-12 and English as a Second Language contexts.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: History of the English Language
description: A study of the periods in the development of English, from Indo-European and Germanic backgrounds to Early Modern English with a focus on both linguistic and social forces of change, development of writing system, and dialects of English. Includes concentrated work with written documents in English language history.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Introduction to Phonetics and Phonology
description: Survey of the fields of phonetics and phonology. Topics in phonetics include: familiarization with the articulation and transcription of speech sounds, vocal tract anatomy, acoustics, hearing and perception. Topics in phonology include: The role of phonemes, phonological analysis, features, and syllable structure. Includes laboratory exercises.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Second Language Acquisition
description: An introduction to the human capacity to learn additional languages, after one’s native language(s) have been acquired. Examines language acquisition processes across the lifespan, in both naturalistic and instructed settings.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Introduction to Morphology and Syntax
description: This course employs linguistic principles to study morphology and syntax. Rather than focus on prescribed grammar, this class focuses on descriptive grammar. Students will examine the morphological and syntactic structures in terms of abstract categories and theory, with illustrations from various languages.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Introduction to Sociolinguistics
description: Provides an introduction to the nature of the relationships among language, culture, and society. Major topics explored are language and thought; conversational theory; the ethnography of communication; language change; language variation; speech communities; pidgins and creoles; diglossia, code switching and mixing; and solidarity and politeness. Several languages are examined in specific social and cultural contexts.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Applied Linguistics in Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: Introduction to theories and methods of second language learning and teaching. The course examines the social, historical, and cultural contexts that shape language and language acquisition; relationships between first and second language acquisition; ways in which classroom practices can facilitate acquisition; and the role of individual learner identities in successful second language acquisition.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: The Politics of Language
description: An overview of connections between language and power in social and political arenas. Emphasis on how language and the construction of racial, gender, and other social categories go hand in hand, and how linguists, policymakers, educators, and different "factions" within the general public talk about linguistic issues differently.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Linguistic Methods
description: A survey of linguistic methodologies, this course emphasizes critical evaluation of a wide range of issues and fundamental questions in linguistics with a strong focus on communicating in the discipline.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Corpus Linguistics
description: An introduction to corpus linguistics, an area of language studies that uses computer technology to aid in the collection, storage, and analysis of spoken and written texts.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)
And one additional upper-division linguistics course

title: Psycholinguistics
description: The study of language in relation to mind and cognition. Topics include the relationship between language, thought, and memory; language acquisition; language disorders; and the psychological processes involved in speaking, listening, reading, writing, and spelling.

title: Linguistic Typology
description: Linguistic typology involves exploring linguistic diversity through the systematic comparison and classification of language structures and their associated functions. Offers a broad overview of the field and experience in exploring structural properties of individual languages from a typological perspective.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING318 - Introduction to Morphology and Syntax (3)

title: Advanced Topics in Linguistics
description: Topic and focus may vary. May be repeated for up to six credits.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)
And at least three credits of 300-level LING courses

title: Pedagogical Grammar
description: An examination of issues related to the teaching of grammar in second language contexts, with a particular emphasis on the description of grammar systems, the acquisition of grammar by second language learners, and the relative effectiveness of different instructional approaches.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
LING317 - Second Language Acquisition (3)
LING318 - Introduction to Morphology and Syntax (3)
LING306 - English Grammar for Teachers (3)

title: Indigenous Languages of North America
description: An exploration of the diverse array of languages indigenous to North America from a variety of perspectives (historical, social, cultural, and linguistic).The course emphasizes both the unique grammatical features of the languages and the causes and consequences of the endangerment and loss of these languages.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Seminar on Language and Identity
description: Topic and focus may vary around the central components of sociocultural linguistics as they pertain to the relationship of language and identity. May be repeated for up to six credits.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Advanced Linguistic Methods
description: Provides students with the tools and methodologies necessary for answering a fundamental question in linguistics through research and application in a specific sub-discipline. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING381 - Linguistic Methods (3)

title: Employing Linguistics
description: This course is designed to help students identify opportunities for applying their linguistic skills and training to the task of researching career paths, identifying organizations, tasks, and collaborators of professional interest. Class will meet online for the first 7 weeks of the semester for readings, reflections, and discussions based on readings, and in person for a weekend workshop during week 8 for discussions and activity-based interactions.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Capstone in Linguistics (FF)
description: In this course, students will implement a research or project plan through hands-on practice in a major domain in linguistics whether field, classroom, or lab based.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
LING381 - Linguistic Methods (3)

title: Foundations of Communication for a Globalized World
description: Introduction to fundamental components of oral communication in support of engagement in our globalized world. Theories, philosophies, and concepts of oral communication with application to cross-cultural communication. Identification, understanding, and critical consumption of information presented from different communication traditions. Oral communication as an essential skill for future success in cross-cultural situations.

title: Transitions: Surviving and Thriving in College
description: Developed specifically for students returning to learning after an absence from formal education. Activities include brushing up on study strategies, along with workshops honoring past life experiences and integrating changing roles and identities as a student. Taught in a positive and encouraging environment in support of personal and academic success.

title: Cultural Diversity in the School
description: An introduction to the forms of diversity most relevant to local schools. In addition to issues of race, gender, class, and sexual orientation, the course introduces students to the psychological, legal, and cultural foundations of bilingual education and English as a Second Language with a special emphasis on Mexican-American culture. Field experience component is required.

title: Film and Contemporary Issues in Education
description: Opportunity to view, discuss, critique and analyze how important facets of contemporary issues impact education as represented (or misrepresented) in film. Topics are likely to include diverse and marginalized individuals, educational institutions creating a more socially just society, representation of students, teachers, community or teacher/student relationships, and identity or positioning in film.

title: Migration Studies in Education
description: Introduces future teachers to issues surrounding migrant and refugee children in today's schools. Covers: history of human migration, patterns of displacement, U.S. immigration policies, United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees policies, resettlement issues for families, trauma in newcomer children, local community resources for teachers, and knowledge of specific newcomer groups in Idaho. Includes field experience/service learning requirement.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Foundations of Linguistics and Language Acquisition
description: Understanding of first and second language acquisition theories and implications for the instruction of English language learners in K-12 settings.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS201 - Education, Schooling, and Society (FS) (3)
ED-LLC201 - Cultural Diversity in the School (3)

title: Developing Biliteracy in the Bilingual Classroom
description: Overview of bilingual program models and approaches to teaching biliteracy within different models. Theories and research on the relationship of first and second language literacy development. Application of course content to lesson planning. Instruction is given in both English and Spanish.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
SPAN202 - Intermediate Spanish II (FH) (4)

title: Methods in Teaching Content to Bilingual and English Language Learners
description: Instructional strategies, techniques, and methods across the content areas for use with bilingual and English language learners. Application of ELD and state standards to lesson and unit plans that integrate content and language instruction. Focus on differentiation and instruction that embeds assessment and scaffolding.

title: Spanish for the Bilingual Classroom
description: A literature-based oral and written communication course for the extended opportunities in expressing and comprehending ideas in Spanish, as it relates to the context of the bilingual classroom. Students may be assigned to local public schools and/or community to gain practice in using the language of the local speech community. Course conducted in Spanish.

title: Field Experience with Bilingual or English Language Learners
description: A field placement working with bilingual or English language learners in a public school setting. Teacher candidate will support individual students or small groups under the guidance of a mentor teacher.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ED-LLC300 - Foundations of Linguistics and Language Acquisition (3)

title: Assessment of Bilingual and English Language Learners
description: Issues of assessment for Bilingual and English language learners including purposes, advantages, and limitations of assessments, including accommodations. Understanding of state and national requirements for identification and exit from language support programs. Classroom-based assessments of content-area learning and language development.

title: Idaho Comprehensive Literacy
description: Provides pre-service teachers with knowledge and strategies involving children's oral language, phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, assessment and intervention, and the role these play in developing literacy with diverse students. Prepares pre-service teachers to meet part of the literacy requirements for an Idaho teaching credential. Includes a field-based experiential component of forty (40) hours.

title: Reading Diagnosis and Intervention
description: A study of reading difficulties of elementary or secondary students with emphasis on diagnosis, as well as intervention materials and methods for teaching reading. After a period of classroom instruction students tutor an elementary or secondary student for approximately 20 sessions.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ED-LLC340 - Idaho Comprehensive Literacy (4)

title: Writing Process, Instruction and Assessment for K-8 Classrooms
description: Develops teacher candidates' knowledge, skills, and dispositions about writing processes, written genres, and students' writing development. Provides opportunities to practice planning, instruction, and assessment skills. Prepares candidates to use Idaho Core Standards for Writing and Language Arts.

title: Children's Literature
description: Books and other resources designed for children are studied and evaluated in terms of literary theory, aesthetic appreciation, collection development and applications with children. Emphasis is placed on literature across the genres with all children in mind and the conventions of communication within the field.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Field Experience in Literacy
description: Literacy-related activities including a variety of skills in the area of reading, writing, and literacy assessment.

title: Constructing a Professional Portfolio (FF)
description: Designed to integrate course content and Professional Year experiences with the opportunity to develop communication skills important in the profession of education. This course helps to achieve the goals of the Foundations program.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Content Area Language Arts: K-8
description: Knowledge, strategies, and tools for comprehension, vocabulary, and introduction to writing of narrative and expository texts in content areas. Prepares pre-service teachers for Standard 2 of the Idaho Comprehensive Literacy Assessment and introduces students to the conventions of communication within the field of teacher education.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Integrated Disciplinary Literacy in the Social Sciences
description: Integrated Disciplinary Literacy in the Social Sciences (K-8). Knowledge, strategies, and tools for integrating comprehension, vocabulary and written text through elementary social studies curricula, philosophies, and methodologies. Prepares pre-service teachers for Standard 2 of the Idaho Comprehensive Literacy Assessment. Focus is on cross disciplinary literacy skills within the ten strands of social studies to develop an integrated unit emphasizing critical thinking, values in a democratic and pluralistic society, and global issues.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Content Literacy for Secondary Students
description: Instructional materials in the various content subjects and instructional strategies to meet reading, writing, and study needs of all learners in today's diverse society. Prepares pre-service teachers for Standard 2 of the Idaho Comprehensive Literacy Assessment. Introduces students to the conventions of communication within the field of teacher education.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Admission to Professional Year for Secondary majors

title: Young Adult Literature
description: Diverse perspectives in young adult literature, including issues in book selection. Intended for teachers, librarians, media generalists, and others working with young adults.

title: Psycholinguistics and Literacy
description: Studies psychological processes and strategies by which readers and writers construct and reconstruct the message of a text. Application of theoretical conclusions to teaching practices.

title: Professional Year I
description: Classroom placement focusing on activities related to planning and preparation of bilingual/ESL curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Teacher candidate will complete a minimum of 250 hours in the K-8 classroom and apply knowledge and skills from all professional education coursework, and participate in weekly seminars with their liaisons.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Professional Year II - Teaching Experience in Bilingual/ESL Education
description: Supervised teaching experience in a classroom with bilingual or English language learners, including activities related to planning and preparation, classroom environments, curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Students will complete a full-time teaching experience, consistent with the calendar of the assigned partnership school under the supervision of university faculty.
prerequisites:

ED-CIFS430 - Fundamental Frameworks for Supporting Teaching and Learning (4)
ED-LLC442 - Integrated Disciplinary Literacy in the Social Sciences (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Orientation
description: Introduces students to the BBA Management program and the fundamentals of online learning. Includes a self-paced foundation or refresher in basic quantitative skills, financial accounting, microeconomics, statistics, and spreadsheets.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA
Approval of Program AND declared Pre-Management

title: Client-focused Design
description: Focus on the four stages of design thinking -- "what is," "what if," "what wows," and "what works." Utilizes advanced brainstorming techniques to generate solutions to identified pain points.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Professional Communication for Managers
description: Provides opportunities to enhance skills in delivering clear, concise, and compelling messages through written and oral communication. This includes meetings and persuasive formal presentations. Students will also develop proficiency in the use of digital media for presentation, collaboration, and interpersonal interaction.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA, or Nonprofit Management Certificate
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Foundations of Management
description: Introduces critical management skills involved in planning, structuring, leading, and controlling in an organization. Emphasizes the analysis and understanding of human behavior in organizations, including teamwork, motivation leadership, power and communication. Additional topics include strategic and operational planning, decision making, and designing adaptive organizations.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Managing Human Resources
description: Introduces the major human resource management functions: planning and recruitment, selection and placement, compensation and benefits, training and development, employee and labor relations, and health, safety, and security.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Marketing
description: Introduces methods of identifying and meeting the wants and needs of people and organizations. Focuses on decisions related to product, price, promotion, and distribution channels, including e-commerce.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Negotiation and Conflict Management
description: Examines negotiation planning, processes, third-party interventions, and conflict management techniques applied in interpersonal, intergroup, organizational, and community settings. Introduces and applies skill development through discussion forums and negotiation role plays.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: International Business Management
description: Examines international business practices, as well as the influence of culture and ethics within the context of the global business environment. Addresses decisions about strategy and entry into overseas markets. Explores functional areas including marketing, finance, and human resource management.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Strategic Tools
description: Examines frameworks and tools used by managers for the strategic analysis and assessment of their competitive environments. Emphasizes developing a working knowledge of application and interpretation of frameworks, tools, and techniques.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Business Intelligence and Analytics
description: Examines information technology resources such as database systems and enterprise systems explained in their role of supporting decision makers. Emphasizes a hands-on-experience using spreadsheets, relational databases, and business intelligence software.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Law for Managers
description: Introduces future managers to the major legal issues involved in the business environment. Emphasizes the implication of these legal issues for business decision-making. Covers legal reasoning and the legal system, agency and business associations, torts, contracts and sales, intellectual property, employment law, and product liability.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Leadership and High Performing Teams
description: Develops an understanding of leadership concepts and principles. Emphasizes leadership skills and the ability to facilitate work in high-performing teams. Coursework includes applied learning within a business development context.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Business Ethics, Responsibility, and Sustainability
description: Explores the role of business in a global society. Includes ethical reasoning and decision-making, business responsibility in social and environmental contexts, and best practices in sustainability.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Operations Management
description: Explores management of the core operations in manufacturing and services firms. Includes planning and control, scheduling, facility location, quality management, supply chain management, and inventory analysis.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Managing Innovation and Change
description: Provides techniques for driving innovation and implementing ensuing business changes into an organization. Explores the role of managers and organizational design in balancing current operations with innovation during the change process.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Finance for Managers
description: Examines the tools and concepts required to make value-added financial decisions. Emphasis on interpretation and analysis of financial reports and data. Topics include financial statement analysis, budgeting, cash flow, time value of money, and capital investment decisions.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Management BBA

title: Capstone (FF)
description: Develops analytical, problem-solving, and decision making skills in situations dealing with complex organizations, with the ultimate objective of formulating policies and strategies, both domestic and worldwide. Builds upon and integrates the knowledge and methods acquired throughout the program to examine all functional areas of the organization. Recommended admission to the Management BBA program

title: Elementary Mandarin Chinese I (FH)
description: Develops beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Introduces Simplified Chinese Characters and aspects of Chinese culture.

title: Elementary Mandarin Chinese II (FH)
description: Continues to develop beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Introduces Simplified Chinese Characters and aspects of Chinese culture.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHINESE101 - Elementary Mandarin Chinese I (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Mandarin Chinese I (FH)
description: Continues building communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Emphasis on Simplified Chinese Characters. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Chinese culture. Course conducted in Chinese.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHINESE102 - Elementary Mandarin Chinese II (FH) (4)

title: Intermediate Mandarin Chinese II (FH)
description: Continues building communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Emphasis on Simplified Chinese Characters. Introduces Traditional Chinese Characters. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Chinese culture. Course conducted in Chinese.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHINESE201 - Intermediate Mandarin Chinese I (FH) (4)

Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Principles of Marketing
description: Understand how to segment markets; target customer groups; and position an organization's offerings via integrated product, price, promotion, and distribution decisions.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ITM105 - Spreadsheet Topics (2)
Or successful completion of the COBE Computer Placement Exam for ITM 105.
Complete 1 of the following
Complete all of the following

title: Consumer Behavior
description: Understand the process by which people make consumption decisions, and the consequences of these decisions on individuals and society. Examine how internal (e.g., cognitive, emotional) and external (e.g., sociocultural, situational) factors drive consumption behaviors and decisions.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission

title: Marketing Insights
description: Generate consumer insights via the marketing research process to support management decision-making and solve marketing problems. Define the research problems, develop research plans, collect data, analyze the data, and communicate recommendations. Course requires statistical skills. Enroll in this course soon after completion of BUSSTAT 208.
prerequisites:

Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, Economics BA, or Business and Economic Analytics BS

MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)

MATH361 - Probability and Statistics I (3)

title: Professional Selling
description: Understand the sales process, ethical selling techniques, digital tools, and careers in sales. Learn to initiate and build long-term business-to-business relationships.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, or Economics BA

title: Resort Sales and Marketing
description: Introduction to sales and marketing theory and principles as applied to resort and hospitality management. The course emphasizes methods of identifying and meeting the wants and needs of people and organizations. The relationship between sales and marketing management techniques is addressed. May be taken for MKTG or ROHM credit, but not both.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission

title: Advertising Agency Operations
description: Understand how an advertising agency works, conduct market research, generate insights for creative action, and write a strategy and communications brief for a national brand.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)

title: Creative Agency Immersion
description: Function as a student-led creative agency and work on an advertising campaign. Students will work together in an agency environment to conceptualize a campaign, design ads, explore communication channels, and pitch the campaign to a client.

title: Marketing Communication
description: Develop comprehensive marketing communication strategies which may include advertising, sales promotions, event sponsorships, direct marketing, public relations, branding, and social and digital media. Consider relevant social, cultural, and ethical issues while developing your integrated marketing communication plan.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MKTG307 - Consumer Behavior (3)

title: Marketing Strategy
description: Apply marketing principles to solve organizational and/or social problems. Identify problems, provide potential solutions, and defend recommended marketing strategy.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)

title: Innovation New Product Development
description: Develop new product and customer experience innovations by applying design thinking concepts. Iterate solutions that best meet customer needs while staying within the bounds of business constraints.
prerequisites:

MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)

title: Category Management
description: Use data-driven shopper insights and an understanding of customer behavior to create value for brands and retailers. This experiential course uses real data in partnership with major Boise-area retailers.
prerequisites:

MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)

title: Marketing Planning Applications
description: Capstone Course. Create a comprehensive marketing plan and present best course of action for a live-case client.

description: Identify opportunities and develop marketing strategies that cross national and cultural borders.

description: Capstone Course. Build, measure, and manage a brand.

title: Sports Marketing
description: Apply marketing principles and concepts including target marketing, segmentation, and positioning to the sports and entertainment industries. Identify critical issues faced by a local sports brand, design solutions, and recommend a strategy to overcome them that includes advertising, promotion, publicity and social media.
prerequisites:

title: Digital Marketing
description: Understand the role of digital tools such as web design, web analytics, search engine optimization, email marketing, social media, and online advertising in marketing strategy.

description: Capstone designed to enhance the ability to apply and conduct strategic market analysis, determine strategic alignment, and formulate specific segmentation, targeting, and positioning strategies as they relate to the goals and objectives of the organization. Teamwork as a simulated marketing department. Conducted as a live case with a client.

Must have a class standing of senior

title: Applied Marketing Management II
description: Capstone builds on MKTG 470 Applied Marketing Management I to leverage strategic market analysis, strategic considerations, targeting, and positioning strategies to develop the marketing mix, tactical execution, measurement techniques, and customer/partner relationships for the organization. Teamwork as a simulated marketing department. Conducted as a live case with a client.

description: Complete supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. Course may be repeated, max 6 credits. (Only 3 of the 6 credits can be used as upper-division elective credit). Recommended admission to COBE and completion of MKTG 301.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN)
description: Introduction to broad classifications of materials, materials properties, and applications of materials

title: Computational Tools for Materials Science
description: Hands on experience solving problems using computers, identifying problems amenable to computation, and a survey of common tools for managing and manipulating code relevant to Materials Science and Engineering field.

title: Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering
description: Exploration of the structure of different classes of materials across length scales from atomic to macroscopic. Relationship between interatomic bonding, structure, and resulting properties.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)

title: Introduction to Materials Science and Engineering Lab
description: Practical experience in testing and processing of engineering materials, data acquisition, data analysis, and technical communication.

title: Intro to Materials Characterization Lab
description: Introduction to laboratory practices including safety, record keeping, error calculation, and ethical practice. Emphasis on characterization techniques and tools used in materials science practice.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Thermodynamics of Materials
description: Basic thermodynamics principles including energy, entropy, and free energy. Equilibrium states, phases and phase transitions of various materials systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)

title: Electrical Properties of Materials
description: Physical principles underlying the electrical properties of metals, insulators, and semiconductors. The effects of energy band structure, thermal properties and impurities on electrical conduction. Concepts covered are applied to electrical devices including nanodevices, MOSFETS, and optoelectronic devices. May be taken for MSE or ECE credit, but not both.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)

title: Mechanical Behavior of Materials
description: Elastic and plastic deformation and fracture in engineering materials, including dislocation theory, alloy hardening and creep deformation, fracture mechanisms, fracture mechanics, toughening of metals, ceramics, and composites, environmentally assisted failure.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)

title: Materials Processing
description: Survey of manufacturing and processing techniques for technological materials including biomaterials, ceramics, metals, nanomaterials, and polymers.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)

title: Phase Transformations and Kinetics
description: Transport Processes and kinetics in materials systems including diffusion, phase transformations, nucleation and growth, gas-solid and liquid-solid reactions, and electrochemical kinetics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)

title: Material Properties Laboratory
description: Use of characterization and analytical techniques for determining the properties of materials, including microstructural, physical, thermodynamic, mechanical, electrical, optical, magnetic, and thermal properties. Emphasis on understanding the significance of, and being able to present, these measurements.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
MSE280 - Intro to Materials Characterization Lab  (2)
Student must have completed FC requirement

title: Materials Analysis Laboratory
description: Use of compositional and structural characterization techniques in materials engineering analysis, with an emphasis on microscopy, spectroscopy and diffraction techniques.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE380 - Material Properties Laboratory (3)
MSE312 - Mechanical Behavior of Materials (3)

title: Electrical, Optical, and Dielectric Materials
description: Physical principles underlying the electrical, dielectric and optical properties of modern solids. Crystalline and energy band structure of materials, thermal properties and electrical conduction in semiconductors and metals, dielectric response and optical behavior of solids are covered.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE311 - Electrical Properties of Materials (3)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)

title: Interfacial Kinetics and Transport Processes
description: Reaction kinetics and mass transport phenomena at materials interfaces important in materials processing and performance, including gas-solid, liquid-solid, and electrochemical processes. Emphasis is placed on understanding fundamental mechanisms that control rates of reactions and mass transport.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)

title: Structural Characterization
description: The theory and practice of x-ray diffraction and analytical electron microscopy; the principles of modern diffractometers and electron-beam instruments, both scanning and transmission, including electron optics, imaging modes, the interaction of electrons and x-rays with matter, diffraction theory, contrast mechanisms, and basic techniques for determining chemical composition, crystal structure, orientation, and defects in crystals.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
MSE280 - Intro to Materials Characterization Lab  (2)

title: Applications of Materials Characterization
description: In-depth applied study of materials characterization techniques. Study of theory and specific applications in materials science and engineering. Students are required to have an approved project. May be repeated for credit. Recommended completion of MSE 321.

title: Introduction to X-ray Diffraction
description: Practical introduction to x-ray diffraction and the optimal use of an x-ray diffractometer for crystalline materials in the form of bulk materials, powders, or films. Students are required to have a planned project that utilizes x-ray diffraction and the approval of their supervisor to enroll in this course. Recommended completion of MSE 246.

title: Interfaces and Dislocation Behavior
description: Structure of interfaces as groups of line defects including dislocations, disconnections, and disclinations; application of general concepts to special situations including epitaxial interfaces, twin boundaries and phase transformations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)

title: Nuclear Materials
description: Introduction to materials used in nuclear reactor systems. Provides students with a fundamental understanding of materials science in nuclear reactor applications. Begins with foundational nuclear reactor concepts and terminology. Follows with types of nuclear reactor systems, materials selection bases, basics of nuclear fission, and materials science topics such as crystal structure, crystal defects, diffusion, radiation damage processes, etc.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
or permission of instructor

title: Nanoscale Transport
description: Fundamental and applied treatment of photons, electrons, and phonons as energy carriers from the nanoscale (<100 nm) to the macroscale. Topics include energy transport in the forms of waves and particles, carrier scattering processes, transport in low-dimensional systems, and experimental methods of transport measurements. Particular attention will be given to 2-dimensional materials and devices.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Topics in Semiconductor Materials
description: Topics such as materials selection, characterization, design, processing, applications, and other field-specific matters. May be repeated for credit. Recommended completion of MSE 245.

title: Materials Modeling
description: Theory and application of computational techniques for modeling materials across length scales (nanometers to centimeters) and time scales (femtoseconds to minutes). Emphasis on stochastic techniques including molecular dynamics, Monte Carlo, and kinetic Monte Carlo simulations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE150 - Computational Tools for Materials Science (3)
MSE101 - Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN) (3)
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)

title: Computational Materials Science
description: Theory and application of computational modeling and simulation to fundamentally understand structure-property-performance relationships in materials. Different length- and time scale modeling techniques (e.g., first-principles quantum simulation, atomistic, mesoscale and continuum modeling), scientific programming, and visualization tools.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE150 - Computational Tools for Materials Science (3)
MSE201 - Fundamentals of Materials Science and Engineering (3)
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Topics in Ceramic Materials
description: Topics such as issues related to advanced ceramic systems as well as their processing, structure, properties and performance. May be repeated for credit. Recommended completion of MSE 246.

title: Physical Properties of Polymers
description: Physical properties of polymers with focus on their underlying physics and chemistry of chain structures, solution properties, glass transition, crystalline state, rubber elasticity, and viscoelasticity. Contemporary topics such as polymer composites and polymers for electronics, energy, and biomedical applications are also introduced.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MSE101 - Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN) (3)
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)

title: Biomaterials
description: Theory of biomaterials science. Medical and biological materials and their applications. Selection, properties, characterization, design and testing of materials used by or in living systems. May be taken for credit as BIOL, ME, or MSE, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)
MSE101 - Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN) (3)

title: Scientific Communication in Materials Science and Engineering
description: Intended for students performing research in materials science and engineering to communicate their latest research findings to specific targeted audiences. Methods are taught to organize and compose scientific scholarly research publication at the conference or journal publication level. Students will implement the methods by writing a scholarly research paper. Recommended student must be pursuing research in Materials Science and Engineering.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides students with an opportunity for supervised research in Materials Science and Engineering. The research will be supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit.

title: Senior Capstone Project I
description: Culminating major design experience that incorporates materials selection, engineering standards and realistic constraints that include most of the following: economic, environmental, manufacturability, ethical, health and safety, social and political.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MSE312 - Mechanical Behavior of Materials (3)
MSE318 - Phase Transformations and Kinetics (3)
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)
MSE315 - Materials Processing (3)
MSE380 - Material Properties Laboratory (3)
MSE311 - Electrical Properties of Materials (3)
Project based Engineering Elective

title: Senior Capstone Project II (FF)
description: Culminating major design experience that incorporates materials selection, engineering standards and realistic constraints that include most of the following: economic, environmental, manufacturability, ethical, health and safety, social and political.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE480 - Senior Capstone Project I (3)

title: Environmental Degradation of Materials
description: Theory of environmental degradation of metals, ceramics, polymers and biomaterials. Scientific principles of materials degradation with emphasis on material interactions within a living organism (in vivo).
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)

Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. title: Materials Science and Engineering Seminar
description: Review of contemporary issues with an emphasis on life-long learning in Materials Science and Engineering. May be repeated for a total of 3 credits.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MSE101 - Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN) (3)

title: Mathematics Transition for Success
description: Focuses on self-efficacy and academic skills related to success in university level mathematics. To build self-efficacy and academic skills, students progress through the course material based on individual needs in preparation for enrollment in the next level math course required by the student's major (i.e., MATH123, MATH133, MATH157). Upon completion of this course, students should see improvement of basic math skills, an increase in the confidence in mathematical abilities, and advancement in ability to relate math to real-life applications. MATH103 is not an FM course. Can not be taken after completion of any college level math course.

title: Intermediate Algebra
description: Radicals, negative and rational exponents, completing the square, quadratic formula. Linear and quadratic inequalities (including absolute value); simple systems of equations and inequalities. Multiplication of polynomials; basic factorization techniques. Manipulation of rational expressions, compound fractions, rationalization of denominator (or numerator). Introduction to the concept of function, graphs of functions and equations. Introduction to exponential and logarithmic expressions. Math 108 is NOT a FM course, and cannot be taken for credit after any MATH course numbered MATH 143 or higher.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH103 - Mathematics Transition for Success (3)
Or satisfactory placement score

title: Medieval Art
description: A survey of the art and architecture of the Medieval world (5th-15th centuries AD) including Byzantine Greece and Turkey, the Islamic Near East and Spain, and Europe from the time of the migrations through the Carolingian, Ottonian, Romanesque, and Gothic periods.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST101 - Survey of Western Art I (FA) (3)

title: Early Modern Material Culture, 1400-1700
description: Explores the role of material culture and the built environment in the lives of individuals and communities of the early modern era, from 1400-1700. It introduces critical theories and debates in art history and material culture studies, highlighting the power of artifacts in shaping lived experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: Italian Renaissance Art
description: A survey of the key artistic monuments in Renaissance Italy (1200-1600 AD), from the work of Cimabue to that of Caravaggio.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: Art of India
description: A survey of the art and architecture of India from the earliest times until the end of the Mughal period, emphasizing artistic expression as a reflection of the general culture and religion.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST103 - Survey of Far Eastern Art (3)

title: Early Modern Art and Visual Culture, 1400-1700
description: Places Europe’s artistic production from 1400-1700 in a wide range of cultural and geopolitical contexts and invites engagement with critical theories and debates in art history and visual culture.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: Eighteenth Century Art
description: A survey of the art of the Enlightenment from the time of Louis XIV through the Napoleonic Wars. Emphasis will be placed on the relationship between eighteenth century visual culture and developments in science, philosophy, and the changing political and social ideologies of the newly industrial nations of Europe and North America.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: History of Modern Architecture
description: History of modern architecture from mid-18th through late 20th centuries.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: History of Twentieth Century American Art
description: Beginning with a short survey of American art from the Ashcan School through the Thirties, with concentration on Abstract Expressionism, Pop, Op, and Minimal.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: History of Photography
description: Examines key photographers, movements, and critical debates in photography. Emphasis on developing student's proficiency at analyzing and interpreting photographs.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: Colloquium in Non-Western Art History
description: Intensive studies of a particular period, topic or problem in non-western art history. Lecture and discussion format will address critical issues in non-western art. Consult current class schedule for specific selections offered each term. May be repeated.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST101 - Survey of Western Art I (FA) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)
ARTHIST103 - Survey of Far Eastern Art (3)

title: Colloquium in Western Art History
description: Intensive study of a particular period, topic, or critical issue in western art history. Consult current class schedule for topics offered each term. Course may be repeated once for credit; topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ARTHIST101 - Survey of Western Art I (FA) (3)
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)

title: Art History Practicum
description: Directed practical experience in organizing and illustrating art history classes, leading exam review sessions, and evaluating student performance. Students will receive credit for working as an assistant in selected classes designated by art history faculty each semester. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours. Recommended to have 12 credits of art history.

title: Contemporary Concepts in Art
description: An exploration of contemporary art in the context of current theoretical concepts. The pluralistic nature of art during the postmodern era will be emphasized and recent developments in criticism will be introduced. Critical writings will be assigned.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ARTHIST302 - History of Twentieth Century European Art (3)
ARTHIST371 - History of Twentieth Century American Art (3)

title: Methods and Theory in Art History (FF)
description: A critical analysis of the historiographical, theoretical, and methodological approaches taken by art historians in their considerations and interpretation of visual culture, past and present. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ART298 - Seminar (3)
ARTHIST101 - Survey of Western Art I (FA) (3)
ARTHIST102 - Survey of Western Art II (3)
And 3 credits of upper-division art history

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Conversations with Working Artists
description: Working artists, arts administrators, and artists working beyond the creative sector participate in a series of discussions giving an opportunity to engage, ask questions, and reflect on key issues that will shape their careers.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student’s major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Arts Entrepreneurship Workshops
description: Possible topics include Pop-Ups, Public Art, The Pitch, Intellectual Property and the Arts, Employment Law and the Arts, Arts Festivals, Best Practices in Self-Producing, The Creative City, and Art as Social Practice. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Preparing for Creative Careers
description: Gains an understanding of one's creative process, learn about the possible career opportunities in the arts and creative economy, and develop the basic entrepreneurial skills needed for a successful career in the arts. Gains knowledge of varied cultural, social, and economic realities impacting contemporary art disciplines, creative sector collaborations, and the role of the arts in society.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
AE105 - Conversations with Working Artists (1)
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Entrepreneurship and Innovation in the Creative Sector
description: Introduces entrepreneurial skills and arts business activities and practices. Brainstorming; developing original concepts; and exploring business models, team-building, and innovation to create a plan of action for a career in the arts and creative economy.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Brand and Marketing in the Arts
description: Develop foundational structure for brand by identifying key factors in personal, company or organizational motivation and purpose. Analyze audiences specific to creative products and or services while using the brand foundation to inform creative messaging and design. The process will help emerging artists identify value in brand and execute effectively in the market.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Arts Management in Action
description: Provides insight into the management of individual arts businesses, arts organizations, events, creative enterprises, and other artistic endeavors. Topics include artistic content, leadership, fundraising and developing financial resources, community development, cultural facilities, technology, audience development, and marketing.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student’s major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Arts Entrepreneurship Workshops
description: Possible topics include Pop-Ups, Public Art, The Pitch, Intellectual Property and the Arts, Employment Law and the Arts, Arts Festivals, Best Practices in Self-Producing, The Creative City, and Art as Social Practice. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Living Learning Community: Arts and Sciences
description: First-year Adventure Idaho (AIC) and Communication and Exploration (CE Living Learning Communities) participants will participate in activities to explore ideas and values related to the specific community topics. These communities and classes provide a seamless educational experience for students from a variety of majors. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: Residential College: Arts and Sciences
description: The Housing and Residence Life Arts and Sciences Residential College community provides a seamless educational experience for students from a variety of majors within the college of Arts and Sciences committed to a well-rounded education. Students participate in activities to explore ideas and values represented in the arts and sciences. Coursework in this living-learning community will challenge the learner to reflect on the human condition as it is revealed through the arts, literature, sciences, and philosophy. May be repeated for credit.

title: Introduction to the Bachelor of Applied Science
description: Explore academic and career goal-setting strategies, innovative thinking frameworks, the connection of theory to practice, academic culture, academic integrity, the vital nature of an academic support community, and the qualities of a reflective and adaptive learner.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Bachelor of Applied Science Online (BAS), or Bachelor of Applied Science (BAS)

title: Creating a Culture of Safety
description: Study of safety as a vital element of human behavior in society, business, and industry. Examines the safety responsibilities of leaders, managers, and supervisors, focuses on developing skills in planning, implementation, awareness, monitoring, and risk management, and covers governmental influence, hazard awareness and control, operational considerations in the workplace, accidents, and planning.

title: Internship
description: A supervised on-site or virtual experiential learning opportunity. Deepen critical knowledge areas, enhance professional skills, and reflect on applied work experiences and academic and professional goals. Internships can be in any field or area of interest and require the approval of the Internship Coordinator. One credit equals 45 hours of on-site or virtual field work. May be repeated up to a maximum of 9 credits.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Bachelor of Applied Science Capstone (FF)
description: Complete and present an integrative capstone project and a culminating self-evaluation.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent Study

title: Elementary Basque I (FH)
description: Develops beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Introduces aspects of Basque culture.

title: Elementary Basque II (FH)
description: Continues to develop beginning abilities in all four language skills: speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Offers basic study of grammatical structures and vocabulary in a communicative context. Introduces aspects of Basque culture.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BASQUE101 - Elementary Basque I (FH) (3)

title: Intermediate Basque I (FH)
description: Continues building communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Basque culture. Course conducted in Basque.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BASQUE102 - Elementary Basque II (FH) (3)

title: Intermediate Basque II (FH)
description: Continues building communicative skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening. Further study of grammatical structures and vocabulary. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Basque culture. Course conducted in Basque.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BASQUE201 - Intermediate Basque I (FH) (3)

title: Intermediate Basque Conversation
description: Cultural topics will serve as the point of departure for conversation and discussion as well as further refinement of linguistic skills. May be repeated once for credit. Course conducted in Basque.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BASQUE102 - Elementary Basque II (FH) (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Advanced Basque
description: Refinement of communication skills in speaking, reading, writing and listening. Advanced topics in grammatical structures and vocabulary. Oral and written skills are practiced through the study of Basque culture. Course conducted in Basque.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BASQUE202 - Intermediate Basque II (FH) (3)
BASQUE203 - Intermediate Basque Conversation (1)
