title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Survey of Western Art Music
description: A preliminary course designed to acquaint the first-year music major with music history (from the Middle Ages to the present), literature, materials, library, and listening skills, and writing about music.
prerequisites: Must be a music major or pre-major status

title: Introduction to Jazz (FA)
description: Develops listening skills, historical understanding, and general appreciation of jazz as an art form within its specifically American cultural heritage and context. Attendance at two live jazz performances is required. No previous musical background is necessary.

title: Elements of Music Theory and Ear Training
description: This introduction to music theory and ear training course is designed for incoming music majors with minimal music theory/ear training background, as determined by the Music Literacy Predictive Exam given at the time of audition to the music program.

title: Introduction to Pop/Rock Music (FA)
description: Survey of the history of rock and popular music from its beginnings in the nineteenth century to the present day.

title: Electronic Music Production
description: Students will be introduced to electronic music production practices through exposure to the building blocks of music, beatmaking, and music composition/form using Digital Audio Workstation software. Students will leave with the ability to create electronic music using virtual instruments and MIDI sequencing. They will also gain exposure to the underlying theories of music and how it can be used to enhance media.

title: Materials of Music I
description: Music fundamentals review: notation, intervals, scales and modes, triads, key signatures, etc.; melody and cadences. Emphasis is on aural and visual recognition, analysis and compositional skills involving the above.
prerequisites: Must be a music major, music minor, or pre-music major.

title: Materials of Music II
description: 4-voice textures (linear and vertical); monophony; diatonic chords and harmonic relationships; cadences; inversions; dominant sevenths; aural and visual analysis; compositional skills.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MUS119 - Materials of Music I (3)
Music Major or Music Minor status

title: Ear Training I
description: Designed to correlate with Materials I. Emphasizes aural training in scales, intervals and rhythms. Includes drill in solfeggio and sight singing, leading to aural recognition of 3- and 4-part harmonic structures.
prerequisites: Must be a music major, music minor, or pre-music major.

title: Ear Training II
description: Designed to correlate with Materials II. Emphasizes aural training in scales, intervals and rhythms. Includes drill in solfeggio and sight singing, leading to aural recognition of 3- and 4-part harmonic structures.
prerequisites: Must be a music major or minor

title: Survey Opera and Music Theatre
description: An historical survey of the development and growth of opera and music theatre through chronological study of scores, recordings, sound filmstrips, and library resource materials from the beginning of the Baroque period to contemporary modern opera and music theatre compositions.

title: Music Technology
description: Develops essential basic skills and technology in the field of music. Students will become familiar with music software including educational, sequencing and notational software; will use word processing, database applications, spreadsheet programs, and graphics to produce sample classroom materials; and will learn sound reinforcement, recording technology, MIDI applications and programs, and CD-ROM applications.

title: Materials of Music III
description: Continuation of 4-part textures. Diatonic sevenths; secondary dominants and introduction to altered chords, augmented sixth and Neapolitan chords; modulations; compositional skills involving the above.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS120 - Materials of Music II (3)
MUS122 - Ear Training II (1)
MUS-APL109 - Class Piano II (1)

title: Materials of Music IV
description: Continuation of 4-part textures. Eleventh and thirteenth chords; twentieth-century melody and harmony; atonality and serial techniques. Compositional skills involving the above.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS219 - Materials of Music III (3)
MUS221 - Ear Training III (1)
MUS-APL109 - Class Piano II (1)

title: Ear Training III
description: Continuation of Ear Training II: more advanced sight-singing, melodic, harmonic and rhythmic dictation with more advanced rhythms in 2-4 voices.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS120 - Materials of Music II (3)
MUS122 - Ear Training II (1)
MUS-APL109 - Class Piano II (1)

title: Ear Training IV
description: Continuation of Ear Training III: more advanced sight-singing (including highly chromatic melodies), and more advanced melodic, harmonic and rhythmic dictation in 2-4 voices.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS219 - Materials of Music III (3)
MUS221 - Ear Training III (1)
MUS-APL109 - Class Piano II (1)

title: Foundations of Music Education
description: Introduction to the fundamentals of music education and teaching techniques for music at all levels. Includes observations of various school music programs. Lab period devoted to visitation in public schools.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Music Education BM
Completed the following: 
MUS120 - Materials of Music II (3)
MUS122 - Ear Training II (1)

title: Marching Band Techniques and Methods
description: Intended for music education majors. Survey of methods and materials necessary for the organization, administration, and instruction of public school marching bands. Required for all wind, brass and percussion music education majors.

title: Vocal Techniques and Methods
description: Primarily for Music Education majors, this course deals with teaching skills to help develop the vocal potentials of young students, describing basic physical components of the voice and their coordination, understanding the young and changing voice, and learning phonetic components of Latin, Italian, and German.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Music Education BM

title: String Instrument Technique and Methods
description: Primarily for Music Education majors, this course deals with methods and materials of string-class teaching in the public schools, while providing the student with a basic performing technique on two or more of the orchestral string instruments: violin, viola, cello, and string bass.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Music Education BM

title: Basic Conducting
description: Fundamental techniques of conducting: baton fundamentals, group rehearsal techniques, and simple score reading.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS120 - Materials of Music II (3)
MUS122 - Ear Training II (1)

title: Woodwind Instrument Techniques and Methods
description: Primarily for Music Education majors, this course deals with methods and materials of teaching woodwind instruments in the public schools, while providing the student with a basic performing technique on two or more woodwind instruments.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Music Education BM

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Teaching Experience I
description: A 50-hour (minimum) teaching experience in the public schools. Students will observe the teaching/learning process, identify best research-based practices in a classroom setting, and teach lessons guided by a mentor teacher in the classroom. Students will be observed by a University Supervisor 2-3 times throughout the semester. Self-paced online modules need to be completed by the end of the semester as part of the course. May be taken for ED-CIFS or MUS credit, but not both.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Music Production and Engineering
description: Audio recording and production techniques in a studio setting. Students will be able to operate in a recording studio environment, engineer multi-track recording sessions, overdubbing, utilize click tracks and visual mediums, edit recorded audio, work with automation and effects, mix recorded audio, and produce finalized audio in a variety of formats. Course concludes with a music production capstone project as part of the course.

title: Orchestration
description: Primarily for music majors. A study of scoring, notation, and arranging for brass, woodwind, percussion, and stringed instruments, and of their textures and uses in various combinations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS220 - Materials of Music IV (3)

title: Jazz Techniques
description: Intended for music education majors. Covers lead instrumental and vocal jazz ensembles in the public schools through the study of rehearsal planning and procedures, jazz articulations and styles, as well as the materials and methods for teaching improvisation.

title: Advanced Piano and Accompanying
description: Choral accompaniments and choral parts, as well as accompaniments, for art songs and folk songs using both printed notation and chord symbols.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS-APL108 - Class Piano I (1)
MUS-APL109 - Class Piano II (1)

title: Film Scoring
description: Students in Film Scoring will use digital audio workstation (DAW) and music notation software to produce scores for several projects throughout the semester. Scores will be realized using virtual instruments and live instruments when possible, and the final project will be in collaboration with adjacent disciplines. Students will also gain an understanding of film music history from early silent films to contemporary productions.

title: Music History and Literature I
description: The analysis of the development of Western art music from early Christian times through the early baroque era. Consideration of music from these periods as artistic entities, their relationships to their contemporary societies, and as foundations for subsequent expressions.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS219 - Materials of Music III (3)

title: Music History and Literature II
description: Encompasses the periods from the mid-baroque through the early 19th century. Attention to the changes in music forms and genres through listening, score-reading, analysis and discussion.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS219 - Materials of Music III (3)

title: Music History and Literature III
description: Encompasses the music of the mid-19th century to the present. Attention to the changes in musical styles and aesthetics through listening, score-reading, analysis and discussion.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS220 - Materials of Music IV (3)
MUS352 - Music History and Literature II (3)

title: Choral Conducting and Practicum
description: A course designed for music majors to deal with the problems and techniques of choral conducting and rehearsal planning for the choral ensemble. Includes score preparation, rehearsal techniques, and choice of appropriate literature for public school choral music programs. Significant time will be devoted to in class rehearsals with students as conductors. Students will work with ensembles as laboratories for conducting experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS261 - Basic Conducting (1)

title: Instrumental Conducting and Practicum
description: A course designed for music majors to deal with the problems of instrumental conducting and rehearsal planning for the instrumental ensemble. Includes baton technique and score reading, score preparation, rehearsal techniques, and choice of appropriate literature for public school instrumental music programs. Significant time will be devoted to in class rehearsals with students as conductors. Students will work with ensembles as laboratories for conducting experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS261 - Basic Conducting (1)

title: Choral Literature
description: Survey of choral works from all time periods. Though secular works are discussed, special emphasis is placed on tracing the development of the Mass, Motet and Requiem throughout history. Strategies for teaching and performing these works. Special projects cover programming for elementary, secondary and collegiate choirs.

title: Percussion Instrument Techniques and Methods
description: Primarily for Music Education majors, this course deals with methods and materials of teaching percussion instruments in the public schools, while providing the student with basic performing techniques on percussion.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Music Education BM

title: Brass Instrument Techniques and Methods
description: Primarily for Music Education majors, this course deals with methods and materials of teaching brass instruments in the public schools, while providing the student with a basic performing technique on two or more brass instruments.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Music Education BM

title: Guitar for Classroom Teachers
description: Designed for teachers or prospective teachers who wish to use the guitar in classroom situations. Emphasis is on accompaniment skills, elementary chord theory, and proper hand position. Musical material is drawn from popular and folk styles useful in elementary classes. May be repeated once for credit.

title: General Music Methods and Materials
description: A course designed for Music Education majors to explore and practice approaches, materials and pedagogy for the general music classroom. An emphasis is placed on elementary general music (K-6), with a secondary focus on general music for grades 7-12. The lab portion is devoted to observation and teaching experiences in public schools. This course is designated as an official Boise State Service-Learning Course.

title: Music Fundamentals and Methods for Elementary Classroom Teacher
description: Course prepares future elementary and special education teachers in awareness, skills , theories, and practices in K-8 general music education. Students will demonstrate skills and mastery with general music materials, facility in music reading, conducting, and playing of classroom instruments, and will design, teach, and assess music lessons.

title: Diction for Singers I
description: Designed for singers, devoted to the understanding of the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) system and the learning of the rules of pronunciation in English, French, German, and Italian.

title: Choral Methods and Materials
description: Designed for music education majors who will be teaching vocal groups in junior and/or senior high schools. A practical workshop in selection and conducting of choral materials, rehearsal techniques, use of small ensembles, planning and organization of vocal groups. Lab period devoted to teaching in public schools.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MUS230 - Foundations of Music Education (2)
And successful completion of Music Education Interview

title: Band and Orchestra Methods and Materials
description: The study of the organization and administration of bands and orchestras at the secondary school level, including equipment purchasing, budgets, public relations, planning, rehearsal techniques, scheduling, programming, and emergency repairs of instruments. Lab period devoted to teaching in public schools.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MUS230 - Foundations of Music Education (2)
MUS257 - String Instrument Technique and Methods (2)
MUS266 - Woodwind Instrument Techniques and Methods (2)
MUS368 - Percussion Instrument Techniques and Methods (2)
MUS369 - Brass Instrument Techniques and Methods (2)
And successful completion of Music Education Interview

title: Survey of Jazz
description: Explores interpretation of America"s original musical art form through listening and through discussion of socio-cultural contexts of jazz. Survey covers stylistic influences of nineteenth-century Africa and Western Europe through current living exponents of jazz.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MUSI100 - Introduction to Music (FA) (3)
MUS101 - Survey of Western Art Music (2)

title: Introduction to Ethnomusicology
description: Musical traditions beyond the scope of Western art music.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Earned a minimum grade of B in each of the following courses: 
MUS353 - Music History and Literature III (3)
And upper-division status in music

title: Advanced Form and Analysis
description: Analysis of harmonic and formal structures of the larger binary and ternary forms; the sonata, the symphony, the concerto, Baroque forms.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS220 - Materials of Music IV (3)

title: Counterpoint Since 1600
description: Study and writing in contrapuntal styles from Baroque period to present day. Invertible counterpoint, canon, fugue, invention, and analysis of procedures in representative works. Additional compositions and/or research for graduate credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS220 - Materials of Music IV (3)

title: Major Instrument Literature/Pedagogy
description: Survey of important literature and comparative study of pedagogical materials, principles and procedures for the major instrument. Reading, lecture, listening, and observation in teaching studios.
prerequisites: Must have upper-division standing in performance

title: Major Instrument Literature
description: A survey of important literature written for the major instrument.
prerequisites: Must have upper-division standing in performance

title: Major Instrument Pedagogy I
description: A survey and comparative study of pedagogical materials, principles and procedures. The course will consist of reading, lecture, listening, and observation in teaching studios.
prerequisites: Must have upper-division standing in performance

title: Major Instrument Pedagogy II
description: Practical application of pedagogical methods and procedures through supervised studio teaching. Further reading, lecture, listening, and discussion involving pedagogical techniques.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS463 - Major Instrument Pedagogy I (2)

title: Diction for Singers II
description: An implementation of the diction rules learned in MUS375 through the examination and performing of repertoire.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MUS375 - Diction for Singers I (2)

title: Professional Year - Elementary Teaching Experience III Dual Option
description: Supervised student teaching in an elementary school. Student will be placed with a master teacher in music for one half-semester (full-time) in music under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required. Recommended concurrent enrollment in MUS 482 or MUS 483.

title: Professional Year - Junior High Teaching Experience IV Dual Option
description: Supervised student teaching in a junior high school. Student will be placed with a master teacher in music for one half-semester (full-time) in music under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required. Recommended concurrent enrollment in MUS 481 or MUS 483.

title: Professional Year - Senior High Teaching Experience IV Dual Option
description: Supervised student teaching in a senior high school. Student will be placed with a master teacher in music for one half-semester (full-time) in music under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required. Recommended concurrent enrollment in MUS 481 or MUS 482.

title: Assessments, Standards, and the Learning Environment for K-12 Music Teachers (FF)
description: Part of the professional year culminating experience for Music Education majors. Allows synthesis of knowledge and skills gained throughout academic coursework in the degree program with application and professional engagement in the field during student teaching.  Familiarity with and use of a variety of music-specific and additional content standards to explore lesson/unit design and assessment strategies for learning and engagement in K-12 music classrooms. Topics include backward design, formative and summative assessments, standards-based grading and proficiency scales, the physical and psychological learning environment, teacher professional dispositions, and classroom management.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Music Seminar
description: A seminar project under faculty direction.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Introduction to Music (FA)
description: Open to all students, with no background assumed, this course will familiarize the listener with the variety of styles and genres of Western concert music through an historical approach. Attending at least two approved live concerts/recitals is required.

title: Introduction to Neurogenetics
description: Explores the intricate relationship between genetics and neuroscience, focusing on how genetic factors influence the development, structure, and function of the nervous system. Delves into the latest research and methodologies in neurogenetics, gaining a comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying various neurological disorders.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL117 - Introduction to Neuroscience Through Disease Models (3)
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
KINES303 - Neuroanatomy and Physiology (3)

title: Cognitive Neuroscience
description: In-depth exploration of the neural pathways and brain regions responsible for shaping thoughts, emotions, and behaviors. explore cutting-edge research on brain imaging techniques. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of how the brain orchestrates cognitive processes.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
BIOL117 - Introduction to Neuroscience Through Disease Models (3)
KINES303 - Neuroanatomy and Physiology (3)

title: Laboratory Techniques in Neuroscience I
description: Cellular and molecular neuroscience lab with a hands-on and in-depth exploration of key concepts and techniques in molecular neuroscience. A combination of theoretical sessions and practical experiments in the intricate molecular mechanisms underlying neural function and dysfunction.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
KINES303 - Neuroanatomy and Physiology (3)

title: Laboratory Techniques in Neuroscience II
description: Applied neuroscience lab course designed to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and real-world applications in neuroscience. Provides practical skills and experiences that are directly applicable to addressing challenges in various fields, including healthcare, technology, and human performance.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CS111 - Introduction to Programming (3)
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
MSE150 - Computational Tools for Materials Science (3)

title: Capstone in Neuroscience (FF)
description: Create a final project that represents the knowledge, skills, and abilities gained throughout the Neuroscience degree program. The nature of the experience (project, paper, case study, etc.) is flexible, based on interest, as it is intended to strengthen experiences necessary for successfully moving along chosen career pathways.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
NEURO395 - Laboratory Techniques in Neuroscience I (4)
NEURO396 - Laboratory Techniques in Neuroscience II (4)
PSYC335 - Biological Bases of Behavior (3)

title: Endocrinology and Human Function
description: Exploration of the human endocrine system, including all endocrine glands neuroendocrine tissue. A heavy emphasis on the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and the sympatho-adrenal axis, and their control of physiologic systems, including reproductive, immune, cardiovascular, digestive. Emphasis on the implications of normal and impaired endocrine function on physical and psychological functional ability and well-being.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Introduction to Nonprofit Management
description: The course addresses the issues of managing nonprofit organizations. Issues concern personnel assessment, managing others, working with elected and appointed public officials, working with board members, volunteer management, working with media, solving problems, communicating supportively, understanding motivational processes, managing conflicts, grant writing, and building teams.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Volunteer Management and the Nonprofit
description: The practical, legal, and technical aspects of directing a volunteer program are addressed. Topics include the employment cycle of volunteers (including recruitment, selection, training, performance evaluation), trends in volunteerism, types of volunteers, building the volunteer/staff relationship, volunteer boards, and corporate volunteers.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
NONPROF240 - Introduction to Nonprofit Management (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Funding for Nonprofits
description: Nonprofits require a unique structure of revenue that includes grant writing, major donor development, social enterprise, event planning, and more. This course will include the research, cultivation and stewardship of major donors as well as exploration of individual nonprofit budget structure and their unique revenue streams. With an emphasis on grant writing, students will be able to create a development plan for a nonprofit, identify funding sources, determine program needs, create a project-specific and general operating budget, define outcomes and benchmarks, and evaluate success.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)
BUSCOM201 - Business Communication (3)
CMGT201 - Construction Communications (3)
BUSMGT306 - Professional Communication for Managers (3)
Completed the following: 
NONPROF240 - Introduction to Nonprofit Management (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in 493, a student must work in a nonprofit organization. Course may be repeated for credit. Recommended completion of NONPROF 340.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Introduction to Philosophy (FH)
description: An introduction to some major issues in metaphysics and epistemology, such as free will, the existence of God, the rationality of religious belief, the mind/body problem, personal identity, skepticism about external world, and the problem of induction.

description: An introduction to the thought of some major figures from the history of western philosophy, such as Plato, Aristotle, Aquinas, Anselm, Locke, Hume, Descartes, Berkeley, Kant, and Marx.

title: Introduction to Ethics (FH)
description: An introduction to philosophical thinking about selected moral problems, such as famine, abortion, euthanasia, the moral status of animals, and whether killing is worse than letting-die.

title: Philosophy in a Datafied World (FH)
description: Provides tools for, and practice in, describing and evaluating the impact of data driven services on the lives of students and their communities.

title: Thinking Well: Introduction to Logic (FH)
description: How do we tell when one claim follows from, or is supported by, another? This course aims to answer that question, and to teach you how to prove that a conclusion follows logically—or that it doesn’t. This skill is important both inside and outside of the classroom—it is central to reading, thinking, and writing well about any question. The focus will be on evaluating reasoning in a formal language—one designed to make the logical features of our reasoning clear.

title: Philosophical Perspectives on Science and Mathematics (FH)
description: Introduction to the historical, social, and philosophical implications of math and science. Laboratory focuses on replication of significant discoveries. Cross-listed with STEM-ED 220, may be taken once for credit.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Contemporary Philosophy
description: This class tackles some central questions in contemporary philosophy, such as what makes a life go well, whether we know anything about the external world, and whether we can explain why the universe is fit for biological life. Students learn how to engage with recent philosophical texts, and how to argue and write like a philosopher. The course is designed to prepare students for the following upper-division courses: PHIL 306, PHIL 308, PHIL 310, PHIL 311, PHIL 312, PHIL 313, PHIL 333, and PHIL 335.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Philosophy of Law
description: Examines fundamental issues in the philosophy of law. Includes the nature and content of law, its relation to morality, theories of legal interpretation, and the obligation to obey the law. Philosophical issues and problems associated with punishment and responsibility, liberty, and legal ethics.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Symbolic Logic
description: A study of techniques of validation in propositional and predicate logic, with emphasis on the construction of formal proofs. Some attention will be given to metalogical notions such as consistency and completeness.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH189 - Discrete Mathematics (4)
PHIL209 - Thinking Well: Introduction to Logic (FH) (3)

title: Ancient Greek Philosophy
description: An introduction to the origins of Western philosophy in the ancient world, with emphasis on Plato and Aristotle.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Philosophy of Science
description: A study of philosophical issues raised by reflection on the nature of science and the results of scientific inquiry.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Medieval Philosophy
description: A survey of major developments in Western philosophy from St. Augustine through William of Ockham, with emphasis on selected figures.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Philosophy of Language
description: An investigation of basic philosophical problems concerning language and communication. Topics may include: truth, meaning, reference, proper names, descriptions, the distinction between semantics and pragmatics, and context-sensitivity.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Modern Philosophy
description: Covers important developments in Western philosophy from the 17th and 18th centuries, developments that still have ramifications today. Philosophers covered may include Descartes, Leibniz, Locke, Hume and Kant. Issues covered may include the foundations of knowledge, rationalism vs. empiricism, the nature of the self, the relation of objects to their properties, whether God exists, and the just organization of society.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Philosophy of Mind
description: An examination of various solutions to the mind/body problem, the problem of other minds, as well as related mental concepts. Problems of action theory may be explored.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Normative Ethics
description: Examines views and issues in normative ethics, such as utilitarianism, egalitarianism, libertarianism, Kantianism, virtue theory, rights, fairness, desert, and causing versus allowing harm. May include the application of these views and issues to the political domain.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Meta-Ethics
description: Examines views and issues in meta-ethics, such as naturalism, non-naturalism, non-cognitivism, error theory, moral epistemology, moral disagreement, moral motivation, and reasons for action.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Analytic Philosophy
description: An investigation of major themes in Anglo-American philosophy during the twentieth century.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Phenomenology and Existentialism
description: An exploration of the nature of conscious experience and the place of dread and choice in human existence, with emphasis on selected figures in the tradition of European philosophy established by Kierkegaard and Husserl.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Philosophy and Critical Theory
description: Presents the rich philosophical context of critical theory, which comprises a variety of critiques that seek to understand and alter our social practices and lived realities, and which presupposes and refashions ideas developed primarily in late modern and contemporary western philosophy. Topics typically covered include power and knowledge, democracy and enlightenment, faith and inter-subjectivity, historical materialism and humanism, racial and sexual difference, globalism and post-colonialism.

title: Eastern Philosophy
description: Philosophical teachings of great Eastern thinkers through a study of classical texts selected from traditions of Hinduism, Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Confucianism in Chinese Culture
description: Introduction to the philosophy of Confucianism as the foundation of Chinese culture. Students will explore how Confucianism provided a framework for the development of traditional Chinese moral standards, family values, education, political philosophy, civil responsibility, and attitudes toward the natural world. Cross-listed from World 322, may be taken once for credit.

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Examination of environmental problems from an ethical point of view. Topics include population control, pollution, animal liberation, the moral and legal rights of nature, and social ecology.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Basic philosophical issues connected with religious belief such as the nature and existence of God, the problem of evil, miracles, and the significance of religious experience.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Metaphysics
description: An investigation of basic problems about the nature of reality. Possible topics include personal identity, the nature of mind, freedom and determinism, and the problems of universals.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Epistemology
description: An investigation of basic problems concerning knowledge and the justification of belief. Possible topics include attempts to define knowledge and related concepts, the problem of skepticism, and the problem of other minds.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Aesthetics
description: The philosophy of the fine arts covering such topics as the existence and nature of works of art, aesthetic experience, artistic creativity, the species of aesthetic value, and the nature of beauty.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHIL101 - Introduction to Philosophy (FH) (3)
PHIL102 - Introduction to Philosophy: Great Thinkers (FH) (3)
PHIL103 - Introduction to Ethics (FH) (3)

title: Advanced Philosophical Topics
description: Detailed examination of a small set of issues within a selected area of philosophy. May be repeated for credit. Recommended completion of PHIL 209, PHIL 301.

title: Classical Political Thought
description: Development of political philosophy from Socrates to Machiavelli. Cross-listed with POLS 441m may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
POLS315 - What is Political Philosophy? (3)
Or one upper-division philosophy course

title: Modern Political Thought
description: Development of political philosophy from Socrates to Machiavelli. Cross-listed with POLS 442, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
POLS315 - What is Political Philosophy? (3)
Or one upper-division philosophy course

title: Political Thought Since the French Revolution
description: Major trends in political thought from the post-French Revolutionary era, which may include German idealism, historicism, existentialism, nihilism, and Marxism. Cross-listed with POLS 443, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Senior Research
description: Directed research culminating in a writing sample, suitable for graduate school applications. Recommended senior standing in a philosophy major.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Senior Comprehensive Assessment (FF)
description: Capstone experience resulting in a portfolio of student work. Recommended senior standing in a philosophy major.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Introduction to Physics (FN)
description: A broad survey of basic physics concepts and principles including motion, energy, electricity, magnetism, light, relativity, atoms, fission and fusion. Some examples will be related to social applications. A one-semester core course that uses some basic algebra.

title: Life in the Universe (FN)
description: Emphasis is on our solar system, the origin of chemical abundances, and astronomical requirements for the development of life; extra-solar planetary systems, and the search for life in the universe. Requires evening labs.

title: Stars and Cosmology (FN)
description: An exploration of star formation and evolution, black holes, galaxies, and cosmology. Explores how the ideas of Albert Einstein, Stephen Hawking, and others form our understanding of the universe. Requires evening labs and/or planetarium visits.

title: Radiation Physics
description: Fundamental concepts involving electricity, magnetism, formation of electromagnetic radiation and radioactivity. Includes basic circuitry of x-ray machine and introduction to radiation dose.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
or satisfactory placement score

title: General Physics I (FN)
description: Kinematics, forces and dynamics, conservation laws, waves, thermodynamics. Uses algebra and trigonometry, and includes one required three-hour lab per week. Recommended background: high school physics or PHYS 101.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH144 - Precalculus II: Trigonometry (2)
or satisfactory placement score

title: General Physics II (FN)
description: Electricity, magnetism, optics, and modern physics. Uses algebra and trigonometry, and includes one required three-hour lab per week.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS111 - General Physics I (FN) (4)

title: Laboratory Only
description: Introductory physics lab for transfer students needing a laboratory experience to gain FN lab credit for a lecture-only PHYS course taken elsewhere. Includes a weekly 2 or 3 hour lab.

title: Physics Symposium
description: Topics in current areas of student interest in physics and related disciplines, introduction to the physics department, degrees, and faculty, to physics degree requirements for graduation, and to jobs and graduate school. Intended for physics majors and prospective majors.

title: Physics I with Calculus (FN)
description: Kinematics, dynamics of particles, statics, energy, work, momentum, rotational motion, wave motion, and superposition. Recommended background: high school physics or PHYS 101.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH144 - Precalculus II: Trigonometry (2)
or satisfactory placement score

title: Physics I with Calculus Lab (FN)
description: Lab to be taken with PHYS 211. Basic experiments in kinematics, mechanics, and wave motion.

title: Physics II with Calculus
description: Heat and thermodynamics, electrostatics, fields, electric potential, electric current, simple circuits, magnetism, electromagnetic induction, electromagnetic waves, polarization, Maxwell's equations, Lorentz force law, diffraction, and geometrical optics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
PHYS211 - Physics I with Calculus (FN) (4)
PHYS211L - Physics I with Calculus Lab (FN) (1)

title: Physics II with Calculus Lab
description: Lab to be taken concurrently with PHYS 212. Basic experiments in heat, electricity, magnetism, and optics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS211L - Physics I with Calculus Lab (FN) (1)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Research in Physics
description: Individual research project carried out by the student in collaboration with a supervising member of the physics faculty. Intended for freshmen or sophomores. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Analog and Digital Electronics
description: Introduction to electronic test instrumentation, discrete semiconductor devices, and their use in integrated circuits. Effective presentation and interpretation of technical data is stressed through written lab reports and oral communication projects.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)
PHYS212L - Physics II with Calculus Lab (1)

title: Introduction to Astrophysics and Astronomical Observing
description: A broad survey of basic astrophysics and astronomical observation. Topics include motion of the night sky, Kepler’s laws of planetary motion, planets in our solar system and beyond, light propagation and interaction with matter, astronomical optics, ground/space-based observatories, multi-wavelength and multi-messenger astronomy, and data analysis. The course will culminate in the collection and analysis of data using Boise State’s astronomical observatories, weather permitting. Requires evening viewing sessions.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)
PHYS105 - Stars and Cosmology (FN) (4)

title: Introduction to Biophysics
description: Application of physical principles and techniques to the study of key biomolecules and biological systems. Stresses examples relevant to cellular and molecular biology and to biomedical research.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHYS112 - General Physics II (FN) (4)
Completed at least 2 of the following: 
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)
PHYS212L - Physics II with Calculus Lab (1)

title: Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications
description: An introduction to modern physics, focused on quantum physics, with examples from atomic, molecular, and statistical physics, engineering, solid-state physics, and nanotechnology.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH175 - Calculus II (4)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)

title: Introductory Quantum Physics Lab
description: Lab to be taken concurrently with PHYS 309. Hands-on experiments and computer simulations applying the principles of modern physics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS212L - Physics II with Calculus Lab (1)

title: Introductory Relativistic Physics with Applications
description: A modern physics course to follow PHYS 309, focused on introductory relativity, nuclear physics, elementary particles, and cosmology.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Scientific Computing
description: Methods and practices of computing and computer modeling, with an emphasis on problems in science and engineering. Topics include model building, simulation of complex systems, numerical solutions of systems of differential equations, and scientific visualization.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CS121 - Computer Science I (4)
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
MSE150 - Computational Tools for Materials Science (3)

title: Optics
description: Geometrical and physical optics, including lenses, fiber optics, Fourier optics, polarization, interference, diffraction, lasers, and special topics.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ECE300 - Electromagnetic and Wave Theory (3)
PHYS381 - Electromagnetic Theory (4)

title: Optics Lab
description: Laboratory to be taken concurrently with PHYS 330. Experiments in optics, including optical systems, thick lenses, interference, diffraction, Fourier optics, image processing, and special topics.

title: Classical Mechanics
description: An advanced treatment of classical mechanics using the methods of Lagrange and Hamilton, with the aid of vector calculus and differential equations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
PHYS211 - Physics I with Calculus (FN) (4)

title: Electromagnetic Theory
description: Electrostatic and magnetostatic fields, including potentials, Gauss's law, solutions of Laplace's equation, dielectrics, vector potentials, magnetization, and an introduction to Maxwell's equations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)

title: Electrodynamics
description: Application of Maxwell's equations to electrodynamics, including the stress tensor, wave equation, guided waves, radiation, and special relativity.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS381 - Electromagnetic Theory (4)

title: Research in Physics
description: Individual research project carried out by the student in collaboration with a supervising member of the physics faculty. Intended for juniors or seniors. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Molecular and Cellular Biophysics
description: Advanced introduction to biophysical concepts and methods, focused on developing an in-depth understanding of the functionality of biological systems at the molecular and cellular level. Includes biophysical properties of water and solutions, characterization of biomolecular interactions, biological relevance of the physical properties of biomolecules, role of physical interactions in driving the self-assembly and conformational changes of biomolecules, membrane transport, molecular and cellular motility, and biophysical aspects of cell function.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)
PHYS307 - Introduction to Biophysics (3)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Astrophysics
description: Techniques and topics of modern astrophysics. Material is selected from the interaction of light with matter, solar system formation, main sequence star structure and evolution, degenerate stars and black holes, interstellar medium, galaxy formation and evolution.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)
PHYS105 - Stars and Cosmology (FN) (4)

title: Cosmology
description: An overview of the large-scale structure and evolution of normal and dark matter. The key elements of observational cosmology including cosmic expansion, the microwave background radiation, and primordial nucleosynthesis. The early universe, inflation and the formation of structure.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309L - Introductory Quantum Physics Lab (1)
PHYS311 - Introductory Relativistic Physics with Applications (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PHYS105 - Stars and Cosmology (FN) (4)
PHYS205 - Stellar Astronomy (4)

title: Intermediate Quantum Mechanics
description: Fundamentals, including properties and solutions of the Schroedinger equation, operators, angular momentum, electron spin, identical particles, perturbations, and variational principle. Applications, such as tunneling, orbitals, magnetic resonance, and nanoscale effects.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
MATH301 - Introduction to Linear Algebra (3)
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Solid State Physics
description: Quantum physics applied to understanding the properties of materials, including semiconductors, metals, superconductors, and magnetic systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Advanced Topics
description: Selected advanced topics from physics and applied physics, such as astrophysics, biophysics, device physics, magnetic materials, nanoscale physics, or medical physics. May be repeated for credit. Recommended upper-division standing.

title: Physical Methods of Materials Characterization
description: Physical principles and practical methods used in determining the structural, electronic, optical, and magnetic properties of materials. Optical, electron, and scanning microscopies, diffraction, surface analysis, optical spectroscopy, electrical transport, and magnetometry.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Thermal Physics
description: Foundations and applications of thermodynamics and statistical mechanics, including temperature, entropy, heat capacity, chemical potential, and free energies. Applications to gasses, paramagnets, chemical systems, electrons, photons, phonons, and superfluids.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS309 - Introductory Quantum Physics with Applications (3)

title: Soft Matter
description: Introduction to the physical principles underlying the properties and behaviors of soft matter, including polymers, gels, colloids, and liquid crystals. Examples of soft matter include glues, paints, soaps, rubber, foams, gelatin, milk, and most materials of biological origin. Recommended preparation: PHYS 309.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)
PHYS212 - Physics II with Calculus (4)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CHEM322 - Physical Chemistry II Lecture (3)
MSE308 - Thermodynamics of Materials (3)
PHYS432 - Thermal Physics (4)

title: Advanced Physics Lab
description: An advanced laboratory course designed to acquaint students with the concepts of modern physics, laboratory techniques, and measurements.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS309L - Introductory Quantum Physics Lab (1)

title: Senior Project
description: Elective. A sophisticated library or laboratory project in some area of physics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PHYS481 - Advanced Physics Lab (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Research in Physics
description: Individual research project carried out by the student in collaboration with a supervising member of the physics faculty. Intended for seniors. Course may be repeated for credit.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Physics Senior Seminars (FF)
description: A culminating experience for physics majors. Provides practice in the search and critical assessment of research articles and current trends in physics. Communication of results for a variety of audiences is emphasized. Recommended completion of  a foundations of communications (FC) course, be a physics major and senior status.

title: American National Government (FS)
description: Institutions and processes of the American political system, emphasizing social, ideological, and constitutional background.

title: Introduction to American Politics and Political Philosophy
description: Students will confront the key texts in the American Politics and Political Philosophy subfields of the political science discipline and react to the key debates addressed in those texts and propose solutions to the issues that are traditional to political analysis.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Current Events in American Politics (FS)
description: Examines a current issue in American politics through the lens of political science research. Compares analysis of current topics from the media to political science approaches. Consult current class schedule for specific selections offered each term. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Introduction to Global Politics
description: Students will confront the key texts in the Comparative Politics and International Relations subfields of the political science discipline and react to the key debates addressed in those texts and propose solutions to the issues that are traditional to political analysis.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Current Events in Global Politics
description: Examines a current issue in global politics through the lens of political science research. Compares analysis of current topics from the media to political science approaches. Consult current class schedule for specific selections offered each term. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Political Science in Popular Media
description: Examines the field of political science and politics through the lens of popular media. Consult current class schedule for specific selections offered each term.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Courts, Judges, and Judicial Politics (FS)
description: Explores, through discussion and active learning, the U.S. judicial system including how judges are selected, the structure of courts, the civil and criminal process, and the policy making role of courts, particularly the U.S. Supreme Court. Also explores the historical context and role of politics in all aspects of the judiciary, including how institutions and processes can impact outcomes for different actors and communities.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Introduction to Political Research
description: Approaches of political science inquiry, both quantitative and qualitative.

title: Polarization in American Politics
description: Examination of institutions, political culture, and political processes throughout the American regime.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
POLS200 - Introduction to American Politics and Political Philosophy (3)

title: Advanced Political Science Methods
description: Techniques of quantitative political science inquiry using data analysis and introductory statistics.

title: Comparative Global Politics
description: Cross-national analysis of the structure and functioning of various types of political systems, with special emphasis on the problem of political change. May be taken for GLOBAL or POLS credit, but not both.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Global Studies BA
Completed the following: 
GLOBAL201 - Around the Globe: World Regions in a time of Connection, Crisis, and Change (3)
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
POLS205 - Introduction to Global Politics (3)

title: International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods
description: Nature of relations among nations with particular reference to contemporary international issues. Analysis of the causes of war and efforts to promote peace. Study of national sovereignty and its relation to international cooperation.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

description: Variable credit course based on experiential learning from work performed in the workplace or other professional settings. Must show records and develop a portfolio to show evidence of learning.

title: Introduction to Psychology (FS)
description: Investigation of mental processes and behavior through the lens of the scientific method. This survey course draws from topics within the American Psychological Association's five pillars of psychology: Pillar 1 Biological (Neuroscience, Sensation, Consciousness, Motivation); Pillar 2 Cognitive (Cognition, Memory, Perception); Pillar 3 Development (Learning, Life Span Development, Language); Pillar 4 Social and Personality (Social, Personality, Intelligence, Emotion, Multicultural, Gender); and Pillar 5 Mental and Physical Health (Abnormal, Health, Therapies).

title: Professional Development: Freshman
description: First in a series of four created to provide direct touch points with students to facilitate academic preparedness, career exploration, establish post graduate trajectories, and develop resources for transitioning post college. With the support of faculty/staff, focus on navigating the social, academic, and logistical challenges associated with college. Exploration of short and long term academic related goals. Specifically, academic and work force related post graduate options and outline experiences to prepare for those options. (Pass/Fail.)

title: Introduction to the Psychology Major

prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

title: The Art of Happiness
description: Focus on the individual behaviors and mental processes that contribute to happiness through a scientific lens. Subjects include, and are not limited to: empathy, humor, kindness, forgiveness, gratitude, compassion, and mindfulness. An emphasis is placed on the application of this science through the development of a personal happiness practice.

title: Cross-cultural Psychology
description: Review of cultural similarity and differences in such areas as child development, gender roles, social behavior, language and communication, and mental illness. Focus on psychological theory and research relevant to explaining how cultural factors influence human behavior and thought.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

title: Professional Development: Sophomore
description: Second in a series of four created to provide direct touch points with students to facilitate academic preparedness, career exploration, establish post graduate trajectories, and develop resources for transitioning post college. With the support of faculty/staff, focus on developing academic plans that align with their anticipated post graduate goals. Identify specific post graduate trajectories, develop academic plans that support those trajectories, and begin to develop a skills narrative that represents their development thus far in college. (Pass/Fail.)

title: Human Sexuality
description: An overview of human sexuality emphasizing both physiological and psychological aspects of sexuality. Topics include sexual anatomy and physiology, sexual response cycle, childbirth, contraception, sexual dysfunction, sex role development, and sexual deviation. Cross-cultural values will be examined and a values clarification unit will be included.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

title: Human Relationships
description: The study of individual sexuality as well as the dynamics of close relationships from a variety of psychological perspectives. Topics covered include sexuality development, sexual behavior, initial attraction, dating patterns, long-term relationships, familial relationships, intimacy and communication, domestic violence, and relationship development.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

description: The psychological processes underlying human development of eating behaviors and the adoption of both healthy and unhealthy cognitions and behaviors concerning food, eating, and body image. Issues addressed include: food choice, food preferences, eating motivation, cultural influences, weight regulation, body image, dieting, obesity, eating disorders, and treatment.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major.
