prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: A descriptive approach to the study of the etiology, development, and dynamics of behavioral disorders, together with a review of current preventive and remedial practices.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Child Development
description: Designed for psychology majors, the course emphasizes theories of human development including psychodynamic, behavioral, social-learning, and cognitive. Contemporary views of genetic and environmental contributions will be examined. Research designs appropriate to developmental issues will be explored. The emphasis will be on development from the prenatal period to adolescence.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Adolescent and Adult Development
description: Designed for psychology majors, the course emphasizes theories of human development including psychodynamic, behavioral, social-learning, and cognitive. Includes contemporary views of genetics, the environmental, and research designs appropriate to developmental issues.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Research Methods
description: The application of scientific methodology to the study of behavior. Design of experiments, methods of analysis, and interpretation of data; reporting of behavioral research.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
PSYC120 - Introduction to the Psychology Major (2)
PSYC295 - Statistical Methods (3)
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Principles of Data Science
description: An introduction to the core concepts of data science including: predictive modeling using machine learning and data mining; data gathering, extraction and cleaning; and exploratory data analysis. Emphasizes practical skills for liberal arts students to examine questions of human behavior using large and complex data sets. May be taken for credit in ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
Must take a statistics course
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: The Psychology of Health
description: This course focuses on how biological, psychological, cultural, and social factors affect health and illness. It evaluates the best ways to promote healthy living and prevent disease and how people react psychologically when they are diagnosed with an illness or asked to make lifestyle changes. It also covers the influence of stress, coping, personality, culture, and family on health.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PSYC295 - Statistical Methods (3)
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Professional Development: Junior
description: Third in a series of four created to provide direct touch points with students to facilitate academic preparedness, career exploration, establish post graduate trajectories, and develop resources for transitioning post college. With the support of faculty/staff, focus on developing the relationships and experiences that will empower them to make transitions post college. Specifically, development of both academic and workforce post graduate plans, identifying specific programs for graduate work and workforce related options. (Pass/Fail.)
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC222 - Professional Development: Sophomore (1)

title: Biological Bases of Behavior
description: Classical and current issues in physiological psychology, including central and peripheral nervous systems, processing of information and organization of behavior, perception, motivation, emotion, and learning.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL107 - Introduction to Human Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Perception
description: A survey of the basic concepts in the psychology of perception. Processes are stressed, although coverage of receptor structure and neural pathways is included.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Cognitive Psychology
description: Foundation for understanding the issues, principles, and models involved in the study of mental processes. Topics range from classic cognitive psychology to more current neuroscience. Applications are emphasized.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Personality
description: A study of the major contemporary theories and concepts of personality, with special emphasis on psychoanalytic, humanistic, and behavioral approaches.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PSYC295 - Statistical Methods (3)
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Introduction to Counseling Skills
description: Explores relevant dimensions of the helping relationship, especially the role of the helper. Emphasis will be on developing effective communication and fundamental counseling skills.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC301 - Abnormal Psychology (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Psychological Neuroscience
description: Consideration of neuroscience with particular emphasis on current approaches and applications relating to human brain research. The goal is to illuminate the complex interaction shared by physiological/biological and mental functions and processes. The objective is to illustrate the dynamic role of neuroscience in transforming conventional ideology of brain processes and function.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL107 - Introduction to Human Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: General Psychology Teaching Assistant
description: Serve as teaching assistant for PSYC 101. Experience may include attending lectures, holding office hours, tutoring students, grading papers, supervising review sessions, guest lecturing, and/or other duties relevant to the course. Recommended PSYC 101, upper-division standing, and a cumulative GPA above 3.00.

title: Psychology Teaching Assistant
description: Serve as teaching assistant for one psychology course. Experience may include attending lectures, holding office hours, tutoring students, grading papers, supervising review sessions, guest lecturing, and/or other duties relevant to teaching the course. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credits. Recommended upper-division standing and a cumulative GPA above 3.00.

title: Advanced Statistical Methods
description: Advanced topics in univariate statistics (for example, repeated measures designs) and multivariate techniques such as discriminant analysis, factor analysis, and principal component analysis.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Skills in Academic Advising
description: A detailed account of the information and skills necessary to serve as an academic advisor as an undergraduate student. Topics include Boise State University’s undergraduate degree requirements, Psychology BS degree requirements, an overview of academic and personal support resources available to students, FERPA training, and the Department of Psychological Science’s expectations for undergraduate students serving as academic advisors. Opportunities to shadow current students serving as academic advisors for the Department of Psychological Science will also be provided.

title: Practicing Skills in Advising
description: Hands-on advising training course. Students will shadow current advisors and begin serving as psychology advisors in training.

title: Psychology Peer Advising
description: Utilize skills learned in PSYC410 and PSYC411 by serving as Psychology Peer Advisors. Assist psychology majors and minors with class scheduling, planning their degree, exploring minors and certificates, and answering academic-related questions.

title: Advanced Psychology Peer Advising
description: Serve as Psychology Peer Advisors. Duties include but are not limited to helping psychology majors and minors with class scheduling, planning their degree, exploring minors and certificates, answering academic-related questions, and helping mentor the incoming cohort of students enrolled in PSYC410 and PSYC411.

title: Children and Families: Multicultural Perspectives
description: Research and theories on child development in the context of family interactions and influences. Examine cultural similarities and differences in parental values and beliefs about child rearing, socialization practices, gender roles in families, and the adolescent struggle for independence from family.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Social Network Analysis
description: Introduces and applies concepts and empirical methods of network analysis in a field-based project. Social networks influence learning, economic behavior, and adoption of new products and organizational innovations. May be taken for credit as ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
And take a statistics course

title: Psychological Measurement
description: Theory and nature of psychological measurement together with a survey of types of psychological tests currently used.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Social Psychology
description: The primary focus is the individual; the unit of analysis, the interpersonal behavior event. A study of individual motives, emotions, attitudes, and cognition with reference to interactions with other human beings. SOC 101 and a course in statistics or research design are strongly recommended. Cross-listed with SOC 431, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
SOC101 - How Society Really Works: An Introduction to Sociology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Community Psychology
description: Focuses on human and social problems in a systemic context. Primary prevention and community empowerment strategies employed are emphasized for individual, community, and social benefit. A course in research methods is recommended but not required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
PSYC295 - Statistical Methods (3)
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Learning
description: Fundamental concepts of learning, with emphasis on classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and observational learning. Human applications of animal learning principles are stressed.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Professional Development: Senior
description: Fourth in a series of four created to provide direct touch points with students to facilitate academic preparedness, career exploration, establish post graduate trajectories, and develop resources for transitioning post college. With the support of faculty/staff, finalize resources for transitioning post college. Specifically resumes (workforce) and curriculum vitae (graduate school), write personal statements, identify and engage/apply for post college opportunities, and practice interview skills. (Pass/Fail.)
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PSYC333 - Professional Development: Junior (1)

title: Industrial/Organizational Psychology
description: Introduces fundamental theories, concepts, methods, issues, and psychology of organizational and employee effectiveness. Topics include employee selection, job analysis, criterion development, predictors of job performance, work teams, leadership, motivation, job attitudes, stress and well-being, and organizational development.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Psychology and Law
description: The course provides an overview of research in the field of psychology and the law, and documents how psycholegal research relates to pressing issues facing the judicial system. A partial list of topics includes: eyewitness testimony, jury deliberations, criminal behavior, evidence, and the structure and function of the legal system. A course in statistics or research design is strongly recommended.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Statistical Modeling in R
description: Focuses on statistical methods for practical data analysis, including parametric and non-parametric analyses, ANOVA, multiple and logistic regression, generalized linear models, and dimension reduction methods using R to examine and understand human behavior. Students will conduct a research project designed in partnership with a professional stakeholder that delivers actionable outcomes. May be taken for credit as ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ITM430 - Predictive Analytics (3)
ITM340 - Big Data and Web Analytics (3)
Completed the following: 
DATA-R322 - Principles of Data Science (3)

title: Capstone Perspectives: History and Systems (FF)
description: A detailed account of the history of psychology encompassing the philosophical antecedents of modern psychology as well as the influential pioneers. Topics include history of psychology as a field of scientific inquiry, overview of development of schools of thought, prominent figures and key theories.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Directed Research in Psychology
description: An undergraduate student assists on a research project, supervised by a member of the psychology faculty. Enrollment is contingent on a voluntary commitment to a research project by both parties (faculty and student). Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credits. Recommended psychology major, cumulative GPA above 3.00 and upper-division standing.

title: Capstone Perspectives on Psychological Issues
description: Controversial issues and social problems are addressed. Students analyze how different areas of psychology contribute to the understanding of contemporary problems making psychological theory and research relevant and understandable to community agencies/groups.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Contemporary Topics in Psychology
description: Provides advanced coverage of topics in the instructor's area of expertise, with particular focus on the application of psychological principles to address contemporary social problems. May be repeated for a maximum of nine credits.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC321 - Research Methods (4)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Internship
description: Some internship experiences are available through the department. Credit may be granted for psychological activities in applied settings. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 12 credits, not to be taken in a single semester. Recommended to be a psychology major and have a cumulative GPA above 3.00, completion of PSYC 321 and upper-division standing.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Senior Thesis
description: An individual research project in psychology selected by student. Proposal must be approved by instructor before enrolling. Recommended projects are those which will contribute to the body of psychological knowledge or will apply psychological principles to practical problems. Recommended for psychology students planning on graduate school. Course may be taken for a maximum of 3 credits. Recommended completion of PSYC 101, PSYC 321, and upper-division standing.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study is an opportunity to earn academic credit outside of the established curriculum. It assumes the confluence of two streams of interest that of a student and that of a professor. Thus, enrollment is contingent on a voluntary commitment to the project by both parties. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credits. Recommended psychology major, have a cumulative GPA above 3.0, completion of PSYC 321 and upper-division standing.

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Experimental Research Sem II
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: How to Plan a Career in Public Service
description: Examines key skills for public servants that are marketable to employers, and career paths that include employment in public, private, and non-profit organizations.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How Public Policy is Made
description: Examines the public policy process including stages, types, and the roles public servants play.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How the Legislative Process Works
description: Examines the process by which decisions are in made in legislative bodies, including parliamentary process and budgetary allocations.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Engage Citizens
description: Examines importance of citizen participation in policy processes, including the role of social capital.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Manage Public Finances
description: Examines key issues in public financial management, including basic government accounting and regulations governing public spending.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Navigate Ethical Challenges
description: Provides an introduction to ethical challenges faced by public servants, including legal responsibilities.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Deal with People
description: Explores issues related to leadership, mentoring and training employees, and working with others.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Negotiate Risks
description: Examines the way in which organizations make an assessment of, control, and transfer risk.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: How to Make Policy Changes
description: Introduction to techniques used to assess the potential impacts of policy choices.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Health Services Administration
description: Overview of the health care industry and the issues that confront this dynamic system, including the changing roles of components of the system as well as technical, economic, political and social forces responsible for those changes.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), Foundations of Public Health PracticeCertificate (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Healthy Environments, Healthy People
description: Introduces students to the field of environmental health, which is dedicated to protecting human health by preventing harmful exposures in the environment. Covers the major sources of environmental pollution and how contaminants in our air, water, soil, and food can cause disease and illness in the population. Students will also learn the important role played by local health departments in protecting the public from these harmful exposures. Major regulations and other strategies for eliminating or reducing environmental exposures that put our health at risk are presented.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), Foundations of Public Health PracticeCertificate (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Foundations of Public Health
description: Introduction to the online BA in Public Health (BAPH). Offers an introduction to public health and explores different disciplines within the field. The course will address the social determinants of health and explain how they influence personal and community health.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Evidence-Based Public Health
description: Evidence-based Public Health provides advanced study in public health prevention strategies, with an emphasis on coalition development, assessment, and evaluation of community-based prevention and health promotion strategies.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Evidence-Based Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Program Evaluation
description: Examines program evaluation as a systematic way to improve and account for public health actions. Covers the utilization of various tools to measure if a program meets the strategic plan or contract outcome goals for the program. Topics include the development of strategic plans, logic models, and evaluation methods.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Evidence-Based Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Health Policy and Ethics
description: Survey of the policy and legal issues related to public health, health care delivery, and environmental health. Examination of how the legislative, judicial, and executive branches of government at federal, state, and local levels interact and create law. Focuses on public policy and transformational ethics as a tool to improve population health, interactions between private and public sector, and legal issues health professionals commonly encounter.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Foundations of Public Health PracticeCertificate (online)

title: Public Health Data Analysis
description: Introduction to statistics and data analysis including descriptive statistics, confidence intervals, risk and odds, categorical data analysis, hypothesis testing, correlation, linear regression. Emphasis on quantitative reasoning, problem solving, communicating ideas, and applications in public health. Includes use of Excel and calculators to handle computations.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)
Completed the following: 
MATH108 - Intermediate Algebra (3)
Or satisfactory placement score or foundations of mathematics course

title: Prevention and Intervention Programming
description: Explores the development of interventions aimed at public health prevention, with an emphasis on collaborating with local communities in the design and evaluation. Outreach efforts and best practices amongst priority groups, such as rural communities, will be investigated and discussed.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Foundations of Public Health PracticeCertificate (online)

title: Promoting Mental, Emotional, and Social Wellbeing
description: Analyzes the relationship between the mental, emotional, and social dimensions of wellbeing from a public health perspective. Covers evidence-based approaches to assessing and promoting these interrelated dimensions of wellbeing, with a focus on practical applications in a range of workplace, educational, caregiving, family, and community settings.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Health and Human Behavior Certificate (online)

title: Community Assessment in Public Health
description: Examines community assessment of public health needs and issues through the lens of the ten Essential Public Health Services (EPHS) utilizing a variety of community assessment models. Utilizes the policy development and assurance of program delivery phases in the EPHS framework.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Community-Driven Health Solutions Certificate (online)

title: Health Behavior Theory and Practice
description: Evidence-based health interventions have a sound theoretical basis. Learn about the social and environmental influences that impact the initiation, maintenance, and change of health behaviors. Learn how to apply these principles when creating interventions focused on improving health and preventing disease.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Health and Human Behavior Certificate (online)

title: Promoting Health in Idaho Communities
description: Evaluates delivery of health programs from Idaho’s major urban areas to its frontier and rural counties. Coverage of program delivery models from local public health districts, Idaho Department of Health and Welfare, non-profit organizations, and for profit organizations. Particular focus on the importance of collaboration between various agencies and organizations to promote community health.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Community-Driven Health Solutions Certificate (online)

title: Human Health and Disease
description: Introduction to practices of health and wellness emphasizing lifestyles and measures of health. This course will cover general concepts related to the human body and chronic and infectious diseases, as well as how organ systems are affected by disease.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Health and Human Behavior Certificate (online)

title: Quality Improvement and Performance Management
description: Provides an overview of methods and efforts to achieve measurable improvements in the efficiency, effectiveness, performance, accountability, outcomes, and other indicators of quality in services or processes, which achieve equity and improve the health of the community.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Research Methods in Health
description: Design of experiments, methods of analysis, interpretation and communication of results, and use of research to support evidence-based practice.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Evidence-Based Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Public Health Communications
description: Familiarizes students with the prominent theories, issues, and topics in the field of health communication with a range of topics, including illness and health, historical and contemporary issues, patient and provider experiences, cultural differences in health, public awareness/prevention/intervention campaigns, and the role of media and relationships in health communication.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Community-Driven Health Solutions Certificate (online)

title: Strategic Planning and Project Management
description: Terminology, concepts, and forms used in both strategic planning and project management. Focuses on the similarities and differences between strategic planning (short and long term planning) and project planning (specific business activity that has a beginning and end).
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), or Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Public Health Project
description: Synthesis of public health concepts into developed projects within various health care venues.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online)

title: Determinants of Health
description: Provides a comprehensive understanding of the environmental, social, and cultural factors that influence health. This course will cover topics, theories, and public health methods associated with determinants of health. Topics include gender, ethnicity/race, relationships, socioeconomic status, built environment, community, political systems, stress and adverse childhood experiences, and culture their influences on individual and community health.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Health and Human Behavior Certificate (online)

title: Collaborating for Change
description: Social change and community work relies on cross-sector collaboration. Investigates key tools and theories that empower and engage communities to address their own issues.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Public Health BA (online), Public Health Certificate (online), or Community-Driven Health Solutions Certificate (online)

title: Epidemiology
description: Study of the distribution and determinants of disease within human populations.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), or Evidence-Based Public Health Certificate (online)

title: Student Outcomes Assessment
description: Seniors in the department of Community and Environmental Health are required to take an exit survey and participate in professional and career building activities. Additional certification exams will be provided by emphasis areas.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online)

title: Capstone Experience
description: Design and engage in a capstone project that synthesizes healthcare and/or public health concepts and addresses current health and wellness-related issues within various contexts.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online)

title: Public Health Internship
description: Practical field experience in public health. Opportunity to apply knowledge and theory learned in classroom to practical setting. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail.

title: Contemporary Issues in Public Health
description: Explores how the identification and amelioration of public health issues change over time. Current leading health threats, including the root causes, social drivers and resulting disparities, will be examined. The application of real-time analysis and intervention will be discussed. May be repeated for a maximum of 9 credits.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Public Health BA (online), Foundations of Public Health PracticeCertificate (online), Health and Human Behavior Certificate (online), Evidence-Based Public Health Certificate (online), or Community-Driven Health Solutions Certificate (online)

title: Intro to Public Relations
description: Public relations as a professional field: history, theory, principles, and practices.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: PR Campaign Strategies
description: Students develop a comprehensive campaign plan for a client to explore campaign strategies. Recommend concurrent enrollment in MEDIA 302.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
PR201 - Intro to Public Relations (3)

title: Media and Social Media Strategies
description: Media relations and social media methods, strategies and practices through discussion and project-driven experience. Completion of MEDIA 301 is recommended.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Advanced PR Writing
description: Advanced theory and practice in producing public relations materials. Content varies from semester to semester. Course may be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA301 - Multimedia Storytelling (3)

title: PR Case Studies
description: Examination of public relations issues, impacts, contexts, strategies and applications through case study research.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA302 - Information and Analysis (2)

title: Strategies
description: Advanced theory and practice in specialized public relations strategies. Content varies from semester to semester. Course may be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA301 - Multimedia Storytelling (3)
PR201 - Intro to Public Relations (3)
PR301 - PR Campaign Strategies (3)

title: Advanced Public Relations
description: Advanced study of public relations applications, areas, issues, and topics. Content varies from semester to semester. Course may be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MEDIA302 - Information and Analysis (2)
MEDIA201 - Intro to Integrated Media and Strategic Communications (2)
MEDIA300 - Media Writing (1)

title: Internship
description: Supervised fieldwork. For more information on internships, see University-Wide Courses in Chapter 11. Recommended completion of PR 201, PR 301, PR 351 and a GPA of 2.75.

title: Introduction to Public Relations
description: Public relations as a professional field: history, theory, principles, and practices.
prerequisites: Upper division standing

title: Technology for Professionals
description: Students gain competencies with technologies used in the contemporary workplace.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Career Planning
description: Individual planning and preparation for a career that aligns with interests and values.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Integrated Strategic Communications BA (online)

title: Public Relations Campaign Strategies
description: Students develop a comprehensive campaign plan for a client to explore appropriate campaign strategies.

title: Professional Writing Basics
description: Foundational writing course with a focus on grammar, formats, AP basics, and writing for business.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Interviewing
description: Examines the process of interviewing in multiple communication contexts including print and broadcast journalism and public information sessions.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Multimedia Storytelling
description: Students use web tools and freely available software to gain concrete skills and understandings of technologies that are transforming the media.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Conflict and Collaboration
description: Theories and techniques to manage conflict and work collaboratively.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Public Relations Ethics
description: Theories and principles used in ethical decision-making for public relations and corporate communications, including digital ethics, transparency, and codes of ethics.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Visual Communication
description: Theory and practice of various forms of visual communication.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Business and Professional Communication
description: Communication tools, processes, and practices in business and professional settings.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Applied Research for Professionals
description: A client-based approach to research and data-driven decision making to prepare students for public relations research demands.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Media and Social Media Strategies for Professionals
description: Media relations and social media methods, strategies and practices through discussion and project-driven experience.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Media Law
description: Media-related legal issues facing media and public relations practitioners.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Global Public Relations
description: Evolution and practice of public relations in different countries with an emphasis on diversity and multiculturalism.
prerequisites: Must have upper division standing

title: Public Relations Case Studies
description: Examination of public relations issues, impacts, contexts, strategies and applications through case study research.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Writing for Professionals
description: Writing for communications professionals, including public relations outputs; writing for media; writing for the web; creating proposals and pitches; reporting research; and visual organization.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Community Relations
description: Principles, philosophy, and techniques for building community relationships that benefit organizations and individuals.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Crisis Management
description: Crisis management theories, methods, and processes. Students apply crisis management knowledge and skills to develop a Crisis Management Plan.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Project Management
description: Project management methods, processes, and software. Students apply project management knowledge and skills to develop a Project Management Plan.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Topics in Public Relations
description: Examination of public relations applications, areas, issues, and topics. Students should consult with their advisor before enrolling in class. Content varies from semester to semester. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Internship
description: Supervised fieldwork. Students have the option of enrolling for either 7- or 15-week internships. For more information on internships, see University-Wide Courses in Chapter 11 and read the Communication and Media Department Internship Guidelines available on the department webpage. Recommended senior standing and a minimum cumulative public relations program GPA of 2.75.

title: Capstone (FF)
description: Students apply their knowledge and skill to produce and present public relations projects, plans and proposals based on research of an industry aligned with their career goals to be included in their senior portfolio.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Patient Assessment
description: Theory and skill application with clinical focus to perform physical assessment to include assessment techniques, standardized data collection formats, body system assessment, normal findings, relevant variations from normal, and documentation.

title: Interprofessional Patient Care Skills Lab
description: An interprofessional disciplinary team approach is used to teach basic patient care skills and interventions to restore and protect health.

title: Principles of Radiographic Imaging I
description: Introduction to the exposure factors, formulas, techniques, and tools employed when acquiring digital radiographic images and their impact on image quality and patient radiation dose.

title: Principles of Radiographic Imaging I Lab
description: Laboratory demonstration and practice of the principles utilized when acquiring digital radiographic images with an emphasis on the safe operation of digital x-ray machinery.

title: Principles of Radiographic Imaging II
description: Analysis of the production and manipulation of digital radiographic and fluoroscopic images with an emphasis on safe and effective operation of x-ray machinery and minimizing patient dose.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI200 - Principles of Radiographic Imaging I (3)

title: Principles of Radiographic Imaging II Lab
description: Laboratory demonstration and practice of the principles of the production and manipulation of digital radiographic and fluoroscopic images with an emphasis on the safe operation of digital x-ray and fluoroscopic machinery.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI201 - Principles of Radiographic Imaging I Lab (1)

title: Radiographic Positioning I
description: Basic concepts and procedures used in obtaining diagnostic radiographs of the upper and lower extremities, chest, and abdomen.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Laboratory Practicum I
description: Laboratory demonstration and practice of the radiographic positions and procedures discussed in RADSCI 222.

title: Introduction to Clinical Experience
description: Introduction to clinical agency structure, health law and ethics, professionalism and initial clinical practice. Professional observation required.

title: Radiographic Positioning II
description: Continuation of RADSCI 222. Basic concepts and procedures used in obtaining diagnostic radiographs of the bony thorax, pelvic girdle, pelvis, hips, spine, and craniofacial anatomy.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI222 - Radiographic Positioning I (3)

title: Laboratory Practicum II
description: Laboratory demonstration and practice of the radiographic positions and procedures discussed in RADSCI 242.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI223 - Laboratory Practicum I (1)

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI104 - Patient Assessment (1)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Pharmacology and Contrast Medias
description: Concepts of pharmacology as it relates to the delivery of contrast medias and selected medications associated with contrast media reactions.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Radiologic Sciences BS, or Radiologic Sciences Certificates Admission

title: Radiobiology and Protection
description: Principles and concepts underlying the biological effects of radiation and federal/state/international radiation protection standards.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Fluoroscopic and Contrast Media Examinations
description: Exploration of routine and specialized procedures which utilize fluoroscopic imaging equipment and require the administration of radiographic contrast media.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI105 - Interprofessional Patient Care Skills Lab (2)
RADSCI222 - Radiographic Positioning I (3)

title: Law and Ethics in Radiologic Sciences
description: Critical analysis of the theory and development of health laws, professional liability and ethics related to radiologic science. The course will examine current policies and ethical challenges that affect the practice of medical imaging.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI242 - Radiographic Positioning II (3)

title: Information Technology in Radiologic Sciences
description: Review of computer hardware and networking principles as applied to information technology utilized in the medical imaging department. Topics to include basic networking, PACS, RIS, HIS, DICOM standards, and information security.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI200 - Principles of Radiographic Imaging I (3)

title: Imaging Pathophysiology
description: General survey of various diseases and pathology of the human body as they pertain to radiology. Emphasis on how pathology is demonstrated on medical images and its effect on radiographic diagnosis.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI242 - Radiographic Positioning II (3)

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI285 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

description: Supervised clinical experience.

RADSCI375 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

RADSCI375 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

Completed the following: 
RADSCI376 - Radiologic Sciences Clinic Experience (4)

title: Radiologic Colloquium
description: Topics will be selected from current health care issues. These topics will be presented for discussion by appropriate health care professionals.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Radiologic Sciences BS

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI385 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

title: Development of an Imaging Department
description: Introduction to the set up and operation of a radiology department including design principles, projection of demands, and providing for growth and development. Structural and shielding requirements will be discussed.

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI395 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI386 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (6)

title: Health Promotion and Leadership (FF)
description: Analysis of considerations related to preventative health care measures and patient advocacy. Particular emphasis on related imaging procedures and advancement of public awareness. Designed to increase student resiliency when navigating the clinical setting as both a student and professional.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI313 - Fluoroscopic and Contrast Media Examinations (2)

title: Senior Recitation and Integration
description: Synthesis and evaluation of radiographic theories and concepts introduced throughout the radiography program.  Areas of weakness will be identified and addressed in preparation for national certification examination.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI311 - Radiobiology and Protection (3)

title: Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience
description: Supervised clinical experience. Terminal clinical competency will be validated.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RADSCI405 - Radiologic Sciences Clinical Experience (4)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Principles of Refugee Resettlement
description: Explores the resettlement process in the United States. Provides knowledge and skills needed to assist in the resettlement experience of refugees. Examination of personal values and beliefs and their impacts on practice are integral. Cross-listed with SOCWRK 407, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Social Work BA

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Orientation
description: Introduces students to resort operations and hospitality management and the fundamentals of online learning. Includes a self-paced foundation or refresher in quantitative skills and industry overviews. (Pass/Fail.)
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Resort and Hospitality Management Certificate, Management BBA, or Bachelor of Applied Science Online (BAS)

title: Resort/Hospitality Management
description: Introduces critical management skills involved in planning, structuring, leading, and controlling in a resort or hospitality organization. Emphasizes the analysis and understanding of the core aspects of management: organizing, planning, leading, motivating, and controlling.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA
Declared or admitted to Resort and Hospitality Management Certificate
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Resort Sales and Marketing
description: Introduction to sales and marketing theory and principles as applied to resort and hospitality management. The course emphasizes methods of identifying and meeting the wants and needs of people and organizations. The relationship between sales and marketing management techniques is addressed. May be taken for MKTG or ROHM credit, but not both.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA
Declared or admitted to Resort and Hospitality Management Certificate
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Hospitality Law
description: Introduces students to the major legal issues and risks involved in the resort and hospitality industry. Emphasizes the implication of the law on business decision-making, risk management policy development, and prevention of liabilities. Topics will include the legal system, torts, contracts and sales, agency and employment law, and other specific legal issues that arise in the resort and hospitality industry, such as liability to guests and facilities management.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA
Declared or admitted to Resort and Hospitality Management Certificate
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Training and Development
description: Covers the skills and knowledge needed to develop and maintain a competent staff. Examines current trends and the strategic role of training. Includes decision-making in hiring, training, promoting, and dismissal; evaluation of HRM processes and systems; and training program design and effectiveness.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Declared or admitted to Management BBA
Declared or admitted to Resort and Hospitality Management Certificate
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to resort operations and hospitality management. No more than 12 internship credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Recommended cumulative GPA of 2.5 and approval of faculty advisor. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credits. Must be admitted to the Management BBA degree or the Resort Operations and Hospitality Management certificate. Recommended: RHM303 or RHM313; and RHM323 or RHM343, or RHM363.

title: Interprofessional Patient Care Skills Lab
description: An interprofessional disciplinary team approach is used to teach basic patient care skills and interventions to restore and protect health.

title: Foundations of Respiratory Care
description: Theory and application of physics, chemistry, basic therapeutics, and disease management for respiratory therapists. Topics to include oxygen and carbon dioxide transport, humidity, aerosol therapy, airway clearance, hyperinflation, medical gasses, and patient monitoring.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Respiratory Care BS

title: Foundations of Respiratory Care Lab
description: Equipment and basic therapeutics for respiratory care. Compressed gas cylinders, oxygen delivery devices, hyperinflation, airway clearance techniques, pulse oximetry, capnography, large and small volume nebulizers, MDI/DPI devices, and introduction to artificial airways.

title: Clinical Practicum I
description: Experience in the hospital with patients, techniques, and equipment. Emphasis on use of medical gases.

title: Pulmonary Assessment
description: Theory and application of basic pulmonary assessment including inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Cardiopulmonary Renal Physiology
description: Normal and clinical physiological functions of the pulmonary, circulatory and renal systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation
description: Principles and application of hyperinflation therapy, chest physiotherapy, medication calculation, arterial blood gas interpretation and an introduction to adult mechanical ventilation.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE203 - Foundations of Respiratory Care (3)

title: Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation Lab
description: Use and application of hyperinflation therapy devices, chest physiotherapy, suction devices, oral endotracheal intubation and adult mechanical ventilation.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE203 - Foundations of Respiratory Care (3)

title: Clinical Practicum II
description: Experience in the hospitals with patients, techniques, and equipment used in hyperinflation therapy and chest physiotherapy.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE203 - Foundations of Respiratory Care (3)

title: Cardiopulmonary Diagnostics
description: Students will develop foundational knowledge regarding gathering and interpretation of laboratory tests, radiographic images and cardiopulmonary diagnostics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE220 - Cardiopulmonary Renal Physiology (3)
RESPCARE203 - Foundations of Respiratory Care (3)

title: Summer Clinical Practicum
description: Experience in critical care units with patients, techniques and equipment as applied to mechanical ventilation and artificial airways. Recommended completion of RESPCARE 228.

title: Introduction to Evidence-Based Inquiry
description: Introduction to locating, selecting, and critically reviewing medical literature relevant to the practice of evidence-based respiratory and critical care. Constructing and researching clinical questions. Skills for keeping abreast of new medical information, deciding which information is valid and applicable to patient care, and using this information to improve patient care.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE203 - Foundations of Respiratory Care (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH153 - Statistical Reasoning (FM) (3)
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Principles of Pharmacotherapeutics
description: Principles, practical uses, and interaction of drugs and their relationship to disease.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)
RESPCARE223 - Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: General Pathology
description: Human pathology pertaining to systems of defense, modes of injury, diseases of development and function, heart, hematopoietic lymphoreticular, and respiratory systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Advanced Mechanical Ventilation
description: Theory and clinical application of advanced mechanical ventilation of the adult, including care and management of artificial airways, and clinical application of hemodynamic monitoring.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE223 - Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Advanced Mechanical Ventilation Lab
description: In-depth practice, application and understanding of a variety of state-of-the-art critical care ventilators used to ventilate adults.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE223 - Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Clinical Practicum III
description: Experience in the hospital with patients, techniques, and equipment as applied to mechanical ventilation and artificial airways.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE223 - Respiratory Care Therapeutics and Introduction to Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care
description: Theory and application of therapeutic techniques specific to neonatal and pediatric patients. Congenital heart disease, prematurity, infant respiratory distress syndrome, pediatric specific disease processes.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE303 - Advanced Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care Lab
description: Equipment and mechanical ventilators specific to neonatal and pediatric patients. High frequency ventilation, neonatal resuscitation, exogenous surfactant, basic care of neonates, and pediatric specific ventilation techniques.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE303 - Advanced Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Clinical Practicum IV
description: Experience in the hospital and other health care environments with any or all aspects of respiratory care.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE303 - Advanced Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Cardiopulmonary Diseases and Management
description: Emphasis placed on cardiopulmonary conditions, disease states, practices and special procedures encountered in hospital, out-patient and rehabilitation setting. Case studies/problem-based learning will be used to develop students who can gather and synthesize information for comprehensive approach to the practice of respiratory care.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE229 - Cardiopulmonary Diagnostics (3)

title: Professional Communications in Health Care
description: Focus on professional written and oral communication as practiced within the scope of respiratory care. Develop audience specific written documents, including writing that is appropriate for a professional journal or conference, and prepare, deliver, and evaluate oral presentations.
prerequisites: Admission to BSRC Degree Advancement Program

title: Culture, Diversity, and Ethics in Healthcare
description: Examine the influence of social, cultural, and religious beliefs on health in multicultural and diverse groups. Students will learn how to respond and navigate to these multicultural and diverse groups in a healthcare setting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE303 - Advanced Mechanical Ventilation (4)

title: Clinical Practicum V
description: Experience in the hospital and other health care environments targeted at desired area(s) of specialty in respiratory care.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE328 - Clinical Practicum IV (4)

title: Research in Respiratory Care I
description: Research project carried out by the student in collaboration with a supervising member of the Respiratory Care faculty.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
HLTH382 - Research Methods in Health (3)
RESPCARE282 - Introduction to Evidence-Based Inquiry (3)

title: Advanced Neonatal, Pediatric and Adult Critical Care
description: Theory and clinical application of advanced neonatal, pediatric and adult critical care management including high frequency ventilation, early mobility, ECLS and ICU Delirium.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE323 - Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care (3)

title: Education and Leadership in Respiratory Care
description: Exploration of organizational leadership methodology and management of respiratory care departments. Discussion on educational methodologies among various populations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE323 - Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care (3)

title: Quality Improvement in Health Care
description: Introduction and evaluation of current approaches to assessing risk and improving health care quality through the practice of continuous quality improvement. Focuses on conceptual understanding and experiential learning.

title: Critical Review of Health Care Research
description: Locating, selecting, and critically reviewing medical and lay literature relevant to the practice of health care. Constructing and researching clinical questions. Skills for keeping abreast of new medical information, deciding which of this information is valid and applicable to patient care, and using this information to improve patient care.

title: Professional Preparation
description: Culmination of portfolio development; review and preparation for credentialing examinations offered by the National Board for Respiratory Care. Emphasis will be placed on understanding of branching-logic examinations as well as computer-based, multiple-choice examinations related to clinical practice of respiratory care.

title: Senior Theory: Advanced Concepts
description: Techniques and methods used to analyze and evaluate the health status of critically ill patients with emphasis on the respiratory and cardiovascular systems.

title: Teaching Techniques for Health Care Professionals
description: An interactive, online course designed to provide health care professionals with the skills needed to provide effective peer and client education.

title: Sleep Medicine
description: Overview of sleep medicine, anatomy and physiology of sleep and breathing. Introduction to sleep disorders and polysomnograpy including monitoring techniques and instrumentation.

title: Current Topics in Respiratory Disease
description: Discussion of current issues related to respiratory disease, including pathophysiology, management and outcomes.

title: Leadership and Management for Health Care Professionals
description: Extensive examination of current practices/trends of techniques used in the leadership of the health care environment. Emphasis will be placed upon specific skill sets used by the managers of today's workforce.

title: Patient Advocacy and Ethical Considerations
description: An advanced exploration of the responsibilities required of health care practitioners. Designed to help students develop a clearer understanding of patient's rights and in turn become advocates for those rights.

title: Introduction to Disease Management
description: An introduction to the purpose and application of disease management in patients with chronic illness. Emphasis will be on management of individuals with COPD, sleep disordered breathing, asthma and congestive heart failure. Discharge planning, patient education, disease management strategies and methods to reduce hospital readmissions will be discussed.

title: Capstone (FF)
description: Students, working individually and in teams, will find and use studies and articles from a variety of healthcare disciplines to develop an evidence-based literature review and a culminating group project that addresses a current respiratory care process/problem from the perspective of several different members of the healthcare team.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Respiratory Care, AS Track BS, or Respiratory Care, AAS or AHS Track BS
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE355 - Professional Communications in Health Care (3)

title: Research in Respiratory Care II
description: Focus on students' understanding of the research process and research writing skills to create a research manuscript.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE323 - Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care (3)
RESPCARE422 - Research in Respiratory Care I (1)

title: Professional Preparation Capstone (FF)
description: Reflection on growth and future plans in respiratory care and preparation for credentialing examinations offered by the National Board for Respiratory Care. Emphasis will be placed on the understanding of branching-logic examinations as well as computer-based, multiple-choice examinations related to the clinical practice of respiratory care.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE323 - Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care (3)

description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student’s major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Online discussions of topics related to respiratory care.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
RESPCARE323 - Neonatal and Pediatric Respiratory Care (3)
Or co-requisites listed.

title: Step 1: Inquiry Approaches to Teaching
description: Theory and practice necessary to design and deliver inquiry-based math and science instruction. Explore and practice the guided inquiry process, modify lesson plans and implement them in a school setting. Requires background check and fieldwork.

title: Step 2: Inquiry-Based Lesson Design
description: Continuation of STEM-ED 101. Develop skills in designing, teaching, analyzing, and assessing STEM lessons. Create inquiry-based lesson plans and implement them in a school setting. Requires a background check and fieldwork.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
STEM-ED101 - Step 1: Inquiry Approaches to Teaching (1)

title: Models and Modeling in the Physical Science (FN)
description: Methods and practices of constructing and evaluating scientific models, with an emphasis on problems in light, color and vision. Topics include model building, representations, communication.

title: STEM-Education Living and Learning Community
description: First year STEM-Ed Living Learning Community participants will explore aspects of success in education and the value of STEM disciplines. Course activities will promote academic achievement, community service, and team-building. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: Knowing and Learning in Mathematics and Science (FS)
description: Introduction to theories of cognition and learning and the research which supports best teaching practices. Topics include learning progressions, memory, individual development, motivation and intelligence. Students will design and teach lessons that apply learning theory. Emphasis on learning in the STEM disciplines.

title: Philosophical Perspectives on Science and Mathematics (FH)
description: Introduction to the historical, social, and philosophical implications of math and science. Laboratory focuses on replication of significant discoveries. Cross-listed with PHIL 220, may be taken once for credit.

title: STEM-Education Living and Learning Community
description: Second year STEM-Ed Living Learning Community participants will explore aspects of success in education and the value of STEM disciplines. Course activities will promote academic achievement, community service, and team-building. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: Classroom Interactions
description: Apply learning theories in secondary settings (grades 6-12). Develop, enact and evaluate activities and strategies for teaching STEM in ways that make learning accessible to all students. Requires current background check and fieldwork. Recommended  completion of STEM-ED 102 and STEM-ED 210.

title: Research Methods
description: Engagement in laboratory-based methods used by scientists and engineers with implications for science and engineering education. Design and implementation of laboratory investigations. Written and oral reports of results.

title: Learning Assistant Seminar
description: Preparation and development of capacity to act as a tutor in STEM education learning settings. Includes inquiry-based theory and methods necessary to support effective one-on-one and small-group practice and review in STEM content. Requires background check and fieldwork. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credits.

title: STEM Teaching Methods
description: Methods used to plan, enact, assess, and reflect upon sequences of lessons for teaching and learning in inclusive secondary STEM classrooms. Requires current background check and fieldwork. Recommended admission to the professional year and completion of STEM-ED 310.

title: Life, Earth, and Physical Science for Teachers
description: Explores in-depth science content in the disciplinary areas of biology, earth science, physics, and chemistry as described in current national and state standards. Emphasis will be placed on middle level science content and engagement with student reasoning and science and engineering practices.

title: Apprentice Teaching
description: Teaching in a secondary STEM classroom under the mentorship of an experienced in-service teacher in the field. Requires background check and fieldwork. Recommended completion of STEM-ED 410.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Seminar in STEM Research
description: Seminars by STEM educators and researchers on a wide range of subjects, including pedagogy and content. Format may include student presentation and discussion. Students will attend seminars, write summaries, and search for relevant literature. May be repeated for credit.

title: Experiences in Public Service
description: Become familiar with how to utilize the characteristics of experiential and career learning (ECL) teaching methods in public service fields through learning and doing. Preparation for future courses that use ECL to enhance learning opportunities.

title: Living Learning Community: Leadership and Engagement
description: First-year Leadership and Engagement Living Learning Community participants will examine leadership theories and concepts as well as leadership in practice through community service projects. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: Problem Solving in Public Service
description: Introduces students to the history and variety of public service professions, examines problem solving strategies and techniques within public service, and provides structured opportunities for career research and planning.

title: Data in Public Service
description: Introduces essential concepts of applied research, data collection, data analysis, and communication of results. Develops familiarity with relevant public databases and community evaluation. Explores common research fallacies and their connection to ethics of public service.

title: Leadership in Public Service
description: Introduction to the skillsets and mindsets needed to effectively and ethically lead others in the public sphere. Skills include knowing personal values, communicating effectively, and working with diverse people and teams. Through practice and reflection, the skills needed to effectively lead and engage in our democracy will be developed.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Advocacy in Action
description: Engages students in designing and leading social impact advocacy campaigns, and developing relevant skills, while exploring various strategies, actions, personal attributes, external factors, bridge-building tactics, and local community elements that are involved in advocacy work.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Human Rights Certificate
Or
Completed the following: 
HIST330 - Human Rights Past and Present (3)
Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Policy Analysis and Implementation
description: Concepts and methods applied in policy analysis and implementation. Exploration of current energy and environmental policy topics at the local community and urban scale, and common tools used to approach policy problems. Topics may include policy field mapping, program process flow analysis, target audience analysis, implementation improvement planning, policy question definition, and policy alternative construction and evaluation.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
SPS240 - Data in Public Service (3)

title: Ethics in Public Service
description: Explores the ethical dilemmas encountered by public servants, covering general organizational issues like conflicts of interest, cheating, and insubordination, as well as challenges specific to public service, such as the dynamics between political leaders and civil servants, and balancing efficiency with responsiveness. All topics are examined within the moral framework of public service.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Policy Analysis
description: Introduces the study and practice of public policy. Addresses fundamental public policy questions and issues: The rationales for public intervention in private affairs, the limitations of collective action, generic public policy instruments, measuring social costs and benefits, and the roles of policy analysts in democratic societies.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Applied Research for Public Service
description: Explores the philosophy, scope, and approaches of research in the public sector. Examines both quantitative and qualitative research methodologies and the structure of research design.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Public Service Studio
description: Immersive experience participating in interdisciplinary undergraduate research in a collaborative setting mentored by faculty with opportunities to bridge research in other courses and community outlets. Students may take studio up to three times for credit. Recommended completion of SPS 200.

title: Interdisciplinary Research Fundamentals
description: Exposure to interdisciplinary research design with an emphasis in qualitative approaches, including how to define a research question, articulate different methods common to this collaborative approach, and navigate ethical challenges in research. Understand the commonalities and differences between traditional and interdisciplinary research design.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Methods in Interdisciplinary Research
