title: Introduction to Vertically Integrated Projects
description: Exploration of VIP projects through direct engagement with VIP faculty and students in seminars, meetings, interviews, and other guided experiences.  Introduction to success in team-based research projects. Learn about the skills and mindsets needed to make progress on ambitious, multi-disciplinary,  long-term projects.  Create and connect academic, career, and personal goals related to VIP projects.

title: Vertically Integrated Projects
description: Orientation to team-based research projects coached by faculty and graduate students. Team members contribute to the project's success by exploring different roles on large multidisciplinary design/discovery teams. Skills learned include a basic level of professional (e.g., communication, project management, time management) and technical skills (e.g., lab skills, scientific method application). Topics considered in the courses are those necessary for the success of the VIP project. Course and topic(s) may be repeated up to 4 semesters for credit.

title: Vertically Integrated Projects
description: Students engage in project work with faculty and graduate students on multi-disciplinary, team-based, research projects. Participants demonstrate advanced research skills. Topics considered in the courses are those necessary for the success of the VIP project. Course and topic may be repeated up to 6 semesters for credit. Recommended upper-division standing.

title: Vertically Integrated Project -- Engineering
description: Students engage in project work on multi-disciplinary, team-based, research projects. Projects have significant engineering components, using math and sciences toward creative applications needed to solve engineering problems. Topics considered in the courses are those necessary for the success of the VIP project. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credits. May be taken for credit as BIOL, ENVSCI, or VIP, but not for more than one discipline. Recommended upper-division standing.

title: First Year Seminar
description: Develops life skills and attitudes needed to set and to achieve educational and personal goals. Explores university resources, services, and policies. Emphasis placed on being a successful student in the Department of World Languages.

title: International Peer Service Learning
description: In this Service-Learning class, students will mentor international students to help them integrate socially and culturally into the American college experience. Students will meet weekly with the international students in class to assist them with linguistic and cultural activities. May be repeated once for credit.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Career Exploration and Portfolio Development
description: Creation of professional portfolio to showcase language proficiency, highlight academic growth, and document specific achievements. Students will also explore career opportunities and create a tailored resume. Project-based.  Recommended completion of FREN 202, GERM 202, or SPAN 202.

title: Digital Skills for Language Learners
description: Advances the level of digital fluency for language learners and develops skills to effectively and ethically interpret information, design content, discover knowledge and communicate ideas in our digitally connected world. Explores a variety of digital toolsets to prepare students for assignments in advanced language courses, as well as contemporary professional life. Project-based.

title: Japanese Culture and Society
description: Structure and substance of Japanese culture. Development of Japanese culture from prehistory to present, the development of the Japanese worldview, cultural patterns, beliefs, behaviors, values, and norms that are reflected in Japanese culture today.

title: Japanese Culture Through Film
description: Screening and discussion of films from Japan for their historical, cultural, thematic, and aesthetic content in the context of modern Japanese culture.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
HIST121 - Asian History from Antiquity to the Present (FH) (3)
FILM220 - Cinema History and Aesthetics (FA) (3)
GLOBAL101 - Global Studies: Conflict, Cooperation, and Change (FS) (3)
GLOBAL201 - Around the Globe: World Regions in a time of Connection, Crisis, and Change (3)
POLS305 - Comparative Global Politics (3)
POLS306 - International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods (3)

title: China Today
description: Survey of contemporary China including cultural and historical roots, nation-building efforts, political, economic and social systems, and domestic and foreign policies. Discussion of Hong Kong, Tibet, and Taiwan. Cross-listed with POLS 433, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
HIST121 - Asian History from Antiquity to the Present (FH) (3)
GLOBAL101 - Global Studies: Conflict, Cooperation, and Change (FS) (3)
GLOBAL201 - Around the Globe: World Regions in a time of Connection, Crisis, and Change (3)
POLS305 - Comparative Global Politics (3)
POLS306 - International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods (3)

title: Chinese Culture Through Film
description: Screening and discussion of films from China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong for their historical, cultural, thematic, and aesthetic content in the context of modern Chinese cultures.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
HIST121 - Asian History from Antiquity to the Present (FH) (3)
FILM220 - Cinema History and Aesthetics (FA) (3)
GLOBAL101 - Global Studies: Conflict, Cooperation, and Change (FS) (3)
GLOBAL201 - Around the Globe: World Regions in a time of Connection, Crisis, and Change (3)
POLS305 - Comparative Global Politics (3)
POLS306 - International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods (3)

title: Confucianism in Chinese Culture
description: Introduction to the philosophy of Confucianism as the foundation of Chinese culture. Students will explore how Confucianism provided a framework for the development of traditional Chinese moral standards, family values, education, political philosophy, civil responsibility, and attitudes toward the natural world. Cross-listed with PHIL 322, may be taken once for credit.

title: Chinese Culture and Civilization
description: Multidisciplinary analysis of the richness and diversity of Chinese culture and civilization, including history, philosophy, religion, and literature. Topics include the aesthetic aspect of Chinese characters, Confucianism and Buddhism in Chinese culture, family values and filial piety, Chinese medicine and cuisine, the art of Chinese calligraphy and painting, traditional festivals, poetry, and short stories.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Korea Today
description: Survey of contemporary South and North Korea including cultural and historical roots, and political, economic, and social systems. Analysis of popular culture, including K-wave film, music, and television. Issues of gender roles in Korean society will also be studied. Course conducted in English.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
HIST121 - Asian History from Antiquity to the Present (FH) (3)
GLOBAL101 - Global Studies: Conflict, Cooperation, and Change (FS) (3)
GLOBAL201 - Around the Globe: World Regions in a time of Connection, Crisis, and Change (3)
POLS305 - Comparative Global Politics (3)
POLS306 - International Relations: Actors, Interactions, and Methods (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Korean Pop Culture and Society
description: Develops a dynamic understanding of contemporary Korean popular culture and Korean society by taking an interdisciplinary approach to a variety of topics. Explores the evolution of Korean popular culture in a Korean and global context. Korean popular culture will be used as a window through which Korean society will be viewed more broadly, in its many facets.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Korean Culture and Civilization
description: Incorporating feature films, novels, music, and original sources in translation, we will explore the culture and history of the Korean peninsula, both South and North, including daily life, art, religion, gender, cuisine and Korean popular culture. Course conducted in English.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Topics in French and Francophone Literature
description: A focused study of French and/or Francophone literature in translation organized around a historical period, a genre, a movement, an author, or a theme. Topics will vary each time the course is offered. Frequent writing assignments. Course conducted in English. May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor. Available once as an upper-division elective toward the French major or minor if writing assignments are done in French.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Topics in Germanic Literature
description: A focused study of Germanic literature in translation organized around a historical period, a genre, a movement, an author, or a theme. Topics will vary each time the course is offered. Frequent writing assignments. Course conducted in English. May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor. Available once as an upper-division elective toward the German major or minor if writing assignments are done in German.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Topics in Germanic Culture
description: Focused study of Germanic culture organized around a specific theme. Topics will vary each time the course is offered. Frequent writing assignments. Conducted in English. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Topics in Hispanic Literature
description: A focused study of Hispanic literature in translation organized around a historical period, a genre, a movement, an author, or a theme. Topics will vary each time the course is offered. Frequent writing assignments. Course conducted in English. May be repeated for credit with permission of instructor. Available once as an upper-division elective toward the Spanish major or minor if writing assignments are done in Spanish.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Fact/Fiction: Arthurian Legends
description: Survey of factual and fictitious contexts of King Arthur with consideration of gender roles in variations of Arthurian Legends.

title: Contemporary Arab Writers
description: Focused study of literary works by contemporary Arab writers in English translation. Particular emphasis on analysis of gender roles and identity in the context of the authors' unique social and political environments. Frequent writing assignments. Course conducted in English.

title: Theories and Methods for Teaching a Second Language in Secondary Classrooms
description: An overview of theories of second language (L2) development and of changing pedagogical practices in L2 secondary classrooms. Topics include understanding how to facilitate input and output activities to foster intercultural competence and improve interpersonal, interpretive, and presentational communication at the beginner and intermediate levels. National and state content standards as well as innovative technologies to augment L2 practice will also be covered.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
LING305 - Introduction to Language Studies (3)
SPAN415 - Introduction to Spanish Linguistics (3)
18 upper-division language credits

title: Understanding and Assessing Literacy for Second Language Learners
description: This course builds on the foundational concepts learned in WORLD 410 to understand, integrate, and assess literacy within the context of second language learning. Students will identify the dynamic and multi-layered definition for literacy and second language learning according to the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Language. Specifically, they will identify how literacy is integrated into the World Readiness Standards and every fiber of the interpersonal, presentational, interpretive, and intercultural modes of communication. As such, they will learn different ways to assess second language (L2) learners’ literacy across the modes of communication at beginner and intermediate proficiency levels using an Integrated Performance Assessment model.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
WORLD410 - Theories and Methods for Teaching a Second Language in Secondary Classrooms (3)

title: Topics in World Cinema
description: A focused study of international film organized around a specific culture, genre, movement, historical period, director, or theme. Topics will vary each time the course is offered. Frequent writing assignments. Course conducted in English. Course may be repeated for credit, topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Professional Portfolio Seminar
description: Creation of culminating portfolio to showcase accomplishments, highlight academic growth, and document specific achievements. Students produce a CV or resume, cover letter, professional social media profile, and other career-based materials. Project-based.

title: Introduction to Professional and Public Writing
description: An introduction to the field of professional and public writing. Explores key skills for the field, critical challenges writers must address today and in the future, career options and pathways, and approaches to professional development.

title: Name, Image, and Likeness
description: Develop strategies to identify opportunities for using name, image, and likeness that are consistent with brand and values. Introduction to tools and techniques to use social media platforms in safe and healthy ways.

title: Creating Digital Value
description: Investigate leveraging digital technologies and services such as user analytics and search engine algorithms in the development of digital value. Practice implementing digital information strategies to create useful, relevant, and engaging social media content.

title: Writing for Public Audiences
description: Study and write for the public in a variety of nonfiction genres. Develop and refine individual style, sharpen research and writing strategies in an area of interest, and adapt content for specific audiences outside the university.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Creative Nonfiction: Genres and Contexts
description: Students will study and write genres of creative nonfiction. Writing practice and analysis of published creative nonfiction will emphasize voice, genre, and style, with particular emphasis on a writer's rhetorical choices.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Introduction to Technical Communication
description: Explores the field of technical communication with a focus on creating practical documents and presentations for professional and workplace settings. Topics include document design, usability, accessibility, identifying user needs, developing technical graphics, and collaborating effectively.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Declared or admitted to Professional and Public Writing BA

title: Social Storytelling and Leadership
description: Introduction to the ethical and effective use of storytelling on social media platforms for the benefit of a community. Practice identifying and using narrative elements to responsibly promote content that engages and inspires audiences. Topics will include characterization, thematic significance, identity development, types of content, shared purpose, and brand voice.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied toward graduation requirements.

title: Teaching Writers in English Language Arts Classrooms
description: Explore approaches to writing instruction that focus on student inquiry and writing processes. Develop strategies for planning and implementing writing instruction in secondary English language arts classrooms.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Communicating Complex Ideas
description: A study of approaches to communicate complex ideas clearly for those considering a career in technical communication or professionals in other fields who must communicate complex ideas to audiences outside their field. Topics include career exploration, instructional writing, information design, visual design, and rhetorical strategies.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)

title: Consulting with Writers
description: Preparation for consulting with writers in the Boise State Writing Center. Emphasis on the theoretical foundations of writing center work, including understanding writing as a cognitive and social act, managing interpersonal dynamics, responding to writing-in-progress, and employing rhetorical grammar principles
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Writing Persuasively
description: Explores rhetorical roots of persuasion, with an emphasis on the relationship between writers and their audiences and the understanding of power dynamics and other ethical considerations. Analyze, write, and workshop persuasive texts.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Permission of instructor

title: Leadership Development for Writers
description: Learn to develop, lead, and evaluate effective team writing projects in organizational settings, including community-based, international, remote, and hybrid collaborations. Acquire and practice the leadership knowledge and skills to build writing teams, set direction for work efforts, advocate for resources, and communicate organizational values, while creating and maintaining inclusive, ethical, and effective teams.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Proposal Development
description: Study of principles of effective proposal development and grant writing for a variety of business and nonprofit contexts. Focus on identifying viable funding sources, researching and assessing requests/calls for proposals, and developing effective proposals.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)
AE302 - Entrepreneurship and Innovation in the Creative Sector (3)

title: Topics in Professional and Public Writing
description: Explores current topics, developments, and issues related to professional and public writing. May be repeated for a total of six credits.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Grammar, Style, and Writing
description: Explores grammar, structure, and style through classical and modern rhetorical texts and student writing. Students compose and revise their own academic and creative work. Workshop format.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Teaching Readers of Literary Texts in English Language Arts Classrooms
description: Explore approaches to reading instruction that promote student dialogue around texts and include reading young adult literature. Develop strategies for planning and implementing reading instruction in secondary English language arts classrooms.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Writing in Communities
description: Centers on how writing circulates by connecting the study of professional and public writing with opportunities to write with/in local communities. Includes advanced practice in field research, consultation and collaboration with writers, and production of audience-informed texts.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
WRITE201 - Writing for Public Audiences (3)

title: Assessing Readers and Writers in Secondary Classrooms
description: Inquire into current topics and trends in reading and writing assessment in secondary English language arts classrooms. Critically examine, develop, and practice using tools and methods for assessing student learning.

title: Editing for Clear Communication
description: An introduction to editing workplace documents so that audiences can understand them easily and use them efficiently. Topics include copyediting, comprehensive editing, proofreading, principles of plain language, working with authors, and preparing documents for publication.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)

title: Writing for Nonprofits and Social Media
description: Study of content strategy for nonprofits and how to connect with those who believe in their purpose. Analyze an organization’s values, identify specific goals, craft compelling stories, create posts that align with those values, and select the right social media channels to distribute content to help organizations achieve their mission.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)
SPS301 - Leadership in Public Service (3)
UX362 - Asking and Listening (1)
AE302 - Entrepreneurship and Innovation in the Creative Sector (3)

title: User-Centered Design Principles
description: Places the user at the forefront of each stage of the problem-solving process. Apply user research to design artifacts that enhance the user's experience including developing and conducting usability tests. Topics include persona development, prototyping, design thinking, and accessibility.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)
UX362 - Asking and Listening (1)

title: Integrating the English Language Arts in Curriculum and Instruction
description: Create instructional plans that facilitate inclusive and dialogic classroom communities and integrate the English language arts: reading, writing, listening, speaking, viewing, and representing.

title: Capstone in Professional and Public Writing (FF)
description: Extensive revision of written work in professional and public writing courses and creation of a portfolio. Reflection on personal experiences, professional development, and transferable skills.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of senior

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: English Language Arts Student Teacher Inquiry Community (FF)
description: With a community of colleagues, reflect on classroom instruction and inquire into interactions with learners in secondary English language arts classrooms. Prepare materials and professional story for your post-graduation goals. This course supports teacher candidates’ classroom teaching during the student teaching semester.

title: Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy
description: The evolutionary development of vertebrate anatomy, fishes through mammals with a focus on form and function. Dissection of the shark, salamander, and cat plus demonstrations of other vertebrate types.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)

title: Entomology
description: The general anatomy, physiology and developmental biology of insects, and ecological and evolutionary relationships and interactions of insects with humans. Field trips to collect and identify local species.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: Morphology, taxonomy, and natural history of the marine invertebrate animals and terrestrial arthropods exclusive of the insects.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)

title: Ornithology
description: Birds as examples of biological principles: classification, identification, ecology, behavior, life histories, distribution, and adaptations of birds. Two weekend field trips.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL304 - Biology III: Foundations of Ecology and Evolution (4)
BIOL323 - Ecology (4)

title: Vertebrate Natural History
description: Classification, identification, evolution, ecological relationships, behavior, and life histories of fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. Two weekend field trips.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)

title: Vertebrate Histology
description: Microscopic anatomy of cells, tissues, and organ systems of vertebrates. Major emphasis will be on mammalian systems.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL320 - Cell Biology (3)
ZOOL301 - Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy (4)

title: Human Physiology (FF)
description: Functional aspects of human tissues and organ systems with emphasis on regulatory and homeostatic mechanisms. Must be taken with ZOOL 411 to fulfill Finishing Foundations requirement.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL310 - Genetics (3)
BIOL320 - Cell Biology (3)

title: Human Endocrinology
description: Physiology, molecular biology, and clinical aspects of the human endocrine system, with focus on the role of the hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, gonads, pancreas, and skeleton.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BIOL320 - Cell Biology (3)

title: Head and Neck Anatomy
description: Use of human cadavers to study prosections of head and neck with emphasis on clinical relevance. Integument, osteology, myology, circulatory systems, lymphatics, oral and dental tissues, neuroanatomy, cranial nerves, general innervation, and salivary glands. Cross-listed with KINES 403, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
BIOL191 - Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology (FN) (4)
BIOL192 - Biology II: Introduction to the Diversity of Life (4)
Completed the following: 
BIOL227 - Human Anatomy and Physiology I (FN) (4)
BIOL228 - Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4)

title: Animal Physiology and Nutrition (FF)
description: Physiological principles common to all forms of animal life with a focus on nutrition are discussed. Physiological adaptations required to live in a variety of environments and deal with a diversity of diets are presented.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL304 - Biology III: Foundations of Ecology and Evolution (4)
BIOL323 - Ecology (4)

title: Human Physiology Laboratory (FF)
description: Laboratory for ZOOL 401 Human Physiology. Methods in the functional aspects of human tissues and organ systems with emphasis on regulatory and homeostatic mechanisms.

title: Mammalogy
description: The biology of mammals: ecology, life histories, reproduction, classification, identification, distribution, and adaptations. One weekend field trip.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL304 - Biology III: Foundations of Ecology and Evolution (4)
BIOL323 - Ecology (4)

title: Aquatic Entomology
description: The taxonomy and ecology of the insects most commonly encountered in freshwater environments. Emphasis on identification and biology of individual taxa, aquatic insect community ecology, environmental pollution assessment, and natural resource management.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL304 - Biology III: Foundations of Ecology and Evolution (4)
BIOL323 - Ecology (4)

title: Animal Behavior
description: Focuses on the concepts and processes of animal behavior, with particular emphasis on proximate perspectives. The history of the study of animal behavior, behavioral genetics, the nervous system and behavior, hormones and behavior, ontogeny of behavior, learning and motivation, and other aspects of behavior such as migration, orientation, and navigation will be presented.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BIOL304 - Biology III: Foundations of Ecology and Evolution (4)
BIOL323 - Ecology (4)

description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. title: Yoga II
description: Basic poses will be refined, with emphasis on all standing poses. Inverted poses (head stand, plow, shoulder stand) will be introduced, as well as a more in-depth exploration of restorative yoga. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BRNCOFIT166 - Yoga: The Iyengar Method I (1)

description: Instruction and participation in tennis for development of intermediate skills and techniques. Students furnish rackets. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:

BRNCOFIT173 - Tennis I (1)

title: Basketball II
description: Instruction and participation in basketball for development of intermediate skills and techniques. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites:

BRNCOFIT181 - Basketball I (1)

title: Volleyball II
description: Instruction and participation in volleyball for development of intermediate skills and techniques. May be repeated for credit.

BRNCOFIT186 - Volleyball I (1)

title: Spirit Squad
description: This course is for NCAA student-athletes who are currently participating in a Boise State Department of Intercollegiate Athletics-sponsored sport. Coach's approval is required. May be repeated for credit.

title: Varsity Sports
description: Instruction and participation in BSU Department of Athletics-approved sports. Coach's approval required. May be repeated for credit.

title: Business for the New Generation
description: Acquaints students with business organizations, functional areas, and current business issues such as ethics, social responsibility and sustainability. Presents the strengths and limitations of the business enterprise, while promoting innovation, creativity, and technology use in a global context. Emphasis on strategy, communication, problem solving, teams and negotiation. Format will be lecture plus interactive breakout groups, simulations and integrated cases.

title: Living Learning Community: Business and Economics
description: Required course for students residing in the University Housing Business and Economics Living Learning Community. Students learn about the campus and community resources, explore various business-related professions, are civically engaged, and participate in service projects. May be repeated for credit.
prerequisites: Admitted to Living Learning Community

title: The Legal Environment of Business
description: Emphasis will be on both the external and internal legal environment of a business organization. Topics will include the nature and function of the legal process, administrative regulations, the interaction of business with the judicial, legislative, and executive branches of government, and the legal responsibilities of business.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Residential College: Business and Economics
description: Required course for students residing in the University Housing Business and Economics Residential College. Students learn about the campus and community resources, explore various business-related professions, are civically engaged, and participate in service projects. May be repeated for credit.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Organizational Behavior
description: The basic concepts, theories, and research findings needed to understand human behavior within work organizations. Focus on principles, practices, and interpersonal skills that provide individual, group, and organizational effectiveness. Topics include: individual differences, decision making, motivation, communication, groups/teams, leadership, and conflict and negotiation.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of junior or higher
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BUSCOM201 - Business Communication (3)
WRITE212 - Introduction to Technical Communication (3)
CMGT201 - Construction Communications (3)

title: Commercial Law
description: This course provides an in-depth study of the legal principles relating to commercial transactions. Special emphasis will be placed on the following areas of law: agency, contracts, sales, commercial paper, secured transactions, and bankruptcy.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
BUS202 - The Legal Environment of Business (3)
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission, or Construction Management BS

title: Law for Accountants
description: Builds on BUS 202 by covering the additional business law topics accountants need to know to succeed. Covers contracts, sales, bankruptcy and property law, agency, partnerships and corporations, secured transactions, estates and trusts, and the legal responsibility of accountants.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
BUS202 - The Legal Environment of Business (3)

title: International Management
description: The course addresses issues of managing multinational corporations, both American firms overseas and non-American firms in the U.S. Specifically, the course provides insights into structure, human resource management practices, managing motivation, communication, staffing and related issues.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
BUS301 - Organizational Behavior (3)

title: Advanced Management Topics
description: An advanced study of a major topic in management. Example topics: Self-management, motivation and work, management of technology, e-commerce, organizational theory and organizational change.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: College of Business and Economics Admission, Radiologic Sciences BS, Health Studies BS, or Construction Management BS
Completed the following: 
BUS301 - Organizational Behavior (3)

title: Managerial Problem Solving
description: The complex issues facing today's organizations require managers to critically analyze the environment and apply various problem solving techniques and tools to navigate challenging situations. This course is intended to provide students with a broad range of tools and practices for solving problems to help them develop the competence and skills necessary to become effective managers. To accomplish these goals, this course will cover major theoretical concepts in problem solving and decision-making, and leverage a variety of practical tools managers commonly use to solve problems.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Business in Society: Ethics, Responsibility and Sustainability
description: Intensive exploration of the role of business in a global society, including ethical decision-making, business responsibility in social and environmental contexts and best practices in sustainability. Recommended completion of BUS 302.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
BUS202 - The Legal Environment of Business (3)
HIST330 - Human Rights Past and Present (3)

title: Business Policies (FF)
description: To develop analytical, problem-solving, and decision-making skills in situations dealing with complex organizations, with the ultimate objective of formulating policies and strategies, both domestic and worldwide. To build upon and integrate the knowledge and methods acquired to examine all functional areas of the organization.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to College of Business and Economics Admission
Completed the following: 
BUS301 - Organizational Behavior (3)
FINAN303 - Principles of Finance (3)
MKTG301 - Principles of Marketing (3)
SCM301 - Principles of Supply Chain Management (3)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 internship credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Recommended admission to COBE.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Business Foundations
description: Utilizes a business plan development model to study the interrelationships among business functional areas and provide an understanding of how businesses create value. Provides skills for successful team management and business communication, including an understanding of the language of business. Introduces the principles of responsible business practices. Considers the economic, legal, and social environments in which business operates.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Admitted to any non-COBE major
Take any Foundations of Math (FM) course
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Understanding Business Value
description: Examines the tools and concepts required to make value-added financial decisions. Emphasis on interpretation and analysis of financial reports and data. Topics include financial statement analysis, budgeting, cash flow, time value of money, and capital investment decisions. Introduces financial spreadsheet tools.

title: Creating Value with People
description: Develops the competencies required to lead and manage people in a variety of contexts. Topics include organizational behavior, team building, conflict management, motivation, negotiation, career development strategies, and ethical decision making.

title: Creating Value for Customers
description: Provides an understanding of how products and services are efficiently developed, sold, and delivered to create value for customers and business. Topics include market analysis, consumer behavior, market segmentation, personal sales, distribution strategies, project management, and supply chain management.

title: Creating Value Through Investment
description: Emphasis on the use of accounting, financial and economic data to measure and manage value creation. Examines valuation methods for financial instruments, measuring the cost of financing, and sources of funds for business. The methods for evaluating and accounting for the profitability of potential business investments, including applied spreadsheet modeling.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BUSBTC301 - Business Foundations (3)
BUSBTC302 - Understanding Business Value (3)

title: Business in Action
description: Uses the business plan development model introduced in BUSBTC 301 to integrate the concepts and practices developed in earlier courses. Emphasis on problem solving and value creation in a sector or service of interest to student teams. Includes the further development of professional skills such as time management, career management, interpersonal relationships, and leadership. Includes team-based experiential learning.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
BUSBTC310 - Creating Value with People (3)

title: Intercultural Business Language Development
description: Multilingual and native English-speaking students will develop their proficiency in business-related English and view various business communication genres through a cultural lens.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL123 - Academic English Writing for Speakers of Other Languages Level III (3)

title: Business Communication
description: Survey course covers effective communication skills, critical thinking, and logical argument development needed in the business environment. Course content integrates technology with a focus on written, oral, and interpersonal forms of communication.
prerequisites:

description: Provides analytic methods applied to case studies utilized by operations, marketing, finance, strategic planning, and other functions relying on ANOVA, simple linear and multiple regression, chi-square, and other business analytics methods to address business problems.

title: Investigating Canada: A Preliminary Survey
description: Examines the development of a Canadian national identity and role in the world. An interdisciplinary approach will be used with comparison to the United States.

title: Controversial Issues in Contemporary Canada
description: Analyzes a range of controversial issues in contemporary Canada. Topics will vary but may include relations with the United States, immigration and multiculturalism, oil resources and environment, marijuana use, health care, and issues related to Quebec and Indigenous peoples.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Concepts of Chemistry (FN)
description: Acquaint students with chemistry and its relationship to other fields of study and modern life. Students who have received credit for CHEM 102 or CHEM 112 may not receive credit for CHEM 100.

title: Introduction to Chemistry (FN)
description: First semester of a sequence course designed primarily for health science majors or students who need an introductory chemistry course prior to taking CHEM 111. Basic concepts of inorganic and physical chemistry are covered.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH108 - Intermediate Algebra (3)
MATH133 - Elementary Models with Functions (FM) (3)
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH149 - Precalculus: Function for Business (FM) (3)

description: Lab to accompany CHEM 101.

description: Continuation of CHEM 101 to include basic concepts of organic and biochemistry.

CHEM101 - Introduction to Chemistry (FN) (3)

title: Essentials of Organic and Biochemistry Laboratory (FN)
description: Lab to accompany CHEM 102.

title: General Chemistry I (FN)
description: The first semester of a one-year sequence course. A thorough study of the fundamentals of chemistry, including atomic and molecular structure, stoichiometry, chemical reactions in solutions, gases, thermochemistry, basic quantum theory, chemical periodicity, and elementary chemical bonding. CHEM 111 assumes that students without one year of high school chemistry have completed a semester preparative course (see CHEM 100).
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH144 - Precalculus II: Trigonometry (2)
Or successful completion of the CHEM 111 Math exam

title: General Chemistry I Laboratory (FN)
description: Lab to accompany CHEM 111.

title: General Chemistry II
description: A continuation of CHEM 111 to include intermolecular forces, thermodynamics, chemical kinetics, chemical equilibrium in solution, acids and bases, oxidation-reduction, electrochemistry, and complex ions.

Completed the following: 
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)
CHEM111L - General Chemistry I Laboratory (FN) (1)

MATH143 - College Algebra (FM) (3)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)
MATH144 - Precalculus II: Trigonometry (2)

description: Lab to accompany CHEM 112.

description: Study of the equilibrium relationships and methods used in gravimetric, volumetric, and some instrumental analysis.

title: Analytical Chemistry I Laboratory
description: Practical application of analytical techniques through analysis of unknown samples using gravimetric, volumetric, and instrumental methods.

title: Undergraduate Research Experience
description: Provides insight into research or creative work through inquiry, investigation, discovery, and application. There is a possibility of collaborating with graduate students who are performing graduate-level research. All research is supervised by a faculty member. May be repeated for credit. Either graded or pass/fail. It is recommended that the 0 credit option be offered as pass/fail.

title: Directed Reading in Chemistry
description: An individual study of a topic in chemistry arranged by the student in conjunction with a supervising member of the chemistry faculty. May be repeated for credit.

title: History of Chemistry: Prehistoric to 1600
description: Origins of chemistry from alchemy to modern chemistry in the Arab, Chinese, Hindu, and western world. Includes early writers and Iatrochemistry.

title: History of Chemistry: 1600 to Present
description: Chemistry from 1600 to the present. Includes the major figures and the major chemical theories of the period.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. title: Research in Chemistry
description: An individual laboratory research project in chemistry arranged by the student in conjunction with a supervising member of the chemistry faculty. May be repeated for credit.

title: Survey of Organic Chemistry
description: For students expecting to take only one semester of organic chemistry. An overview of organic chemistry covering the fundamental principles of nomenclature, reactions, synthesis, mechanisms, stereochemistry, spectroscopy, lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates.

Completed the following: 
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)
CHEM112L - General Chemistry II Laboratory (1)

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: For students expecting to take two semesters of organic chemistry. More in-depth treatment of structure and bonding in organic molecules, mechanisms of organic reactions, chemical transformations of some of the functional groups of organic chemistry, synthesis, and determination of chemical structures.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)
CHEM112L - General Chemistry II Laboratory (1)

title: Organic Chemistry I Laboratory
description: Lab to accompany CHEM 301 and CHEM 307. Introduction to organic laboratory techniques, spectroscopic methods and organic syntheses. One three-hour laboratory and one hour of recitation per week.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: A continuation of CHEM 307, covering additional functional groups and advanced topics in organic chemistry.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM307 - Organic Chemistry I (3)

title: Organic Chemistry II Laboratory
description: Lab to accompany CHEM 309. More advanced organic laboratory techniques, syntheses, organic qualitative analysis, spectroscopic methods, and an introduction to molecular modeling. Three hours of laboratory and one hour of recitation per week.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM308 - Organic Chemistry I Laboratory (2)

title: Physical Chemistry I Lecture
description: The first semester of a one-year sequence course. Comprehensive study of the theoretical aspects of physical- chemical phenomena. Emphasis is placed on classical and statistical thermodynamics, kinetics, symmetry, spectroscopy, and quantum chemistry.

description: Methods of physicochemical measurement, introduction to computerized data analysis, technical report writing, and the use of the chemical literature. description: A course designed for non-majors who need one semester of biochemistry to satisfy program or professional school requirements. An overview of the biochemical principles governing the properties and activities of biologically relevant molecules: nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins. The emphasis will be on biomolecule structure and function as they relate to human metabolism and disease.

description: Professional development to prepare students for career success.  Prepares students for pursuit of jobs and entrance into graduate or profession programs.  Also includes training in important areas such as scientific ethics and library research.

CHEM111 - General Chemistry I (FN) (3)

title: Geomatics and Geospatial Data
description: Introduction of plane surveying systems including control points, coordinate systems, distances, angles, areas, volumes, and leveling. Other topics include introduction to GPS, photogrammetry, LiDAR, remote sensing, and other modern mapping and surveying tools.

title: Civil Engineering Case Studies
description: Review of projects, historical and ongoing, from various aspects of civil engineering.

title: Civil Engineering Laboratory
description: Laboratory exercises, presentations, and field trips to investigate different aspects of civil engineering.

title: Civil Engineering Computational Methods
description: Introduction to programming and computational methods in civil engineering.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH175 - Calculus II (4)

title: Introduction to AutoCAD for Civil Engineers
description: Introduction to engineering drafting and computer-aided design for civil engineering applications. The course covers mechanics and standards used to create and interpret engineering drawings made by hand and with AutoCAD.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Technical Communication for Civil Engineers
description: Explore writing conventions for civil engineers using content from co-requisite laboratory course. Draft, revise, and edit civil engineering documents for specific audiences, focusing on effective textual and visual communication. Topics include analyzing audience needs, effective engineering style, communicating data, and creating technical graphics.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Advanced Surveying
description: A continuation of CE 210 including mapping, state plane coordinate systems, title searches and an introduction to GIS.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CMGT210 - Construction Surveying and Layout (2)
CMGT211 - Construction Surveying and Layout Lab (1)

title: Hydrology
description: Interdisciplinary earth science concerned with movement and occurrence of water. Watershed based hydrologic phenomena including hydrologic water-cycle analysis, precipitation, evapotranspiration, snow/snowmelt, streamflow, floods, routing and surface runoff events. Application of analytical techniques to solve water resource problems. Cross-listed with GEOS 316, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
GEOS212 - Water in the West (4)
MATH175 - Calculus II (4)

title: Principles of Environmental Engineering
description: Environmental consideration in water management, water quality, wastewater generation, and air pollution. Design of water and wastewater treatment systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE220 - Coupled Environmental Processes (2)

title: Environmental and Fluids Laboratory
description: Introduction to water-based environmental systems. Properties of water and basic fluid flow experiments. Assessment of environmental quality parameters.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
MATH360 - Engineering Statistics (3)
MATH361 - Probability and Statistics I (3)

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Physical properties of fluids, fluid mechanics, measurements, viscous flow, turbulent flow, momentum, lift, drag, boundary layer effects, pipe flow, and open channel flow.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)

title: Principles of Hydrology and Hydraulic Systems
description: Principles of the hydrologic cycle and water flows in open channels. Emphasis on the physics of free-surface flow and includes open channel flow design principles, uniform flow, gradually varied flow, steady and unsteady flows, mechanics of sediment transport. Includes computer aided analysis and design.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)
CE284 - Civil Engineering Computational Methods (2)

title: Principles of Civil Engineering Materials
description: Physical and engineering properties, behavior, design, and utilization of various civil engineering construction materials.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CE350 - Mechanics of Materials (3)

title: Civil Engineering Materials Laboratory
description: Characterization and evaluation of soils and materials used in civil engineering.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
MATH254 - Statistical Methods (FM) (3)
MATH360 - Engineering Statistics (3)
MATH361 - Probability and Statistics I (3)

title: Mechanics of Materials
description: Principles of stress, strain, and deformation applied to the analysis of engineering structures including beams, shafts, and columns.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGR210 - Engineering Mechanics I (3)
MATH175 - Calculus II (4)

title: Codes and Official Documents
description: Survey of codes and related works influencing the design and construction of projects. Requirements generated by the IBC, ASCE-7, and the Americans with Disabilities Act. Determination of structural loads, resolution of conflicts among governing codes, and interpretation of documents.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of junior or higher

title: Principles of Structural Engineering
description: Analysis and design of statically determinate and indeterminate structures, under static or moving loads, using classical methods. Equilibrium, stress-strain relations, and compatibility.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE350 - Mechanics of Materials (3)

title: Structures II
description: Analysis and design of structural systems. Stiffness method including the development of element properties, coordinate transformations, and global analysis theory. Three-dimensional building systems and an introduction to the Finite Element Method.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)

title: Principles of Geotechnical Engineering
description: Descriptive terminology, physical and engineering properties, measurement techniques, and behavior of soils.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE350 - Mechanics of Materials (3)

title: Principles of Transportation Engineering
description: Planning, design, and operations of multi-modal transportation systems.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE270 - Geomatics and Geospatial Data (2)
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)

title: Computational Techniques
description: Introduction of numerical methods to solve Civil Engineering problems with emphasis on Geotechnical Engineering problems. In-depth treatment of finite difference and integrated finite difference. Brief introduction to finite element methods and programming using MATLAB.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CE360 - Principles of Geotechnical Engineering (3)
MATH333 - Differential Equations with Matrix Theory (4)
Must have knowledge of programming

title: Engineering Hydrology
description: Integrated approach to hydrology, using the hydrologic/system or control volume as a mechanism for analyzing hydrologic problems and hydrologic processes' water cycle, atmospheric water, surface and subsurface water, hydrologic analysis and design, design storms and peak flow and design flow estimation; hydrologic design methods; snowmelt runoff and evapotranspiration.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)
MATH275 - Multivariable and Vector Calculus (4)

title: Hydrologic Systems: Groundwater
description: Introduction to the hydrologic cycle focusing on subsurface water and its relationships to surface water. Physics of flow through porous media, physical properties of aquifer systems, methods to determine aquifer characteristics, groundwater modeling and relationships between groundwater and streamflow. May be taken for CE or GEOS credit, but not both.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH175 - Calculus II (4)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
GEOS212 - Water in the West (4)
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)

title: Environmental Process Chemistry
description: Chemical principles of water and wastewater treatment processes and reactions in receiving waters. Topics include chemical thermodynamics, reaction kinetics, acid-base equilibra, mineral precipitation/dissolution, and electrochemistry.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE320 - Principles of Environmental Engineering (3)

title: Hazardous Waste Engineering
description: Physical, chemical, and biological treatment of hazardous wastes. Consideration of legal and political issues.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)

title: Air Pollution Control Engineering
description: This course surveys the sources, fates, effects and control of air pollutants. Industrial, agricultural, and municipal contributions to acid rain, smog, and toxic air pollutants in fish and humans are covered. Students will demonstrate skill in the use of mathematical and computer predictions for the fate of air pollutants in the design of air pollution control systems and be able to communicate engineering concepts in oral presentations and in writing.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE320 - Principles of Environmental Engineering (3)

title: Water Treatment Design
description: Theoretical and practical engineering aspects of advanced chemical and physical phenomena and processes applicable to the design for removal of impurities from ground and surface water sources, including experimental problem analysis, conveyance systems and optimal treatment solution reporting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE320 - Principles of Environmental Engineering (3)

title: Wastewater Treatment Design
description: Theoretical and practical engineering aspects of advanced chemical, physical and biological phenomena and processes applicable to the design for removal of impurities from wastewater and industrial wastes and to their transformation in receiving waters, including experimental problem analysis, collection system conveyance and optimal treatment solution reporting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE320 - Principles of Environmental Engineering (3)

title: Aqueous Geochemistry
description: Basic tools and topics of aqueous geochemistry with an emphasis on low temperature process in natural waters Essentials of thermodynamics, kinetics, aqueous speciation, mineral-water interaction, and elemental cycling in the context of surficial earth processes and environmental challenges. Completion of or co-enrollment in Math 175 is recommended. Cross-listed with GEOS 426, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CHEM112 - General Chemistry II (3)
MATH170 - Calculus I (FM) (4)

title: Hydraulics
description: Applied principles of fluid mechanics, pipe flow, open channel flow, flow nets, and hydraulic machinery. Design.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)

title: GIS in Water Resources
description: Applications of geographic information systems (GIS) in pre- and post-processing of model inputs and outputs, digital elevation models, flow direction and flow accumulation, spatial analysis and interpretation, Model builder, data model, tools, functionality and examples of real-world water and natural resource problems and integration of external models (e.g., SWAT).
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
GEOG360 - Introduction to Geographic Information Systems (3)
CE316 - Hydrology (4)

title: Water Resources Engineering
description: Flood frequency analysis, reservoir characteristics and design, open channel flow applications, probability, risk and uncertainty analysis, water project design, model studies, water resources planning and management, and system analysis.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE330 - Fluid Mechanics (3)

title: Pavement Analysis and Design
description: Pavement design processes, materials selection and characterization methods, analysis and design of flexible pavements, analysis and design of rigid concrete pavements, pavement condition survey and ratings, distress evaluation and maintenance and rehabilitation techniques.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE340 - Principles of Civil Engineering Materials (3)

title: Microstructure, Properties, and Performance of Concrete
description: Basic properties of cements and mineral aggregates and their interactions in concrete from a microstructural perspective. Special emphasis on: properties of hydrated products and hardened concrete; modifications through admixtures; production, handling, and placement problems; specifications; quality control and acceptance testing; lightweight, heavyweight, and other special concrete mixtures. Project topics will include design and testing of advanced concrete concepts for durable, sustainable, and resilient infrastructure.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CE340 - Principles of Civil Engineering Materials (3)
MSE101 - Introduction to Materials Engineering (FN) (3)

title: Reinforced Concrete Design
description: Design of reinforced concrete structures, such as beams, columns, one way slabs, and simple footings, in accordance with latest ACI Code for Reinforced Concrete.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)

title: Structural Steel Design
description: Design of steel structures, such as beams and columns, in accordance with latest AISC Manual of Steel Construction, LRFD edition.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)

title: Timber Design
description: Design of wood, and wood composite, structures and systems based on mechanical and structural characteristics and specifications.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)

title: Masonry Design
description: Design of masonry structures and systems based on mechanical and structural characteristics and specifications.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)

title: Geotechnical Engineering Design
description: Subsoil exploration and site investigation methodologies. Soil mechanics in design of earth retaining structures, shallow and deep foundations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE360 - Principles of Geotechnical Engineering (3)

title: Geotechnical Engineering Design II
description: Application of soil mechanics in the design of embankments, slopes, and excavations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE360 - Principles of Geotechnical Engineering (3)

title: Ground Improvement Design
description: Introduction to ground improvement techniques for various problematic soils necessitated by the growing demand for construction in challenging geologies. Emphasis on understanding methods available to strengthen existing ground as alternative to costly foundation designs. Exploration of pros and cons of multiple ground improvement techniques, under four, broad categories: mechanical, hydraulic, chemical/thermal, and physical. Design and implement the most appropriate ground improvement technique for a given geological condition.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE360 - Principles of Geotechnical Engineering (3)

title: Highway Systems Design
description: Design of urban and rural highway systems. Use of software is required.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE370 - Principles of Transportation Engineering (3)

title: Transportation Planning
description: Theory and practice of transportation planning at the metropolitan as well as regional levels. Use of software is required. Recent advances in transportation planning will be introduced.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE370 - Principles of Transportation Engineering (3)

title: Traffic Systems Design
description: The course covers the design of operations, control, and management of traffic systems. Use of software is required.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE370 - Principles of Transportation Engineering (3)

title: Capstone Preparation and Professional Readiness
description: First course in the capstone course sequence. Initial capstone project development including planning for integration of previous coursework, team formation, and project impact assessment. Other topics include engineering ethics, professional communication skills, and professional transition preparation.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE280 - Civil Engineering Case Studies (2)
CE320 - Principles of Environmental Engineering (3)
CE332 - Principles of Hydrology and Hydraulic Systems (3)
CE340 - Principles of Civil Engineering Materials (3)
CE352 - Principles of Structural Engineering (3)
CE360 - Principles of Geotechnical Engineering (3)
CE370 - Principles of Transportation Engineering (3)
ENGR360 - Engineering Economy (3)

title: Capstone Design (FF)
description: Second course in the capstone course sequence. Major design experience integrating previous course work with appropriate engineering standards and multiple constraints. Applied through a comprehensive group project, integrating multiple criteria.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CE481 - Capstone Preparation and Professional Readiness (1)

title: Review of Civil Engineering
description: Review of basic engineering and science material covered in civil engineering curriculum.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Work U
description: Designed to provide students with professional experiences, regardless of their major. Students will develop their professional network alongside a mentor at a Treasure Valley employer. Time spent on site with employers and in a weekly class session. The class sessions are designed to unpack what the students are learning on the job, attend interactive workshops, engage with dynamic guest leaders from partner employers, and learn skills that translate directly to a professional setting. No more than 12 credits of Work U and/or internship may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Civil Engineering Engagement
description: Exploration of current civil engineering topics. Course may be repeated for credit, topics are not repeatable.

title: Intro to Cloud Computing
description: Detailed overview of cloud concepts including but not limited to: core services, security, architecture, pricing, and support. Learners are prepared to pursue optional and relevant industry certifications. Does not require any prior background or coursework in programming or computing.

title: Applied Personal Finance
description: Develop healthy and positive mindset around managing finances to improve quality of life. Understand and apply financial skills including credit, taxes, insurance, retirement funds, increasing income through investment and entrepreneurship, budgeting, and paying off debt. Series of projects culminate in a fully-developed personal financial plan.

title: Project Management I: Start, Plan, Run
description: Learn foundational project management terminology and gain a deeper understanding of the role and responsibilities of a project manager. Create effective project documentation and artifacts throughout the various phases of a project. Develop an immersive understanding of the practices and processes used by project managers to lead, plan, and implement critical projects to help their organizations succeed.

title: Project Management II: Apply, Execute, Deliver
description: Learn the foundations of Agile project management, with a focus on implementing Scrum events, building Scrum artifacts, and understanding Scrum roles. Practice strategic communication, problem-solving, and stakeholder management through applied scenarios. Produce capstone deliverables that may also serve as learner portfolio assets.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
COID264 - Project Management I: Start, Plan, Run (3)

title: IT Support I: IT Support and Computer Networking
description: Learn the fundamentals of day-to-day IT support, including identifying problems, troubleshooting, and debugging. Learn networking basics, including protocols, cloud computing, and practical applications of network troubleshooting.

title: IT Support II: IT System Administration, Infrastructure, and Security
description: Learn the basics of operating systems, system administration, and IT infrastructure services necessary to manage a fleet of devices and software on premises and in the cloud. Learn IT security concepts, tools, and best practices to address threats and attacks. Produce capstone deliverables that may also serve as professional portfolio assets.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
COID266 - IT Support I: IT Support and Computer Networking (3)

title: Data Analysis I: Prepare, Process, Analyze
description: Learn the foundational skills necessary to source, prepare, process, and analyze data, including how to clean and organize data for analysis and complete analysis and calculations using spreadsheets and SQL. Develop an immersive understanding of the practices and processes used by data analysts to help organizations gain insights from their data.

title: Data Analysis II: Visualize, Share, Act
description: Learn the foundational skills necessary to present the findings of their analyses, including how to conduct a complete analysis, share insights using tools such as spreadsheets, SQL, R programming, and data visualization. Create and publish a data analysis case study. Produce capstone deliverables that may also serve as professional portfolio assets.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
COID268 - Data Analysis I: Prepare, Process, Analyze (3)

title: Design Thinking: Creative Problem Solving
description: Learn to work better in teams, apply new thinking to solve challenges, and uncover your creative capacity using Design Thinking as a framework for gaining insights about people's needs. Unleash your creative confidence and make a positive impact in your community by collaborating with a local purpose-driven organization for a team-based design challenge.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Introduction to Digital Innovation and Design
description: Explore academic and career goal-setting strategies, innovative thinking frameworks, the connection of theory to practice, academic culture, academic integrity, the vital nature of an academic support community, and the qualities of a reflective and adaptive learner.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Applications of AI
description: This course introduces students to the application of AI in the workplace. Using a projects format, students will utilize a variety of emerging AI tools, inclusive of text and image generators, to create content and products, develop productivity skills, and explore the limitations and opportunities of these emerging technologies.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher

title: Creative Concepting
description: Using human-centered problem-solving methods, learn to hack your brain to achieve new levels of creativity. Use professionally proven creative processes and tools through immersive projects that relate to real-world challenges. Self-select challenge topics, conduct empathy interviews, design and prototype creative messaging, innovative new products, and viable business ideas. Gain confidence in sharing emerging ideas with others. Practice giving and receiving actionable feedback to help move beyond good in pursuit of excellence and to deliver value to the end user.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher
Declared or admitted to Digital Innovation and Design BA online

title: Growth Hacking
description: Learn innovative techniques to reach and engage customers at scale through a variety of digital channels to spur growth for developed concepts. Create prototype companies at the beginning of the course and learn new techniques to build upon concepts. Skills learned include but are not limited to: web design, email marketing, social media, and digital advertising, while also earning digital platform certifications.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher
Declared or admitted to Digital Innovation and Design BA online

title: Analytics for Design
description: Develop an understanding of how to gather user needs, create journey maps, conduct user testing, turn data into insights, and produce user experiences that deliver results. By using industry-standard enterprise research and analytics tools, students will begin to understand how to collect data, visualize it, and most importantly, how to understand and apply it. The developed experiences will showcase how a user can seamlessly travel across physical and digital touchpoints to achieve their desired goal. Produced works may also serve as learner portfolio assets.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Must have a class standing of sophomore or higher
Declared or admitted to Digital Innovation and Design BA online

title: Emerging Technology
