How their construction was influenced by political, religious, economic, and other factors will also be examined. Basic construction techniques, both historical and modern, will be covered.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CMGT110 - Construction Materials and Methods (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Project Cost Controls
description: Theory of cost accounting and cost control, with emphasis on cost determination as a tool of management and project cost control. Includes bidding, budgeting, and developing project cost record-keeping system for managing cash, receivable, payroll, and subcontractors.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CMGT367 - Construction Estimating (3)

title: Construction Jobsite Management
description: Management of construction project jobsites including jobsite logistics, constructability analysis, quality assurance/quality control, SWPPP, formwork and other temporary structures.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CMGT367 - Construction Estimating (3)
CMGT310 - Statics and Mechanics of Materials for Building Construction (4)
CMGT326 - Construction Safety Management (2)

title: Land Development
description: Overview of the land development process, including planning, design, construction, and sale of various types of real estate. Topics include key concepts in successful development, feasibility studies, site selection and improvement, government policy and regulation, project planning and master planning, design of public infrastructure, and construction of site improvements.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Construction Project Management (FF)
description: Topics related to the procurement of work and the management of construction projects including business development and proposal preparation; contract, risk and change management; safety and quality management; jobsite layout and control; leadership and team building; and sustainability and ethics.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CMGT367 - Construction Estimating (3)
CMGT385 - Construction Contracts and Law (3)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher

title: Senior Outcome Assessment
description: A comprehensive review of professional construction management principles and technical skills in preparation for the AIC Level 1 Certified Professional Constructor Exam which students are required to take and pass.

title: Professional Skills for Construction Careers
description: Resume writing and interview skills for construction industry employers, professional phone and email communications, preparation and delivery of effective proposal presentations, how to prepare for and conduct effective meetings, business etiquette, and appropriate and ethical demeanor in client and subcontractor relationships.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CMGT120 - Introduction to Construction Management (3)
CMGT201 - Construction Communications (3)

title: Internship
description: Cooperative education/internship in construction management provides practical, on-the-job experience in blueprint reading, material takeoffs, estimating, equipment management, and project planning.

title: Independent Study
description: Construction studies as supervised by a construction faculty member.

title: Counseling in P-12 Schools
description: Prepares teacher candidates to work with school counselors and understand guidance and counseling issues in the schools. Topics may include the role of the school counselor, student mental health issues, and multiculturalism in the student population. Self-awareness and socio-emotional development in teacher preparation may also be addressed.

title: Depression
description: An overview of the symptoms and underlying causal factors associated with the range of depression-based disorders. Depression based problems are discussed in terms of the interactions between cognitive, behavioral, and affective factors and related treatments are presented.

title: Fears and Phobias
description: An overview of the symptoms and underlying causal factors associated with the range of anxiety-based problems. Anxiety based problems are discussed in terms of the interactions between cognitive, behavioral, and affective factors and related treatments are presented.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Writing Creative Nonfiction
description: Introduction to writing creative nonfiction. Close reading of creative nonfiction texts, creative nonfiction writing exercises, and discussion of student's original creative nonfiction in a workshop setting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Writing Poetry
description: Introduction to writing poetry. Close reading of poetry texts, poetry writing exercises, and discussion of student's original poetry in a workshop setting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Writing Fiction
description: Introduction to writing fiction. Close reading of fiction texts, fiction writing exercises, and discussion of student's original fiction in a workshop setting.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Fundamentals of Narrative
description: An introduction to the origins, evolution, theory, and practice of storytelling.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Writing Studio
description: Intensive one-on-one study of selected creative writing discipline with faculty mentor. May be repeated for credit. Must be taken twice in the Creative Writing BFA and Narrative Arts BFA degree programs.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGL102 - Writing and Rhetoric II (FW) (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Intermediate Creative Nonfiction Writing
description: Exploration of genres of creative nonfiction with an emphasis on contemporary writers. Students will write original creative nonfiction and discuss it in a workshop format. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CW201 - Writing Creative Nonfiction (3)
WRITE204 - Creative Nonfiction: Genres and Contexts (3)

title: Intermediate Poetry Writing
description: Exploration of poetic technique and the study of how poets read and learn from other poets. Students will write original poetry and discuss it in a workshop format. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CW202 - Writing Poetry (3)

title: Intermediate Fiction Writing
description: Exploration of narrative technique, dialogue form, and the short story. Students will write original fiction and discuss it in a workshop format. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CW203 - Writing Fiction (3)

title: Literary Translation
description: Exploration of the theory and practice of literary translation. Students will translate short works of literature and discuss them in a workshop format. Languages and genres translated vary with instructor. May be taken twice for credit.

title: Form and Theory of Creative Writing
description: An intensive study of aspects of craft in either fiction, poetry or creative non-fiction. Course will expose students to particular methods, approaches, and techniques in a genre and their aesthetic effects. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credits.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
Or permission of instructor

title: Advanced Creative Nonfiction Writing
description: Intensive work in writing and critiquing creative nonfiction. Students seeking graduate credit will produce a greater quantity and higher quality of original work, will have a separate and more extensive reading list, and will be expected to participate more fully in class activities. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CW301 - Intermediate Creative Nonfiction Writing (3)
WRITE304 - Writing Persuasively (3)

title: Advanced Poetry Writing
description: Intensive work in writing and critiquing poetry. Students seeking graduate credit will produce a greater quantity and higher quality of original work, will have a separate and more extensive reading list, and will be expected to participate more fully in class activities. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CW302 - Intermediate Poetry Writing (3)

title: Advanced Fiction Writing
description: Intensive work in writing and critiquing fiction. Students seeking graduate credit will produce a greater quantity and higher quality of original work, will have a separate and more extensive reading list, and will be expected to participate more fully in class activities. May be repeated once for credit.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CW303 - Intermediate Fiction Writing (3)

title: Literary Editing and Publishing
description: Study of literary magazines and/or small presses with the intention of preparing students to submit their own work for publication, as well as develop hands-on editorial skills and experience by assisting with The Idaho Review or Ahsahta Press.

title: Senior Portfolio in Creative Nonfiction (FF)
description: Extensive revision of previous written work in creative writing courses and creation of portfolio focused on creative nonfiction. Students will also learn about the publishing process and how to apply effectively for graduate programs in creative writing. Portfolio may include other documents to use in post-college writing careers, such as cover letters and agent queries. Recommended senior standing.

title: Senior Portfolio in Poetry (FF)
description: Extensive revision of previous written work in creative writing courses and creation of portfolio focused on poetry. Students will also learn about the publishing process and how to apply effectively for graduate programs in creative writing. Portfolio may include other documents to use in post-college writing careers, such as cover letters and agent queries. Recommended senior standing.

title: Senior Portfolio in Fiction (FF)
description: Extensive revision of previous written work in creative writing courses and creation of portfolio focused on fiction. Students will also learn about the publishing process and how to apply effectively for graduate programs in creative writing. Portfolio may include other documents to use in post-college writing careers, such as cover letters and agent queries. Recommended senior standing.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student’s major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice
description: Philosophy, history, objectives, and functions of the criminal justice system as a social institution. The relationship of this system to society; and a general overview of the administration of justice.

title: Introduction to Law and Justice (FS)
description: Examines issues of social justice; e.g., poverty, racism, sexism, alienation, and use of law for social control.

title: Introduction to Police
description: Examines the police function and role in society with an emphasis on history, human resources, common practices, administration challenges, and current issues.

title: Introduction to Courts and Sentencing
description: An introduction to U.S. Criminal Courts, including: sources of law, court structure and jurisdiction, courtroom actors and juries, and processes such as pretrial procedures, trials, and sentencing.

title: Introduction to Corrections
description: Explores the history, theory, practices, and research in community and institutional corrections. Criminal sentencing, the death penalty, and legal issues in corrections will also be discussed.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Careers in Criminal Justice
description: Personal and professional ethics and ethical decision making among criminal justice organizational agents and administrators are explored. Overview of criminal justice and related professions in the public and private sectors, regarding specific criteria, such as employment outlooks, procedures of obtaining positions, work conditions and responsibilities.

title: Organized Crime
description: Explores the history of international organized crime as well as the scope and forms that organized criminal activity itself takes. The range of law enforcement and political responses to organized crime in addition to criminological and sociological explanations for the prevalence of organized crime are examined in depth.

title: Drugs and Crime
description: Comprehensive overview of the nature and types of drug use and their effects, theories of drug use, drug related problems and issues, criminal justice involvement with drug offenders and drug policy, and the impact of the U.S. policy of drug prohibition on the criminal justice system (police, courts, and corrections).
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Victimology and Victimization
description: Provides an introduction to the concepts of victimology and the various forms of criminal victimization.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major or declared criminal justice minor

title: Criminal Justice in Popular Media
description: Examines how crime and the criminal justice system are portrayed in popular media and how this portrayal comports with reality. Popular media will be used as the basis for discussion.

title: Theories of Crime
description: Explores the biological, psychological, and sociological theories of crime and criminality. The range of available and realistic policy options for the criminal justice system and society are explored.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Juvenile Justice
description: Study of the philosophy and function of the juvenile court, court procedures and law, theories of causation, and intervention strategies for juveniles. Includes an evaluation and analysis of law, institutions, policies, and practices of the court since inception.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Criminal Law
description: Examines elements of crime and application of federal and state criminal statutes. Differences between various offenses and court procedures, such as defense to criminal liability, will also be discussed.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Leadership in Criminal Justice Organizations
description: Examines major challenges faced by leaders at various organizational levels in a variety of criminal justice organizations and identifies the core leadership competencies necessary for meeting these challenges
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ101 - Introduction to Criminal Justice (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Corrections in the Community
description: Explores the development, organization, operation, and efficacy of community-based correctional programs. Specific topics include supervised probation and parole, specialty courts, work release, halfway houses, diversion, furlough, and various community/social agency rehabilitative programs.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ204 - Introduction to Corrections (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Interviewing and Counseling in Criminal Justice
description: Theory and skills involved in effective communication, interviewing, and counseling for criminal justice personnel. Basic communication skills and process of problem solving with criminal justice clients emphasized.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Methods of Legal Research
description: An introduction to methods of legal research with emphasis on the utilization of law library resources, private and government organizations as courses of legal information, and on the formulation of briefs, memoranda, and other documents appropriate to legal practice.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Correctional Theory and Practice
description: The historical development, processes, and methods of operating the adult correctional system. Detailed study of the philosophy and development of treatment strategies in local, state, and federal correctional institutions. Cross-listed with SOC 362, may be taken once for credit.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ204 - Introduction to Corrections (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Criminal Justice Management
description: An overview of organizational theory and administrative behavior in criminal justice agencies. Effects of leadership, technology, information systems, decision making, court cases, personnel policies, budgeting, and planning on the justice system are analyzed.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ101 - Introduction to Criminal Justice (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Corrections Law
description: Explores the Constitutional provisions, court decisions, and statutes that apply to U. S. corrections, as well as the function that law plays in the juvenile and adult correctional context. Topics include inmate rights, habeas corpus procedures, civil and criminal liability issues, and the history of corrections law.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Criminal Procedure
description: Presentation of the regulations and case law associated with arrest, searches and seizures, rules of evidence, trials and plea bargaining, and corrections. Procedures of the entire criminal justice system will be examined.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Criminal justice associate degree standing
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ101 - Introduction to Criminal Justice (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Inequality, Crime, and Justice
description: Analysis of inequality and its influence upon differential criminal engagement and victimization, criminal justice system response, and criminal justice practitioners. Emphasis on race and ethnicity, sex and gender, socioeconomic status, sexual orientation, and age.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ101 - Introduction to Criminal Justice (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Environmental Crime
description: History, theories, law and the nature of crime are key points of analysis. Reviews law enforcement, prosecutorial and judicial practices involving environmental crime. Past, current and potential issues examined regarding environmental crime.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ101 - Introduction to Criminal Justice (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Research Methods
description: Exploration of the philosophy of science, research designs, and their implementation. Introduction to basic quantitative and qualitative research methods in criminal justice.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Statistics
description: Introduction to elementary analytic techniques including descriptive and inferential statistics. Emphasis is placed on guiding students in selecting, computing, and interpreting criminal justice statistics.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Admission to criminal justice major
Completed the following: 
CJ425 - Research Methods (3)

title: Occupational Deviance and Crime
description: Nature and extent of corporate and occupational criminality, including measures, reporting, and categories. Emphasis on organizational, governmental, and organized transnational crime. Functions of social control, punishment, and regulatory agencies examined.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: The Death Penalty in America
description: Historical, philosophical, and empirical examination of capital punishment with an emphasis on race/ethnicity, class, gender, and religion. Legal issues including jury-decision making, ineffective legal representation, cruel and unusual punishment, mental illness, wrongful conviction, costs, international law, and other policy issues examined. Living and working on death row, methods of execution, and philosophies of punishment explored.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Comparative Criminal Justice
description: International analysis and comparison of criminal justice systems at all levels including, but not limited to, law enforcement, law, courts, and/or correctional administration.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Contemporary Issues in American Policing
description: Study of the major issues facing today’s state and local policing organizations. Topics may include challenges to discretionary policing, effectiveness, problem-solving, community relations, accountability, and emerging areas.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ202 - Introduction to Police (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Contemporary Issues in American Criminal Courts
description: Study of the major contemporary issues facing the criminal court system at local, state, and federal levels of government. Topics include, but are not limited to, the study of individual courtroom actors, problem-solving courts (drug court, etc.), and determinants of court processing decisions. Topics are considered from historical, legal, philosophical, sociological and psychological perspectives.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CJ203 - Introduction to Courts and Sentencing (3)
CJ321 - Criminal Law (3)
CJ375 - Criminal Procedure (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Contemporary Issues in Offender Rehabilitation
description: Study of the major contemporary issues facing the treatment of offenders at the local, state, and federal levels of government. Topics include, but are not limited to, treatment-centered programming and advances in rehabilitation of high-risk offenders.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ204 - Introduction to Corrections (3)
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Criminalistics
description: Major concepts of forensic science and investigator role in crime scene evidence collections.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Criminal Justice BS, or Criminal Justice Minor

title: Field Work I
description: Placement in selected criminal justice agencies with assigned duties of regular personnel. Relevant research project required. Weekly seminar meeting to review research and agency progress. Must complete 150 contact hours in one semester.
prerequisites: Admission to criminal justice major

title: Field Work II
description: Continuation of CJ 491.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Senior Seminar (FF)
description: Exploration of current and anticipated critical issues and problems in the criminal justice system.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CJ425 - Research Methods (3)
Must have a class standing of senior or higher
Admission to criminal justice major.

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level.

title: Colloquium in Critical Theory
description: Provides an in-depth examination of a specific area or current trend in critical theory and/or its application in disciplines such as art, social sciences, humanities, etc. Course may be repeated once for credit; topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Topics in Critical Theory
description: Provides intensive examination, analysis, and application of a contemporary topic in critical theory. Course may be repeated once for credit; topics are not repeatable.
prerequisites: Must have a class standing of upper-division

title: Critical Theory Seminar
description: Capstone course focusing on intense individual research projects on topics of interest to the students.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
ENGLIT393 - Literary Criticism and Theory (3)
GENDER200 - Intro to Gender Studies (3)

title: Camp University Success
description: Designed to meet the specific academic needs of CAMP students. Students develop academic strategies needed to achieve educational goals and expand their awareness of social support systems available within the university and the community. Recommended admission to the CAMP program.

title: Camp Career Success
description: Students are guided through a self-reflection process, examine career goals and how they fit with their long term planning, and provided with multiple networking opportunities. Recommended admission to the CAMP program.

title: TRIO STEM Community Cohort
description: Introduction to Boise State University campus resources, financial literacy, and student and life success strategies. Students begin building a TRIO STEM Scholars community and allow for confidence in expanding their support network. Recommended admission to TRIO STEM Scholars.

title: TRIO STEM Career and Research
description: This course is designed for students to explore STEM careers, internships, and research opportunities. The purpose is to improve scientific communication with the development of written and oral communication skills necessary for future careers in STEM. Skills include summarizing and evaluating scientific research, and communicating scientific information to targeted audiences. This course focuses on the continuation of your college journey in TRIO STEM.

title: TRIO Rising Scholars Community Cohort
description: Introduction to Boise State University campus resources, financial literacy, and student and life success strategies. Students begin building a TRIO community to expand their support network. Recommended admission to TRIO Rising Scholars.

title: TRIO Rising Scholars Mentoring and Leadership Development
description: Topics are designed to develop soft skills such as emotional intelligence, the power of mentoring and how to be a catalyst for change. The goal is to improve students' written and verbal communication skills necessary for their future careers. Summarizing and evaluating written material in the humanities and social sciences as well as the exploration of the dual role of receiving mentorship and developing mentor skills will be the focus of the content provided.

title: Education, Schooling, and Society (FS)
description: Study and evaluate schooling within the United States as a social institution that both shapes and is shaped by societal forces. Critical examination of its origins, evolution, and current forms. Provides a conceptual framework from which students will reflect upon and question American public education.

title: Child and Educational Psychology
description: Introduction to children's development and its universal characteristics across cultures, educational psychology, theories of learning, cognitive development, motivation and self-concept, and educational measurement. Designed primarily for Elementary Education majors.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Teaching Experience I
description: A 50-100 hour teaching experience in the public schools. Students will observe the teaching/learning process and identify best research-based practices in a classroom setting. May be taken for ED-CIFS or MUS credit, but not both. (Pass/Fail.)
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Learning and Instruction
description: Introduction to educational psychology, principles of learning and instruction, and general methods of teaching. Theories and models of learning and teaching, cognitive development, motivation and self-concept, classroom management and educational measurement.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission, or College of Education Upper Division Standing Admission

title: Foundations of Gifted and Talented Education
description: Overview of gifted/talented education. Topics include identification, assessments, talent areas, curriculum adaptations, social needs, critical and creative thinking, legal aspects, and resources.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ED-CIFS203 - Child and Educational Psychology (3)
ED-CIFS302 - Learning and Instruction (4)

title: Creativity and Critical Thinking Skills
description: Definition, identification, and facilitation of creativity and critical thinking skills. Topics include overview, cognitive development, related brain research, assessment instruments, creative people, processes, and conditions for fostering creativity and models of critical thinking including creative problem solving. Demonstration of competency in identifying, fostering, assessing, demonstrating, and describing programs that foster creativity and critical thinking are required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ED-CIFS203 - Child and Educational Psychology (3)
ED-CIFS302 - Learning and Instruction (4)

title: Social and Emotional Needs of Gifted and Talented Learners
description: Identification and basic intervention for basic affective needs of gifted and talented learners. Topics covered will include: emotional aspects of giftedness, suicide, perfectionism, underachievement, peer relations, gender issues, risk taking, family relations, cultural factors, twice exceptional, self-esteem, career counseling, asynchronous development, and counseling skills for teachers.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ED-CIFS203 - Child and Educational Psychology (3)
ED-CIFS302 - Learning and Instruction (4)

title: Assessment in Teaching and Learning
description: Assessment strategies in the classroom discussed. Analysis, administration and interpretation of standardized assessment instruments, performance assessments using national and state standards, teacher-constructed assessment tools, and evaluation and grading will be examined.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
For Elementary Education majors
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS332 - Elementary Classroom Management Skills (3)
ED-CIFS460 - Professional Year I (5)
For Dual Special Education, Elementary Education/Dual Early Childhood Intervention, Elementary Education majors.
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS332 - Elementary Classroom Management Skills (3)
ED-CIFS459 - Teaching Experience in Elementary Education (8)

title: Elementary Social Studies Curriculum and Instruction
description: Examines elementary social studies curricula, philosophies, and methodologies. Instructional strategies and materials are presented and evaluated in accordance with developmental theory. Focus on the ten strands of social studies, values in a democratic and pluralistic society, and global issues. These areas are integrated across the curriculum, emphasizing process, critical thinking, technology, and assessment.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Elementary Mathematics Curriculum and Instruction
description: Examines elementary mathematics curricula, philosophies, and methodologies. Instructional strategies and materials are presented and evaluated in accordance with developmental theory. Focus on the process and content strands in elementary mathematics. These areas are integrated across the curriculum, emphasizing critical thinking and assessment.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Elementary Classroom Management Skills
description: Examines how to structure classrooms and learning environments, enhancing opportunities for all children to succeed. Varied classroom management skills and strategies to support appropriate behavior. Communicating and collaborating with parents is addressed along with democratic community building within the classroom.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
For Elementary Education majors
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS329 - Assessment in Teaching and Learning (3)
ED-CIFS460 - Professional Year I (5)
For Bilingual Education and Elementary Education TESOL majors
Completed the following: 
ED-LLC460 - Professional Year I (5)
For Dual Special Education, Elementary Education/Dual Early Childhood Intervention, Elementary Education.
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS329 - Assessment in Teaching and Learning (3)
ED-CIFS459 - Teaching Experience in Elementary Education (8)

title: Elementary Science Curriculum and Instruction
description: Examines elementary science curricula, philosophy, and methodologies. A variety of instructional strategies and materials are presented and evaluated in accordance with developmental theory. Emphasis is placed on inquiry in the science curricula. These areas are integrated across the curriculum, emphasizing process, critical thinking, technology, and assessment.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Curriculum Adaptations for Gifted and Talented Students
description: Curriculum adaptations for gifted and talented learners including curriculum compacting, independent study, project-based learning, research-based learning, enrichment programs, mentoring programs, acceleration, dual enrollment, and more.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
PSYC101 - Introduction to Psychology (FS) (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
ED-CIFS203 - Child and Educational Psychology (3)
ED-CIFS302 - Learning and Instruction (4)

title: Professional Inquiry, Reflection, and Capacity for Change (FF)
description: Designed as a capstone experience to integrate coursework across the program. Students will engage in a developmental process that  furthers the application of an inquiry stance toward teaching and learning by engaging in a continuous cycle of reflection on self, equitable practice, and community to develop a capacity for change. Educators will reflect on their experiences across their programs to develop an accurate insight into their own strengths and areas of growth and apply their reflections toward further developing their professional identity and practice.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Professional Year - Teaching Experience II
description: Students will work with a master teacher for a minimum of 150-200 hours. They will observe the teaching/learning process and demonstrate teaching competence in a P-12 classroom setting.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission

title: Teaching Secondary Science
description: Local, state and national science curricula and standards. Materials, methods and instructional technologies to develop science lessons to develop scientific inquiry skills, an understanding of the nature of science, and critical understanding of selected science concepts and procedures.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Completed the following: 
ED-ESP350 - Teaching Students with Exceptional Needs at the Secondary Level (3)

title: Teaching Secondary Social Studies
description: Prepares teachers to engage young people in an inquiry about fundamental ideas and values from history and/or social science disciplines as well as to assist and encourage them to become informed, active participants in a democratic society. Examine professional literature on best teaching practices.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Completed the following: 
ED-ESP350 - Teaching Students with Exceptional Needs at the Secondary Level (3)

title: McNair Junior Seminar A
description: Introduction to graduate school and academic culture. Exploration of discipline and graduate programs. Literature search to develop research question for summer research. May be repeated for credit. Recommended admission to the McNair Scholars program.

title: McNair Junior Seminar B
description: Develop research proposal for summer research. Prepare for GRE. Develop components of graduate application package. Explore graduate school funding possibilities. May be repeated for credit. Recommended admission to the McNair Scholars program.

title: McNair Senior Seminar A
description: Prepare research journal article for publication. Present research at National McNair conference. Finalize graduate school application components and apply. May be repeated for credit. Recommended admission to the McNair Scholars program.

title: McNair Senior Seminar B
description: Prepare for and attend graduate school visitations or interviews. Manage graduate school acceptance deadlines and offers. Prepare for graduate school transition and relocation. May be repeated for credit. Recommended admission to the McNair Scholars program.

title: Fundamental Frameworks for Supporting Teaching and Learning
description: Theory and practice for P-12 educators in developing and maintaining learning environments. Interpretation of assessment data and knowledge of motivation theories will be used to inform differentiated instruction, tiered intervention, and structures of classroom learning environments that enhance opportunities for all children to succeed.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Professional Year Admission, or College of Education Upper Division Standing Admission

title: Supervised Clinical Field Experience
description: Required supervision for teaching candidates adding an endorsement to current teaching certificate or for an alternate route to initial certification. Full-time classroom placement with performance assessment aligned with state certification requirements. Placement and credits required determined by Office of Teacher Education.

title: Teaching Experience in Elementary Education
description: Teaching experience in an elementary education classroom for students pursuing dual certification in an Early and Special Education program. Teaching experience includes activities related to planning and preparation, classroom environments, curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Students will complete a teaching experience consistent with the calendars of the assigned partnership schools and degree program requirements.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Professional Year I
description: Classroom placement focusing on activities related to planning and preparation of curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Students complete a minimum of 250 hours in the K-8 classroom and apply knowledge and skills from all professional education coursework.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Professional Year II - Teaching Experience in Elementary Education
description: Supervised teaching experience in a partnership school, including activities related to planning and preparation, classroom environments, curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Students will complete a full-time K-8 teaching experience consistent with the calendar of the assigned partnership school and including specific experiences in their middle level endorsement area under the supervision of university faculty.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
ED-CIFS460 - Professional Year I (5)
ED-CIFS430 - Fundamental Frameworks for Supporting Teaching and Learning (4)
ED-LLC442 - Integrated Disciplinary Literacy in the Social Sciences (3)
Completion of all Elementary Education requirements

title: Professional Year - Teaching Experience III
description: Supervised student teaching experience in a partnership school, including activities related to planning and preparation, classroom environments, curriculum and instruction, and professional responsibilities. Student will complete a full-time teaching experience consistent with the calendar of the assigned partnership school in their major/minor fields under the supervision of university faculty. Attendance at seminars is required.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Professional Year Admission

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Independent Study
description: Upper-division students may earn credits in independent study, usually through directed reading or by completing a special project. Students may earn no more than 4 credits in a semester and no more than 6 credits during a single academic year, and no more than a total of 9 credits may be used to meet degree requirements or university graduation requirements. Before enrolling for independent study, a student must obtain the approval of the department chair, acting on the recommendation of the instructor who will be supervising the independent study. An independent study cannot be substituted for a course regularly offered at Boise State, nor can independent study credits be used to improve a grade in a course the student has already taken.

title: Seminar
description: A seminar is a small class that examines a particular topic. Seminars are typically discussion oriented and are most commonly offered at the junior, senior, or graduate level. May be repeated for credit.

title: Secure Design and Computational Thinking
description: An introduction to secure design and computational thinking for cyber operations and resilience major. The fundamentals of resilience system and computation solutions reside in secure design and ability to think critically. Implementations will be carried out with selected language.

title: Blue Team U
description: An introduction to cybersecurity operations. The fundamentals of blue team operations, applied network monitoring, log management, applications patch management, human aspect of cybersecurity, and controls.

title: Operating Systems and Cloud Operations
description: An introduction to software, operating systems, databases, and cloud computing in cyber operations. A language will be used as the learning tools with varieties of operating systems and cloud platforms. How to harden operating systems and databases.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CORE101 - Blue Team U (3)
CORE100 - Secure Design and Computational Thinking (3)

title: Cyber Operations Networking
description: An applied introduction to network and how to design a resilient network. Different techniques will be introduced on designing secure and resilient networks.
prerequisites:
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CORE101 - Blue Team U (3)
CORE100 - Secure Design and Computational Thinking (3)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Cyber Systems Thinking
description: An introduction to systems thinking, lateral thinking, and resilience thinking as they relate to cybersecurity. Topics include understanding the complexity/interconnectedness cybersecurity, applying lateral thinking in solving cyber problems, interacting among people, processes, and technologies, and managing disturbances/surprises/uncertainty to be more resilient.

title: Cyber Risk Assessment
description: Study of risk assessment and management techniques, methods, and models used in industry to minimize, control and communicate risks, including NIST and OWASP.

title: Introduction to Cybercrime and Investigation
description: An exploration of the fundamental concepts and definitions of cybercrime. Acquire essential insights into the complexities of cybercrime, psychology and motives behind criminal behavior and the role of digital technology as both a tool and catalyst for victimization with an overview of investigations.

title: Cyber Project Management and Design
description: Develop cybersecurity project management skills and provides a roadmap for implementing cybersecurity technologies and process changes into organizations and projects. Principles and applied techniques to be discussed include risk assessment, project coordination, threat intelligence, communication, schedule, and cost management.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)
CORE400 - Cyber Systems Thinking (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CORE470 - Cyber Risk Management (3)
CORE406 - Cybersecurity Risk Quantification (3)

title: Cybersecurity Risk Quantification
description: An introduction to the use of probabilistic methods for quantifying cyber risk. Includes Bayesian and Monte Carlo methods, an introduction to the risk analysis process, and hands-on work with mathematical models and vulnerability analysis.

title: Applied Cybersecurity Programming
description: An introduction to Python and Powershell, and how these can be used to simplify cyber related tasks.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
description: An overview of how machine learning and artificial learning can be applied to cybersecurity.

title: Internet of Things Architecture
description: Introduction to embedded systems, basic concepts of IoT, and making smart things. Covers IoT architecture including sensor, hardware, firmware, internet connection, and data mining.

title: Cyber Security Operations Center
description: An overview of centralized security functions where people, processes, and technology are employed to continuously monitor and improve an organization's security posture while preventing, detecting, analyzing, and responding to cybersecurity incidents.

title: Cyber Business and Regulatory Operations
description: Introduction to existing regulations that companies and organizations must follow to safeguard information and systems.

title: Cyber Red and Blue Teams
description: A study of how to think like a blue team and how to think like a red team.

title: Cyber Operations Operating Systems Hardening
description: Learn the fundamental principles and best practices for securing computer operating systems. Through practical exercises and expert guidance, you'll gain the knowledge and skills needed to protect systems from potential threats and vulnerabilities

title: Industrial Control Systems Cybersecurity
description: An introduction to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Cybersecurity to support critical infrastructure and maintain national security.  Understanding secure ICS architecture principles; risks and considerations for integrating Information Technology (IT), Operational Technology (OT), and Internet of Things (IOT); and ICS specific threats, vulnerabilities, and attack patterns.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CORE400 - Cyber Systems Thinking (3)
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)

title: Introduction to Incident Response
description: An introduction to incident response to prepare for, detect, contain, and recover from a cybersecurity breach. Understanding how incident response supports an organization's broader mission, approaches to incident response, phases of incident response, roles and responsibilities of an incident response team, and legal considerations.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)
CORE400 - Cyber Systems Thinking (3)

title: Cyber Threat Intelligence
description: Study and experimentation of platforms, open source tools, and techniques for cyber threat intelligence. Connection between cyber threat intelligence and risk assessment. Advanced practicum in artificial intelligence applications in cyber threat intelligence.

title: Cyber Resilience Systems Design
description: A study of resilient systems, networks, infrastructure design, and business process on the ability to anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises.

title: Cyber Operations Challenge and Competition
description: Provides a practical, hands-on approach to mastering IT and cybersecurity problem-solving, complemented by active participation in cybersecurity competitions for practical application and skill development.

title: Cyber Risk Management
description: An overview of cybersecurity risk management frameworks and practices. Students will model cybersecurity risks and apply both qualitative and quantitative risk assessment methods.

title: Cyber Capstone (FF)
description: Capstone design experience integrating previous coursework with cyber operations and resilience design theory and methodology.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
CORE405 - Cyber Project Management and Design (3)
CORE400 - Cyber Systems Thinking (3)
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CORE406 - Cybersecurity Risk Quantification (3)
CORE470 - Cyber Risk Management (3)

title: CORe Experiential Learning
description: Variable credit hour course based on activities such as cyber competitions. Records will be provided to show evidence of learning. (Pass/Fail.)

title: CORe Prior Learning
description: Variable credit course based on prior learning from work performed in the workplace or activities such as cyber competitions. Records will be provided to show evidence of learning. (Pass/Fail.)

title: CORe Certification
description: Variable credit hour course based on cybersecurity industry certification. Records will be provided to show evidence of learning and completion of certification. (Pass/Fail.)

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Fundamentals of Cybersecurity
description: Introduction to cyber and physical security for all students. Explores the principles and practices to keep device information safe; discussions of hacking, ethics, privacy, compliance, and an overview of policies and regulations related to security in cyber-physical systems.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed the following: 
MATH108 - Intermediate Algebra (3)
Or satisfactory placement score

title: Internet of Things: Connected Devices
description: Introduction to applications, operating systems, computer hardware, and computer programming related to uses and practices in the internet of things. Discussions will include analysis of best practices in each area that makes those systems less vulnerable to hacking or interference.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS100 - Fundamentals of Cybersecurity (3)

title: Hands-On Security: From Home to Office and Everything Between
description: Modern communication systems, home networking, office network, operation networks, firewalls, intrusion detection systems (IDS), intrusion protection systems (IPS), cybersecurity vulnerabilities, risks and threats at all levels will be discussed. Analysis related to critical infrastructure, vulnerabilities of physical systems, and the protection of group information security will be discussed.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS100 - Fundamentals of Cybersecurity (3)

title: Introduction to Digital Forensics and Incident Response
description: Planning and recovery for critical incidents will be covered. Topics include assessing systems for possible breach, preparation, identification, containment, eradication, and recovery related to cyber-physical attacks. Methods and approaches to create root cause analysis reports, digital forensic analysis, and remediation recommendations to improve systems to reduce the likelihood of future incidents.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS100 - Fundamentals of Cybersecurity (3)

title: Information Assurance and Critical Thinking
description: Topics related to business needs and requirements, information assurance, information security, risk management, logic, communication and critical thinking in cyber-security or cyber-physical systems security. The course also examines the components of a comprehensive information assurance plan, day-to-day operation enterprise risk management, NIST cybersecurity framework, and NIST risk management framework.
prerequisites: Complete a Foundation of Mathematics course

title: Defensive Security
description: Provides a baseline of fundamental knowledge of defensive security. Includes systems engineering, scripting, cyber-informed engineering, cyber-kill chain, cloud security, MITRE ATT&CK human elements, logging, detection, prevention, monitoring, policies, programs, and procedures at different levels of an information system. Discusses creating and assessing security architectures, including DoDAF, SABSA, and MORDA.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)

title: Offensive Security
description: Discusses the tools and techniques used to look for weaknesses and vulnerabilities in a lawful and legitimate manner, along with the tools and techniques to strengthen security of systems. Discusses network mapping, cloud security, scripting, basic IETF protocols and analyze how they work and how they have been exploited.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)

title: Recovery and Forensics
description: Introduces the techniques and skills needed in computer forensics and data recovery. From the use of enterprise computer forensics tools to analyze trace evidence left behind by unauthorized access.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)

title: Foundational Essentials for IT Practitioners
description: A guided course with the goal of obtaining industry relevant certification. The objective is to obtain credential for technical support and IT operational roles. Student will gain knowledge and skills necessary to manage, maintain, troubleshoot, install, operate and configure office computing equipment, describe computing technologies, basic principles, adhere to professional standards, and use testing tools.

title: Foundational Essentials for IT Network Practitioners
description: A guided course with the goal of obtaining network industry relevant certification. The objective is to obtain networking credential by covering troubleshooting, configuring, and managing networks. Students will gain knowledge and skills necessary to manage, maintain, troubleshoot, install, operate and configure basic network infrastructure, describe networking technologies, basic design principles, and adhere to wiring standards and use testing tools.

title: Foundational Essentials for IT Cybersecurity Practitioners
description: A guided course with the goal of obtaining industry relevant certification. The objective is to obtain cybersecurity certification with the core knowledge required of any cybersecurity role and provides a springboard to intermediate-level cybersecurity jobs. Students will gain knowledge and skills necessary to identify and address potential threats, attacks and vulnerabilities and establish techniques in risk management, risk mitigation, threat management and intrusion detection.
prerequisites:
Completed the following: 
CPS301 - Information Assurance and Critical Thinking (3)

title: Intermediate-Level Essentials for IT Cybersecurity Practitioners
description: A guided course with the goal of obtaining industry relevant certification. Uses active learning with performance-based questions, and management skills used to plan, scope, and manage weaknesses, not just exploit them. Students will gain knowledge and skills to identify, exploit, report and manage vulnerabilities on a network.

title: Introduction to R Programming
description: Introduces R language and environment, including how to load data, prepare data for analysis, and manipulate data frames. Overviews basic programming skills, conditional expressions, loops, and functions in R. May be taken for credit in ANTH, BIOL, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC but not for more than one discipline.

title: Internship
description: Internship credits are earned in supervised fieldwork specifically related to a student's major. To enroll in courses numbered 293 or 493, a student must have attained a cumulative grade-point average of 2.00 or higher. No more than 12 credits of internship and/or Work U may be applied towards graduation requirements.

title: Principles of Data Science
description: An introduction to the core concepts of data science including: predictive modeling using machine learning and data mining; data gathering, extraction and cleaning; and exploratory data analysis. Emphasizes practical skills for liberal arts students to examine questions of human behavior using large and complex data sets. May be taken for credit as ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Completed at least 1 of the following: 
CS133 - Foundations of Data Science (3)
DATA-R155 - Introduction to R Programming (1)
Must take a statistics course
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher

title: Behavioral Time Series
description: Survey of rhythmic patterns of behavior, particularly in large data sets, and examination of the rationale and computational procedures for the analysis of time series in the time and frequency domains.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
And take a statistics course

title: Social Network Analysis
description: Introduces and applies concepts and empirical methods of network analysis in a field-based project. Social networks influence learning, economic behavior, and adoption of new products and organizational innovations. May be taken for credit as ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Must have a class standing of upper-division or higher
And take a statistics course

title: Statistical Modeling in R
description: Focuses on statistical methods for practical data analysis, including parametric and non-parametric analyses, ANOVA, multiple and logistic regression, generalized linear models, and dimension reduction methods using R to examine and understand human behavior. Students will conduct a research project designed in partnership with a professional stakeholder that delivers actionable outcomes. May be taken for credit as ANTH, DATA-R, PSYC, or SOC, but not for more than one discipline.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
Completed the following: 
ITM430 - Predictive Analytics (3)
ITM340 - Big Data and Web Analytics (3)
Completed the following: 
DATA-R322 - Principles of Data Science (3)

title: Community and Career: Planning for Success
description: Students begin to develop a personal academic plan for meeting their individualized goals. Goals will be identified, potential courses to take in future semesters will be explored, and students will receive peer and staff feedback for support in obtaining their goals.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Community and Career Readiness Studies Certificate

title: Job Exploration
description: Students explore and identify career interest and skills, develop a resume, practice interview skills and self-advocacy, and (with PEERS staff support) secure paid or unpaid employment for the following semester on or off campus.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Community and Career Readiness Studies Certificate

title: Employment Practicum
description: Students work in an inclusive employment setting for at least six hours per week. Employment may be paid or unpaid, on or off campus. Students' individual needs are supported via PEERS staff.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Community and Career Readiness Studies Certificate
Completed the following: 
ED-ESP100 - Community and Career: Planning for Success (3)
ED-ESP120 - Job Exploration (3)

title: Employment Practicum II
description: Students work in an inclusive employment setting for at least six hours per week. Employment may be paid or unpaid, and off campus. Students' individual needs are supported via PEERS staff. An emphasis on learning to self-direct formal supports necessary for sustaining employment is included and students focus on using technology for scheduling, communication, and work-related tasks.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Community and Career Readiness Studies Certificate
Completed the following: 
ED-ESP121 - Employment Practicum (3)

title: Employment Practicum III
description: Students work in an inclusive employment setting for at least six hours per week. Employment is paid in most circumstances, and off campus. Students' individual needs are supported via PEERS staff. An emphasis on clearly stating accommodations needed and integrating job supports into work routines is included.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Community and Career Readiness Studies Certificate
Completed the following: 
ED-ESP122 - Employment Practicum II (3)

title: Capstone: E-Portfolio
description: Students design and develop an e-portfolio to share with potential employers. The development of the e-portfolio includes self-assessment of individualized goals and reflection on students' learning and growth in the PEERS program. Students give a closed or public presentation of their e-portfolio depending on students' individualized goals. Public presentations will be encouraged. Recommended admission to Certificate in Community and Career Readiness Studies and concurrent enrollment in ED-ESP 123.

title: Foundations of Professional Practices: ECE/ECSE
description: Principles and practices of early childhood education/early childhood special education. Developmentally appropriate practices in the teaching/learning process of young children with and without special needs, in natural learning environments.

title: Child Growth and Development (FS)
description: Growth and development from birth through eighth grade, addressing physical, cognitive, communication, adaptive, social, and emotional domains. Emphasis on the role of the families as well as individual differences in the study of human development. Includes applied assignments and experiences.

title: Exceptionality in the Schools
description: An overview of student ability and disability in the schools, including characteristics of students with disabilities, legal requirements for educating students with disabilities, and basic educational strategies.

title: Educational and Assistive Technology
description: Educational and assistive technology applications aligned with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles to support all learners; assistive, adaptive, and rehabilitative devices and technologies, including Augmentative Alternative Communication (AAC) for individualized supports and instruction.

title: Special Education Policies and Procedures
description: Legal and procedural guidelines and practices in special education service delivery in current federal and state legislation, Individualized Education Programs, issues of culture and diversity, and professional collaboration.

title: Operations in Early Childhood Administration
description: Provides a comprehensive understanding of the essential operational aspects of operating an early childhood care and education program. Focus on budgeting and resource management, ethical and legal considerations, health and safety, and licensing standards.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Enrollment Management in Early Childhood Administration
description: Equips professionals to effectively manage the enrollment process in early childhood programs and schools. Focus on creating and implementing enrollment strategies that align with program mission and goals. Understand the role of marketing, outreach, and recruitment in attracting prospective families and become familiar with enrollment policies, admission criteria, and legal considerations specific to early childhood programs.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Personnel Management in Early Childhood Administration
description: Prepares early childhood professionals to effectively recruit, hire, orient, and manage an effective team of early childhood educators and staff. Emphasis on the critical role of personnel management in creating a high-quality learning environment for young children.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Program Curriculum and Environments in Early Childhood Administration
description: Prepares program professionals to develop or adopt comprehensive, developmentally appropriate curricula and learning environments. Focus on establishing a program’s educational framework and guidelines, and integrating best practices and early learning standards.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Observation, Assessment and Documentation in Early Childhood Administration
description: Focuses on developing systems and structures to support authentic observation, assessment and documentation of children’s learning to guide curriculum and make children’s learning visible.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Positive Guidance Practices in Early Childhood Administration
description: Explores the development of systems and structures to support consistent, predictable, positive and safe environments. Focus on development of guidance policies and practices, including collaborative problem-solving for targeted and systematic support.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Self-Leadership in Early Childhood Administration
description: Prepares professionals to lead effectively in the dynamic field of early childhood care and education. Focus on self-awareness, self-motivation, self-management, communication skills, decision-making, and reflective practice.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Facilitative Leadership in Early Childhood Administration
description: Explores advanced leadership skills and strategies that foster collaboration, cooperation, and collective decision-making within early childhood care and education settings. Emphasis on fostering psychologically safe environments, coaching and empowering teams to work together effectively to achieve shared goals.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Working with Families in Early Childhood Administration
description: Prepares professionals to effectively engage and partner with families of young children. Emphasis on the critical role of leadership in creating collaborative and supportive relationships between early childhood programs and families, while also managing conflict and challenge.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Program Culture in Early Childhood Administration
description: Explores the importance of program culture in early childhood education and its pivotal role in effective administrative leadership. Focus on articulating a program vision and mission and on developing, maintaining, and enhancing a positive and welcoming program culture for all young children and families.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Program Quality in Early Childhood Administration
description: Focuses on early childhood program quality. Emphasis on quality improvement systems, accreditation, and continuous improvement.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Program Evaluation in Early Childhood Administration
description: Focuses on assessing and improving early childhood education programs. Emphasis on effectively evaluating program quality, making data-driven decisions, and creating meaningful improvements that benefit children, families, and the broader community.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to Early Childhood Program Administration and Leadership Certificate

title: Family and Community Relations: ECE/ECSE
description: Partnering with families of young children, both typically and atypically developing. Family systems theory, roles and functions of special service colleagues and community resources. 30 hours of fieldwork required.
prerequisites: Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission, Family Studies Minor, or College of Education Upper Division Standing Admission

title: Inclusive Methods: ECE/ECSE
description: Application of a linked system of assessment, goal development, intervention and evaluation. Focus on implementation of developmentally appropriate practice in inclusive environments. Weekly classroom fieldwork required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Natural Environments, Birth to Three: ECE/ECSE
description: Development of infants, both typically developing and those with delays and disabilities. Focus on attachment processes, learning in naturalistic environments, and communication with families. A minimum of 20 hours of fieldwork is required in specific early intervention agency settings.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division college of education standing

title: EI/ECSE Assessment
description: Assessment of infants and young children ages birth to eight, both typically and atypically developing. Concepts of assessment and direct experience with both formal and informal assessments.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Intervention Methods: ECE/ECSE
description: Application of a linked system of assessment, goal development, intervention and evaluation. Focus on implementation of evidence-based targeted and individualized instructional supports for children with diverse needs. Weekly classroom fieldwork required.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Behavior Support in Early Childhood
description: Application of behavior support for young children and their families. Focus on implementing positive, preventive, and function-based interventions in school, home, and community environments.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Assessment for Instructional Decision-Making
description: Assessment procedures for making data-based instructional decisions; differences, strengths, and weaknesses of norm- and criterion-referenced tests; and the purpose of standardization are reviewed. Included is an emphasis on the alignment between curriculum, instruction, and assessment for identifying instructional needs and supports of students with disabilities. Issues regarding bias, discrimination, and disproportionality are discussed.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Language Arts for Students with Disabilities
description: Research-based explicit instruction in reading and writing for students with disabilities. Response to Intervention (RTI) and integrated formative assessment and interventions in language arts.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Mathematics for Students with Disabilities
description: Research-based explicit instruction in mathematics for students with disabilities. Response to Intervention (RTI) and integrated formative assessment and interventions in mathematics.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to Teacher Education Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Positive Behavior Intervention and Support
description: Development of research-based positive behavioral interventions and supports for students with behavioral/emotional disabilities, including functional and applied behavioral analysis.
prerequisites:
Complete all of the following
Declared or admitted to one of the following: Teacher Education Admission, or Early and Special Education Upper Division Standing Admission
Or upper-division College of Education standing

title: Teaching Students with Exceptional Needs at the Secondary Level
description: Characteristics of students from common areas of exceptionality, relevant litigation and legislation, assessment techniques, instructional strategies, and collaboration.
prerequisites:
Complete 1 of the following
